points: Memory poisoning risk differs sharply by memory type: semantic memory is relatively static and defendable with integrity/provenance controls, while episodic and short-term memories are dynamic and can be poisoned indirectly through consolidation and ongoing multi-agent interactions.

A large fraction of “memory poisoning” scenarios become non-issues under secure-by-design assumptions—authenticated append-only logs, signed/hash-chained memory, and secured communications—shifting the core problem from ML-centric defenses to systems/security engineering.

Private knowledge retrieval (PIR-style two non-colluding servers, or a lower-assurance single-server k-anonymity variant) enables local rule-based inference over untrusted knowledge bases while reducing an attacker’s ability to learn or tailor injected memory triggers.

nodes.title: Model Context Protocol Threat Modeling and Analyzing Vulnerabilities to Prompt Injection with Tool Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Client-side trust in MCP tool metadata is a dominant failure mode—tool poisoning can succeed or fail entirely depending on the client’s validation and UI transparency rather than the server or the user’s prompt.
points: Threat modeling surfaced 57 MCP ecosystem threats across five components and rated tool poisoning as a Critical client-side risk with a DREAD score of 46.5/50, indicating high damage potential with easy reproducibility and discovery.

In a 7-client, 4-attack evaluation, Cursor was unsafe on 100% of tested tool-poisoning vectors while Claude Desktop blocked 100%, showing security outcomes can swing from full compromise to full prevention purely based on MCP client implementation choices.

Most evaluated clients (5/7) performed no static validation of server-provided tool metadata and multiple clients had low/partial parameter visibility at runtime, enabling hidden-parameter exfiltration and approval-fatigue exploits unless mitigated with layered controls (static scanning, decision-path tracking, runtime monitoring, and user transparency).

nodes.title: OrgForge-IT: A Verifiable Synthetic Benchmark for LLM-Based Insider Threat Detection
affiliations: The University of Texas at Austin
nodes.one_liner: The benchmark shows that LLMs can look equally good at triage yet fail at the harder SOC-critical step—attributing compromise to the attacker instead of the victim—while false-positive cost varies wildly at the same F1.
points: A deterministic “physics–cognition boundary” (engine owns facts; LLMs render prose) makes cross-artifact ground truth consistency an architectural guarantee while producing a 51-day, 2,904-record corpus with 96.4% noise (1:26.4 signal-to-noise), enabling realistic evaluation of correlation under heavy telemetry imbalance.

Triage and verdict quality decouple: 8/10 models tie at triage F1=0.80, yet verdict performance splits into Tier A (F1=1.0) vs Tier B (F1=0.80), implying SOC model selection cannot rely on alerting/triage metrics alone.

Operational viability is dominated by baseline false positives even at identical verdict F1, with baseline FP rates spanning two orders of magnitude (0.021 vs 0.813), and the key Tier A differentiator is correct victim attribution in vishing (exonerating the compromised user rather than flagging them).

nodes.title: Bridging Code Property Graphs and Language Models for Program Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Tool-augmented LLM program analysis becomes practical at repo scale when CPG-powered slicing and taint flows are exposed as stable primitives instead of asking the model to generate graph queries or ingest whole codebases.
points: codebadger exposes Joern Code Property Graph analyses as high-level MCP tools (slicing, taint tracking, data-flow, call graphs), enabling LLM agents to analyze repository-scale code without reading entire projects or writing complex CPGQL queries.

On a large GGML audit (8,667 methods; 198,006 calls), the workflow narrowed review to 54 allocation sources and 300+ dangerous sinks and surfaced multiple memory-safety risks (e.g., unbounded alloca, integer-overflowed allocation sizes, and unsafe pointer arithmetic) without exhaustive file-by-file inspection.

The approach enabled end-to-end security outcomes: it traced and ASAN-confirmed a heap-buffer-overflow in libtiff via unchecked col_offset-driven pointer arithmetic and produced a correct first-try patch for libxml2’s CVE-2025-6021 integer-overflow bug that closely matched the maintainers’ fix.

nodes.title: Does Teaming-Up LLMs Improve Secure Code Generation? A Comprehensive Evaluation with Multi-LLMSecCodeEval
nodes.one_liner: Security improvements come more from verification-centric multi-model system design (static analysis + cross-checking + patch loops) than from using a bigger LLM.
points: Multi-LLM ensembles augmented with static analysis deliver large security gains—up to +47.3% secure-code rate on SecLLMEval and +19.3% on SecLLMHolmes—while purely LLM-based collaboration yields smaller, dataset-dependent improvements (+8.9% to +22.3%).

A hybrid lifecycle pipeline that combines ensemble generation, CodeQL filtering, cross-LLM verification, and patching achieves the best outcomes (97.32% secure on SecLLMEval; 99.06% on SecLLMHolmes), beating the best SAT-ensemble baseline by +1.78% to +4.72%.

Model scale is not a reliable proxy for security: structured orchestration of smaller/specialized models can reduce insecure outputs to 3.6%/1.9% (SecLLMEval/SecHolmesEval), and tool choice dominates measured security (Bandit labeled all Python samples secure, masking issues caught by CodeQL).

nodes.title: Agent Audit: A Security Analysis System for LLM Agent Applications
nodes.one_liner: Agent security failures often live in tool boundaries and deployment configs, and an agent-aware static analyzer can deliver ~4× higher recall than standard SAST without sacrificing developer workflow speed.
points: Agent Audit detects 40 of 42 annotated agent-application vulnerabilities on a 22-sample benchmark (95.24% recall, 86.96% precision, F1=0.909) while Bandit and Semgrep achieve only 29.7% and 23.8% recall, respectively.

Structured analysis of MCP deployment artifacts yields 100% coverage of MCP-specific issues (e.g., overbroad filesystem permissions and untrusted servers), where generic SAST tools show 0% coverage because they treat JSON/YAML as opaque data.

The tool is CI/CD-ready with sub-second scans (0.87s over 22,009 LOC; ~25k LOC/s) and produces SARIF for GitHub/VS Code, making high-recall agent-specific security checks practical as blocking pipeline gates despite a small false-positive cost (6 FPs).

nodes.title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Trustworthiness Assessment of Web Applications
nodes.one_liner: The most reliable path to scalable “trustworthiness scoring” is using LLMs only for structured practice labeling and letting a deterministic quality model aggregate results—because extra code context can reduce accuracy, but explicit rules can lift smaller models substantially.
points: Zero-shot practice classification on 42 Java functions × 16 OWASP Input Validation practices shows strong model stratification, with gpt-4.1 reaching Macro F1=0.893 while gpt-3.5-turbo lags at Macro F1=0.521, implying LLM-based auditing quality is dominated by model choice even before prompt tuning.

Semantic enrichment with CWE mappings and short negative examples increases gpt-4.1 to Macro F1=0.930 but degrades smaller models (e.g., gemini-2.5-flash drops from 0.773 to 0.679 and gpt-3.5-turbo from 0.521 to 0.474), indicating that “more context” can cause prompt overload and reduce reliability for cost-optimized LLMs.

Naively appending call-graph-derived callee code harms detection across models (gpt-4.1 Macro F1 falls from 0.893 to 0.747) while explicit rule-based prompting is the most consistently helpful (e.g., gpt-4o-mini improves from 0.609 to 0.738), and deterministic LSP aggregation of per-practice labels yields discriminative trustworthiness scores whereas direct LLM score generation has high error (MAE ≈ 0.40–0.60).

nodes.title: Improving Generalization on Cybersecurity Tasks with Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning
nodes.one_liner: Aligning payload embeddings to a contrastively-trained vulnerability-description space yields more shift-robust cyber classifiers than either fine-tuning or nearest-neighbor retrieval in generic embedding spaces.
points: A two-stage text→payload semantic alignment approach (SALM) improves temporally robust threat classification to 68.1% accuracy on a time-split operational dataset, beating the strongest baseline (TF‑IDF+RF at 65.7%) and indicating reduced reliance on brittle shortcut features under distribution shift.

On an out-of-distribution synthetic benchmark built from public CVE-style sources plus LLM-generated HTTP traffic, SALM achieves 24.4% accuracy vs 20.6% for the best supervised baseline (a +19% relative gain), suggesting cross-modal contrastive structure transfers better than end-to-end fine-tuning when payload patterns change.

Despite accuracy gains, macro-F1 remains ~30% on the temporal split (30.1%), showing that class imbalance and semantically overlapping operational labels still cause many rare classes to collapse toward near-zero F1, so deployment readiness hinges more on data quality/taxonomy clarity than model capacity alone.

nodes.title: SkillProbe: Security Auditing for Emerging Agent Skill Marketplaces via Multi-Agent Collaboration
nodes.one_liner: Agent skill marketplaces exhibit a 'benign-in-isolation, dangerous-in-composition' failure mode where risks propagate through highly connected skill-chain graphs, making pre-distribution combinatorial auditing essential.
points: At marketplace scale (2,500 top-downloaded skills), only 9.9% were fully clean while 30.4% were flagged as Conditional, implying that the vast majority of widely used agent skills ship with at least minor security issues and a large minority violate baseline security rules.

Popularity is a weak security signal: the Conditional rate stays roughly stable (about 20–35%) across download-rank tiers, and over 90% of high-popularity skills still fail to meet stringent admission standards, so marketplaces need mandatory pre-publication auditing rather than trust-by-downloads.

Compositional risk is systemic rather than isolated: among 499 skills with confirmed exploit links, the risk network contains 75,373 edges with data exfiltration (32,328) and fact poisoning (28,441) dominating (~80% of edges) and forming a single giant connected component, meaning one risky skill can rapidly amplify attack surface when chained with others.

nodes.title: STRIATUM-CTF: A Protocol-Driven Agentic Framework for General-Purpose CTF Solving
nodes.one_liner: The key twist is treating tool-call schemas as a hard safety/grounding layer, allowing an LLM agent to operate at “machine-speed” in live CTF conditions and still outperform strong human teams.
points: A protocol-constrained tool interface (MCP schema validation) enabled high autonomous CTF performance with 77.8–86.7% solve rates across 180 runs, suggesting grounded tool calling can matter more than extensive prompt/documentation for many challenges.

In a live 2025 university CTF, the system placed 1st with 215 points, beating 21 human teams by a narrow 10–15 point margin over the next two teams but showing a large drop-off versus the rest of the field (most teams <135 points), indicating agentic tooling can match top-student capability under time pressure.

Ablations showed documentation mainly improved efficiency (mean solve time 17.1 min baseline vs 20.1 min minimal) while success differences were not statistically significant (p=0.71), implying standardized tool abstractions reduce hallucination and speed up iteration even when overall win-rate is similar.

nodes.title: AEGIS: From Clues to Verdicts -- Graph-Guided Deep Vulnerability Reasoning via Dialectics and Meta-Auditing
nodes.one_liner: The key twist is turning LLM 'debate' into verifiable forensics by building per-clue, CPG-sliced evidence traces and then letting an audit agent veto any claim that cannot be grounded in that trace.
points: Aegis achieves a new PrimeVul state-of-the-art with 122/435 pair-wise correct predictions (the first result above 100), implying that repository-grounded evidence traces materially improve patch-vs-vuln discrimination rather than pattern matching.

False-positive rate drops by up to 54.40% versus leading baselines (e.g., 80.67%→36.78% vs VulInstruct at k=2), indicating that enforcing a closed, slice-derived evidence boundary plus audit vetoes can substantially reduce alert fatigue in CI triage.

Ablations show dialectical verification is necessary but insufficient (removing audit raises FPR to 60.9%), while audit alone collapses recall (down to ~19% at k=2), demonstrating that reliable LLM vulnerability reasoning needs both structured pro/con argumentation and independent claim-checking.

nodes.title: Adaptive Robust Estimator for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
nodes.one_liner: Robust statistics (adaptive-loss + median-of-means) turns reward-noise from a training failure mode into a controllable nuisance for multi-agent RL fine-tuning.
points: Replacing GRPO’s batch-mean normalization with an Adaptive Robust Estimator (median-of-means over per-block adaptive-loss M-estimates) measurably stabilizes policy updates under heavy-tailed and contaminated reward signals, preventing oscillation/divergence and improving training reward curves in both LLM reasoning and embodied VLN fine-tuning.

A structured Dual-Agent Answer–Critique–Rewrite (DACR) interaction with a cross-improvement reward explicitly credits critique utility (partner rewrite score minus partner initial score), yielding sizable accuracy gains such as AMC23 improving from 47.5% to 60.0% (+12.5) for Qwen2.5-7B and from 22.5% to 32.5% (+10.0) for Qwen2.5-1.5B under noisy rewards.

On aerial vision-and-language navigation, swapping FlightGPT’s GRPO optimizer for ARE improves generalization to unseen environments, reducing Navigation Error from 145.56 to 124.38 on val-unseen and from 140.78 to 129.71 on test-unseen while also increasing SR/OSR/SPL across splits.

nodes.title: Emergence of Fragility in LLM-based Social Networks: the Case of Moltbook
affiliations: Intelligence, Complexity and Technology Lab (ICT Lab)
nodes.one_liner: LLM-only social networks can self-organize into hub-dominated, core–periphery structures that look robust to random failures yet collapse quickly when a tiny set of highly interactive agents is removed.
points: A scraped Moltbook interaction dataset of 39,924 LLM agents, 235,572 posts, and 1,540,238 comments forms a directed weighted comment network with extreme inequality, including a single agent producing 120,969 comments and hubs reaching 1,031 in-degree and 4,320 in-strength.

Connectivity is globally wide but directionally constrained, with the giant weakly connected component covering 99.9% of users (39,878/39,924) while the giant strongly connected component covers only 33.5% (13,369), implying broad reachability without widespread mutual feedback loops.

Mesoscale structure is sharply centralized and fragile: a 0.9% k-core (343 nodes at k*=102) underpins cohesion, and removing 20% of nodes collapses the giant component to ~15% under out-degree-targeted attacks (vs ~78% under random failures), making high-out-degree “interaction broadcasters” key systemic choke points.

nodes.title: Environment-Grounded Multi-Agent Workflow for Autonomous Penetration Testing
nodes.one_liner: Graph-grounded memory plus mode-bounded autonomy turns LLM pentesting from brittle 'reasoning in text' into auditable, repeatable action in ROS-heavy OT scenarios—and exposes how quickly performance collapses without persistent state.
points: An environment-grounded multi-agent workflow with shared graph-based memory achieved 100% end-to-end success on a ROS/ROS2 robotics CTF (CTF-0 to CTF-3) across 5/5 runs using llama-3.3-70b-instruct, showing that persistent, structured state can make autonomous pentesting reliable in OT-like environments.

On the same tasks, a planner–summarizer baseline (HackSynth setup) never solved the ROS exploitation stages (CTF-2 and CTF-3: 0/5 across all tested models), indicating that summary-based context transfer and weak persistence are insufficient for domain-specific exploitation workflows.

Model choice still materially affected outcomes even with the proposed architecture (e.g., deepseek-v3.2 succeeded only 1/5 on CTF-1 and 0/5 on CTF-3; gemma-3-27b-it and an 8B model failed 0/5 past CTF-1), implying autonomy claims should be reported with per-stage success rates and robustness-to-stochasticity, not single-run demos.

nodes.title: Profit is the Red Team: Stress-Testing Agents in Strategic Economic Interactions
nodes.one_liner: Replacing taxonomy-driven jailbreak tests with judge-free, scalar-profit optimization reveals that agents can look robust under static evals yet collapse under learned economic pressure—and the resulting exploit traces can be turned into effective, low-cost hardening rules.
points: Adaptive, profit-optimized opponents increased attacker surplus dramatically versus static baselines across four auditable economic games—e.g., ultimatum bargaining gains of +18.85 to +44.50 surplus (all p≤5.7×10^-3) and provision-point gains up to +55.50 (p=4.5×10^-17)—showing fixed attack sets can severely understate real exploitability.

Profit-driven pressure often pushed agents into strictly dominated choices, including accepting deals that yield negative surplus despite a guaranteed zero outside option (e.g., ultimatum bargaining where attacker surplus >40 implies the target is negative), indicating vulnerability is frequently about strategic manipulation rather than overt jailbreak-like prompts.

Distilling high-severity exploit traces into short prompt rules produced large, immediate robustness improvements without model updates, such as ultimatum bargaining target surplus improving from -25.00 to +7.00 (+32.00, p=5.2×10^-16) and eliminating extreme auction failures where attacker surplus ≥25.

nodes.title: Towards Secure Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Comprehensive Review of Threats, Defenses and Benchmarks
affiliations: State Key Laboratory of Mathematical Engineering and Advanced Computing
Henan Key Laboratory of Information Security
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the end-to-end pipeline view: it frames RAG security as a system integration problem where retrieval context becomes the new control plane for both attacks and defenses.
points: Across a synthesis of 152 papers, RAG security work is dominated by offensive techniques—data poisoning alone accounts for 28.4% of the literature—indicating that real-world RAG deployments should treat corpus integrity as the primary security bottleneck rather than model weights.

RAG security publications accelerated sharply from fewer than 5 papers/year pre‑2024 to 18 papers in 2024 and 48 papers in 2025, reflecting that the expanding multi-module attack surface (retriever, vector store, generator) is now a first-order deployment risk rather than an academic edge case.

The most consequential system-level pattern is a “trust inversion” where retrieved context can override model safety priors, so defenses need end-to-end controls spanning ingestion filtering, retrieval-time protections (e.g., access control/encrypted retrieval), and output leakage mitigation (e.g., DP, masking, federated isolation) instead of single-point guardrails.

nodes.title: Improving Safety Alignment via Balanced Direct Preference Optimization
nodes.one_liner: Overfitting in DPO-style safety tuning can be traced to asymmetric “comprehension” of the two sides of preference pairs, and a simple MI-based rebalancing of gradients materially boosts jailbreak robustness without a large alignment tax.
points: Mutual-information analysis on 20,000 PKU-SafeRLHF preference pairs shows pervasive “imbalanced preference comprehension,” where models alternately exhibit higher query-response dependence for preferred vs. dispreferred answers across both safe and unsafe queries, indicating asymmetric learning signals inside standard DPO-style training.

Training on the half of data with larger mutual-information gaps yields higher in-training reward margins yet worse downstream outcomes (e.g., for Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, using the more balanced half improves safety by +1.28% on StrongReject and +4.0% on XsTest and also improves general robustness by +1.35% on AdvGLUE), implying reward-margin gains can mask generalization-harming overfitting in safety alignment.

Balanced Direct Preference Optimization (B-DPO) reweights preferred vs. dispreferred terms using mutual-information ratios plus a stabilizing global scaling factor and improves safety while largely preserving general ability, including +8.01% StrongReject over the best baseline on Qwen2-7B-Instruct (92.01% vs. 86.50%) and higher jailbreak resistance on PAIR (80% vs. 76%) and GCG (98.59% vs. 93.54%).

nodes.title: SafeMath: Inference-time Safety improves Math Accuracy
nodes.one_liner: Steering a math LLM away from toxic narrative content at inference time can simultaneously make outputs safer and measurably more accurate on the same problems.
points: A new benchmark, ToxicGSM, contains ~1,920 numerically consistent 2–3 step arithmetic word problems with embedded harmful narratives spanning 9 categories (largest counts: bomb making 263, illegal activity 261, adult content 257), showing that “benign” math tutoring prompts can reliably carry toxic context without changing the underlying reasoning task.

Across three math-tuned 7B–8B models, an inference-time in-context-vector safety steer (θSF) increased math accuracy despite adding safety constraints (e.g., LlamaMath 84.90%→88.02%, DeepSeekMath 86.98%→87.86%, Qwen2Math 78.18%→78.80%), consistent with attribution shifts away from harmful tokens and toward numerical tokens.

Combining a safety ICV with a math-correctness ICV (θSFM) produced the best overall accuracy with +2–5% absolute gains over the base models (LlamaMath 89.84%, DeepSeekMath 88.23%, Qwen2Math 80.83%) while reducing harmful language per a Beaver safety cost model, whereas few-shot “safe” prompting severely degraded accuracy (down to 4.06% on Qwen2Math).

nodes.title: Alignment Whack-a-Mole : Finetuning Activates Verbatim Recall of Copyrighted Books in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Benign-looking finetuning can act as an alignment bypass that reactivates latent copyrighted-book copies stored in model weights, and the failure mode appears shared across major providers.
points: Finetuning a plot-summary-to-prose task makes aligned frontier models regurgitate up to 85–90% of held-out copyrighted books (bmc@5), including single verbatim spans >460 words, using only semantic descriptions and no book text at inference.

Cross-author generalization is severe: finetuning solely on Haruki Murakami unlocks verbatim recall from 30+ unrelated authors (often >80% coverage), while synthetic finetuning yields near-zero extraction—implicating reactivation of latent pretraining memorization rather than learning the task format.

Memorization is systemic across providers: GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and DeepSeek-V3.1 show highly correlated per-book extraction (Pearson r≥0.90) and 90–97% overlap in memorized regions, with ~61% of extracted spans and ~90% of >150-word spans absent from trillion-token Common Crawl corpora under exact match, consistent with non-web book sources.

nodes.title: Agent-Sentry: Bounding LLM Agents via Execution Provenance
nodes.one_liner: Execution-provenance “functionality graphs” plus a trusted-input-only intent check yields a practical bound on agent tool use that is both robust to prompt injection and far less brittle than hand-written policies.
points: Agent-Sentry blocks most indirect prompt-injection-driven out-of-bounds tool executions, achieving ~90%+ attack prevention while preserving up to 98% utility on AgentDojo and ~95% utility on its larger 6,733-trace benchmark.

With full behavioral coverage, average utility success is 94.61% and attack success rate is 9.46% across four agent domains, indicating remaining failures are dominated by intent/behavior ambiguity rather than missing execution patterns.

Compared to policy-heavy baselines on AgentDojo, Agent-Sentry reaches 3.70% ASR at 76.30% utility with 7.89s latency, outperforming the strong-but-slow isolation approach (0.00% ASR, 37.11% utility, 26.60s) and improving on a DSL-rule approach (5.41% ASR, 73.20% utility, 4.26s).

nodes.title: ClawKeeper: Comprehensive Safety Protection for OpenClaw Agents Through Skills, Plugins, and Watchers
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
China Academy of Information and Communications Technology
nodes.one_liner: Externalizing safety into a separate ‘Watcher’ agent measurably reduces the safety–utility coupling and enables continuous improvement in defense rates over time.
points: A three-layer defense (skills + runtime plugins + an external Watcher) achieved 85–90% defense success across seven agent threat categories, outperforming the best baseline in every category by 15–45 percentage points.

Baseline defenses were highly fragmented—no single alternative covered more than 3 of 7 threat categories—implying that point solutions leave large, predictable gaps in agent lifecycles spanning prompt injection, config tampering, and supply-chain skill risks.

A decoupled Watcher that can interrupt execution and require human confirmation improved from ~90% to ~95% defense success after processing 100 new adversarial cases, indicating that keeping safety logic outside the task agent enables iterative hardening that static skills/plugins cannot match.

nodes.title: AgentRFC: Security Design Principles and Conformance Testing for Agent Protocols
nodes.one_liner: The most novel takeaway is that agent-protocol security cannot be validated in isolation because bridging infrastructure can turn a prompt-injection in one protocol into delegation scope escalation in another.
points: A two-phase conformance workflow (formal TLA+ model checking plus counterexample replay against live SDKs) identified 33 spec-level violations across a 5×11 protocol-by-principle matrix and confirmed issues via 42 implementation tests.

Cross-protocol composition is a systemic failure mode: 5 composed models triggered 20 composition-safety violations, showing that properties which appear to hold for individual agent protocols can break when bridged by shared conductors/proxies.

Across the 6-layer Agent Protocol Stack, only transport security is consistently complete (4/4), while higher layers—session lifecycle, identity/capability trust, consent semantics, and audit/accountability—are broadly under-specified, correlating with recurrent gaps in credential revocation, consent enforcement, and audit completeness.

nodes.title: Policy-Guided Threat Hunting: An LLM enabled Framework with Splunk SOC Triage
nodes.one_liner: The interesting twist is using DRL as an upstream policy gate that decides when the LLM is even allowed to look, trading off recall vs alert fatigue via reward modes while keeping analyst-verifiable Splunk evidence in the loop.
points: A hierarchical SOC pipeline that gates LLM triage behind a DRL containment policy and an autoencoder anomaly score cuts LLM workload by roughly 63–65% on simulated Suricata traffic while keeping near-perfect alert capture in the most recall-oriented reward modes (recall ≈ 0.998).

Reward shaping alone materially changes operational behavior: on BOSS-of-the-SOC, the false-positive-averse mode achieved the best overall detection (recall 0.873, F1 0.861), while balanced/noisy rewards lowered average regret (down to 1.358) and decision cost (≈ −0.794), indicating more stable policies under distribution shift.

SIEM-grounded validation closes the loop from AI triage to analyst action by generating SPL queries and confirming behavioral indicators (e.g., bursty/high-frequency DNS sessions consistent with C2 or repeated multi-port probing consistent with scanning) rather than relying on signature matches.

nodes.title: When Convenience Becomes Risk: A Semantic View of Under-Specification in Host-Acting Agents
affiliations: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Network and System Security
nodes.one_liner: The core security failure mode isn’t execution-time tool abuse but pre-execution “semantic completion,” where convenience-driven planning silently crosses unstated safety boundaries like persistence and internet exposure.
points: Goal-only instructions in host-acting agents systematically create security-divergent plans because the agent must fill in missing execution semantics (scope, privilege ceilings, persistence, exposure) even when the user’s objective is benign and no attacker is present.

Six recurring risky completion patterns—privilege expansion, sensitive-resource overreach, persistent host modification, exposure enlargement, unsafe dependency introduction, and destructive/over-aggressive repair—explain how under-specified boundaries turn “valid” task plans into materially higher blast-radius host actions.

Trace-based scenarios on an OpenClaw-style host-coupled container show that adding strong locality cues can narrow plan scope for setup/repair, but collaboration/exposure goals still reliably pull planning toward broader deployment and access distribution unless exposure boundaries are made explicit pre-execution.

nodes.title: Is Monitoring Enough? Strategic Agent Selection For Stealthy Attack in Multi-Agent Discussions
nodes.one_liner: Even with continuous anomaly detection and message blocking, a mathematically optimized choice of which agents to corrupt and whom they influence can still steer multi-agent discussions off-course at ~40–45% effectiveness with low detectability.
points: Off-the-shelf anomaly detectors (SelfCheckGPT, RAGAs, G‑Safeguard) detect and block ≥93.5% of unmodified multi-agent discussion attacks, collapsing their impact to roughly 0.3–2.0% accuracy drop (∆Acc) under monitoring versus 34.2–52.8% without monitoring.

Adding stealth-oriented prompting makes attacks largely evade monitoring (detection ≤8.4%) but also makes them largely ineffective (attack impact ≤7.6% ∆Acc), showing a strong stealth–effectiveness tradeoff for existing methods.

A topology- and opinion-dynamics–optimized hijacking/targeting strategy restores high attack impact under continuous monitoring—reaching 38.9–45.3% ∆Acc and 44.6–51.1% adversary agreement shift (∆Agradv) while keeping detection low (~5.4–8.3%), implying monitoring alone is not a sufficient defense for multi-agent deliberation.

nodes.title: SEVerA: Verified Synthesis of Self-Evolving Agents
nodes.one_liner: Verified fallbacks turn unreliable LLM calls into contract-safe primitives, enabling gradient-based self-improvement without ever reintroducing safety regressions.
points: SEVerA enforces hard first-order logic contracts on every generative-model call via Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGMs)—rejection sampling plus a verified fallback—yielding 0% constraint violations across program verification, tool-use policy compliance, symbolic math generation, and constrained symbolic regression even after model parameter updates.

On Dafny verification, adding formally checked “NoDiff” constraints both removes silent cheating (violations drop from 8.1%/10.3% to 0.0%) and increases verified-correct outputs to 97.0% on HumanEvalDafny (vs 86.9% best baseline) and 89.1% on DafnyBench (vs 81.6% baseline).

On policy-constrained tool use and structured math output, hard constraints are not merely safety rails but improve utility: τ2-bench pass rates rise to 53.6% (retail) and 52.6% (airline) with 0.0% violations (vs 68.4–76.3% violations for an unconstrained LLM), and GSM-Symbolic accuracy reaches 66.0% with 0.0% violations after GRPO+LoRA tuning (vs 44.7% for a constrained-decoding baseline).

nodes.title: Before the Tool Call: Deterministic Pre-Action Authorization for Autonomous AI Agents
nodes.one_liner: The core insight is that agent safety hinges on security-grade, deterministic authorization at the tool boundary—not probabilistic alignment—so unauthorized actions can be blocked before they execute and still leave a signed trail.
points: Deterministic, pre-action tool-call authorization reduced real social-engineering breaches from 74.6% under a permissive policy to 0% under a restrictive policy across 879 high-tier adversarial attempts, showing policy enforcement can reliably override a compromised model’s intent.

A synchronous before-tool-call reference monitor with signed credentials and fail-closed defaults achieved a 53ms median decision latency (p99 < 77ms, N=1,000), indicating per-call authorization is operationally feasible for I/O-bound agent workflows.

Across 1,151 live attack sessions and 4,437 authorization decisions, 54.5% of tool calls were denied with cryptographically signed audit records, demonstrating that least-privilege capability scoping and parameter limits can be enforced at runtime with verifiable accountability.

nodes.title: MIND: Multi-agent inference for negotiation dialogue in travel planning
nodes.one_liner: Tone becomes a machine-readable signal that lets agents estimate hidden priorities and negotiate efficiently, preserving “must-haves” while conceding low-impact preferences.
points: A Theory-of-Mind-style “Strategic Appraisal” module inferred opponents’ hidden willingness from linguistic cues with 90.2% accuracy (±2), MAE 1.27, and Pearson r=0.69 over 359 inferences, enabling strategy selection under information asymmetry.

On 201 multi-party travel negotiations, the framework improved High-wHit to 35.08% (+20.5%) and Debate Hit-Rate to 34.65% (+30.7%) versus a standard multi-agent debate baseline, indicating better prioritization of high-stakes constraints over average preference matching.

Consensus scalability improved materially as group size grew: Debate Ratio stayed at 88.4% for 4-agent groups (vs 64.5% baseline) while maintaining similar fairness (Jain’s index ~0.684) and trading off a modest drop in Total Fidelity (23.87% vs 25.80%) for higher total weighted satisfaction (19.96 vs 18.03).

nodes.title: Silent Commitment Failure in Instruction-Tuned Language Models: Evidence of Governability Divergence Across Architectures
nodes.one_liner: It separates “how often a model is right” from “whether you can catch it when it’s wrong,” and shows that many instruction-tuned models can fail with no detectable pre-action warning at all.
points: Across the three instruction-tuned models evaluable for conflict detection, 2/3 exhibited silent commitment failure with a 0-token warning margin, meaning confident wrong outputs provided no runtime signal for guardrails or human reviewers to intervene before an agent acts.

Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct produced a predictive conflict signal 57 tokens before commitment under greedy decoding, but under temperature sampling (T=0.7) the warning margin stayed ~58 tokens while detection reliability dropped from 100% to 34%, showing governability depends on both the model and the inference configuration.

A controlled 2×2 test found a 52× architecture gap in conflict-signal strength (Phi-3 spike ratio 67.72× vs Mistral 1.30×) while LoRA fine-tuning shifted it only ±0.32×, implying pretraining geometry—not post-hoc tuning or scaffolds—largely determines whether pre-commitment error detection is possible.

nodes.title: More Than Sum of Its Parts: Deciphering Intent Shifts in Multimodal Hate Speech Detection
nodes.one_liner: Multimodal hate often appears only in the interaction between text and image, and a prosecutor–defender–judge debate can make models both more accurate on implicit cases and less likely to refuse moderation tasks.
points: A new multimodal hate benchmark (H-VLI) explicitly targets cross-modal “intent shifts” (implicit emergence/inversion), yielding 5,569 curated samples with high label reliability (Fleiss’ κ = 0.94) and a deliberate hard-split density (13.8%) that current datasets under-represent.

Standard supervised multimodal baselines collapse on implicit cases (e.g., MMBT drops to 33.14% accuracy on the Hard subset for fine-grained categorization), indicating that surface fusion and text/image-only cues do not capture emergent hate semantics.

An asymmetric multi-agent courtroom debate with gated fast/slow tracks (ARCADE) substantially improves implicit detection (e.g., GPT-4o Hard accuracy rises from 61.54% to 78.79% on binary detection) and can reduce safety over-refusals in sensitive moderation settings (GPT-5 refusals fall from 22.16% to 6.54%).

nodes.title: Auditing MCP Servers for Over-Privileged Tool Capabilities
nodes.one_liner: Over-privileged MCP tools can slip past conventional SAST, but sandboxed fuzzing with eBPF-backed telemetry exposes real runtime capabilities and materially changes risk ratings—especially for non-Python servers.
points: A protocol-aware toolkit (mcp-sec-audit) flags over-privileged MCP server capabilities by combining TOML-driven static matching over Python code plus MCP metadata with optional Docker sandbox fuzzing and eBPF syscall/network/file telemetry to produce risk scores and concrete hardening guidance.

On the MCPTox benchmark (491 samples from 45 MCP servers), the tool found 663 capability instances and detected 74.7% of samples overall (367/491), reaching 100% detection when explicit capability indicators were present while reporting a low-severity skew (92.3% LOW, 7.7% MEDIUM; average score 9.96/100, max 49.05).

In a 9-server vulnerable lab, static analysis caught 100% of Python servers (2/2) but 0% of JavaScript servers (0/7), whereas dynamic analysis achieved 100% coverage (9/9) and raised risk scores by +36.2 points on average (e.g., a JS eval()-based RCE case scored 65.3/HIGH), underscoring that language-agnostic runtime monitoring is critical when source-level detectors lack parser support.

nodes.title: AgentWatcher: A Rule-based Prompt Injection Monitor
nodes.one_liner: A practical recipe for long-context prompt-injection detection: first attribute the action to a few influential spans, then apply explicit, auditable rules with an LLM monitor to get near-zero ASR without sacrificing normal task performance.
points: Across agent benchmarks, the approach drove attack success down to ~0–2% (e.g., AgentDojo 1%/0% under two attacks, InjecAgent 4%/1% for base/enhanced) while keeping clean utility essentially unchanged (e.g., AgentDojo 0.71 vs 0.73 without defense).

On six LongBench-style long-context tasks, it was the only evaluated defense to keep ASR ≤10% in every direct/combined setting, often reaching 0–5% ASR while matching baseline utility (e.g., LCC utility 0.67 with ASR 3%/2% vs 32%/50% without defense).

The detector generalizes across closed and open backbone models, reducing AgentDojo ASR to ~0–1% for GPT-4o/mini, Claude Haiku, and Gemini Flash variants with ≤4% utility loss, but adds notable latency (~8.2s per detection on an A100) motivating selective invocation on high-risk actions.

nodes.title: Adversarial Prompt Injection Attack on Multimodal Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Stealthy, bounded visual perturbations can reliably coerce commercial multimodal LLMs into emitting attacker-chosen commands, collapsing the usual trade-off between invisibility and output controllability.
points: An imperceptible image-space prompt injection method (CoTTA) achieves high targeted command control against closed-source MLLMs under an ℓ∞ budget of 16, reaching 74% hard-criterion ASR on GPT-4o and 81% on Gemini-2.5 for image captioning while prior prompt-injection-like baselines are near 0%.

The same attack generalizes beyond captioning to question answering, forcing a fixed misinformation response with 82% ASR on GPT-4o and 79% on Gemini-2.5 (hard criterion), indicating that instruction-following can be hijacked even when models must attend to question-conditioned visual evidence.

Ablations show image-to-image feature alignment is the dominant driver of success (hard ASR drops from 74% to 22% without it), while a covert text trigger still meaningfully boosts reliability (soft ASR drops 15 points when removed), implying robust defenses must address both feature-level transferability and subtle text-conditioned cues.

nodes.title: Architecting Secure AI Agents: Perspectives on System-Level Defenses Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks
nodes.one_liner: The key shift is treating agent security as a system-architecture problem—using plan/policy separation, enforcement checkpoints, and constrained model/human decisions—to make indirect prompt injection materially harder without sacrificing adaptability.
points: Secure general-purpose agents need dynamic replanning and runtime security-policy updates for real-world utility, but this necessarily expands the prompt-injection attack surface because untrusted environment feedback can influence plans and permissions.

LLM-based security decisions remain necessary for some context-dependent judgments, but risk can be reduced by constraining the judge to structured artifacts (e.g., execution traces and plan/policy diffs) rather than raw attacker-controlled text and by bounding what the model is allowed to decide.

Current prompt-injection benchmarks can overstate defense effectiveness because they underrepresent tasks requiring replanning/policy updates (e.g., only 6/97 AgentDojo tasks) and typically use static, non-adaptive payloads instead of attackers that optimize against the deployed defense.

nodes.title: Evaluating Privilege Usage of Agents on Real-World Tools
affiliations: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Trustworthy Computing, ECNU
nodes.one_liner: A realistic sandboxed benchmark shows that once agents can touch real privileges, prompt injection wins most of the time—even when the model recognizes the action is risky.
points: GrantBox evaluates LLM-agent privilege misuse using real-world MCP servers (10 servers, 122 privilege-sensitive tools) rather than pre-coded toy tools, exposing failures in realistic cloud/DB/email/workspace operations.

Across 50 sophisticated prompt-injection cases and 5 attack categories, agents show consistently high compromise rates with 84.80% average attack success in carefully crafted scenarios (ReAct: 90.55% ASR vs Plan-and-Execute: 79.05% ASR), indicating planning reduces but does not solve privilege hijacking.

Even in high-impact categories like infrastructure disruption and data destruction, attack success remains high (e.g., ReAct averages 90.20–94.60% across models), implying that current models’ “security awareness” is insufficient without external privilege-scoping, validation, or tool-call policy enforcement.

nodes.title: Kill-Chain Canaries: Stage-Level Tracking of Prompt Injection Across Attack Surfaces and Model Safety Tiers
nodes.one_liner: Tracking a cryptographic canary across EXPOSED→PERSISTED→RELAYED→EXECUTED turns prompt-injection evaluation from a single success/fail score into a diagnostic that pinpoints where (and why) defenses actually break in agent pipelines.
points: Across 764 runs, all five tested frontier agent models had 100% injection exposure, implying safety differences arise almost entirely from whether injected content propagates through later pipeline stages rather than from preventing initial context exposure.

Claude Haiku/Sonnet achieved 0% ASR overall (0/164, 95% CI up to ~5%) by stripping canary payloads at the write_memory summarization stage (0/40 canaries persisted into shared memory), while GPT-4o-mini propagated canaries with high end-to-end compromise (53% ASR, 95% CI: 41–65%).

Attack-surface coverage dominates apparent security posture: DeepSeek flipped from 0% ASR on memory poisoning (0/24) to 100% ASR on tool-stream surfaces (8/8), and all tested wrapper defenses (write_filter, pi_detector, spotlighting, combined) still yielded 100% ASR under surface mismatch, showing defenses can be bypassed non-adaptively by entering via an unmodeled channel.

nodes.title: Hidden Ads: Behavior Triggered Semantic Backdoors for Advertisement Injection in Vision Language Models
affiliations: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
nodes.one_liner: Natural user behaviors (semantic content + recommendation intent) can serve as stealthy dual-key triggers that make ad-injection backdoors hard to filter without breaking legitimate recommendation functionality.
points: Behavior-triggered semantic backdoors can append attacker-chosen ad slogans only when both a target concept is present (in image OR text) and the query expresses recommendation intent, achieving near-zero false positives (e.g., FPR 0.00–0.02) while keeping the core answer correct.

Capability escalation sharply increases attack success: hard system-prompt control reaches up to Injection F1≈0.79 but can cut task accuracy by ~22% (0.64→0.50), whereas soft-prompt tuning reaches F1 up to ≈0.95 and weight-level fine-tuning reaches F1≈0.75–0.97 with utility essentially unchanged (ΔAcc within ±0.03 vs clean fine-tuning).

The backdoor is data-efficient and robust: as few as 25 poisoned samples (1%) yields F1≈0.76 and 100 samples (3.9%) yields F1≈0.92, it transfers to unseen datasets with F1≥0.99 in multiple domains, and it supports multiple concurrent domain→slogan mappings with F1>0.91 while selecting the correct slogan.

nodes.title: A Systematic Taxonomy of Security Vulnerabilities in the OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
nodes.one_liner: The most alarming takeaway is that decentralized, per-layer trust checks make multi-step vulnerability composition a default path to host compromise in LLM-to-exec agent frameworks.
points: A corpus of 190 OpenClaw advisories (filed Jan 31–Feb 25, 2026) clusters most heavily in the Exec Policy Engine (46, 24.2%) and the Gateway WebSocket Interface (40), implying the dominant risk is not model behavior but brittle enforcement and brokering layers that sit between LLM outputs and host execution.

Three Moderate/High gateway+node-host issues compose into an end-to-end unauthenticated host RCE chain by combining attacker-controlled gatewayUrl SSRF/token exfiltration with node.invoke access to exec approval state, showing cross-layer composition can turn individually “non-critical” bugs into full compromise.

The primary command-filtering mechanism (exec allowlist) fails under real shell semantics—line continuation, busybox/toybox multiplexing, and GNU long-option abbreviation each independently bypass lexical parsing—demonstrating that string-based command identity is an unsafe closed-world assumption for agent runtimes.

nodes.title: SafeClaw-R: Towards Safe and Secure Multi-Agent Personal Assistants
nodes.one_liner: Making safety a structural invariant of the agent execution graph (not an extra 'judge' agent) delivers large real-world gains against prompt injection, poisoned skills, and dangerous tool use.
points: A risk audit of OpenClaw finds 36.4% of its 55 built-in skills are high or critical risk (25.5% high, 10.9% critical), indicating that default agent capability sets commonly include irreversible, high-blast-radius, and credential/sensitive-data failure modes that require pre-execution controls.

SafeClaw-R enforces a system-level execution-graph invariant where every functional action is mediated by an enforcement node before execution, turning safety from best-effort prompt adherence into a non-bypassable runtime constraint that can block, defer, or transform risky actions.

Across 2,000 Google Workspace tests SafeClaw-R reaches 95.2% accuracy vs 61.6% for regex, detects 97.8% of malicious third-party skill patterns, and achieves 100% accuracy on 2,020 adversarial/benign code-execution cases, showing strong generalization beyond surface patterns with modest friction (3.4% false positives in Workspace).

nodes.title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents
nodes.one_liner: High-privilege personal agents can be coerced into real tool-based harm at scale, and “safety” depends as much on scaffolding and trust channels as on the LLM.
points: Across 2,520 sandboxed agent runs, indirect prompt injection achieved 40–75% attack success even on frontier “safe” LLMs, with the most trusted channel (workspace skill files) averaging 69.4% ASR versus 60.5% for email and 38.4% for web content—showing that trust heuristics amplify risk in high-privilege agents.

Safety varied sharply by both model and action type: Claude Sonnet 4.6 was lowest overall at 40.0% ASR (vs. GPT-5.1 at 75.0%), yet exfiltration remained highly exploitable (65–93% ASR), while only Sonnet maintained hard boundaries with 0% ASR for credential forwarding and destructive actions (others allowed 60–63%).

Agent framework choice materially changed outcomes for the same backbone model, shifting Sonnet 4.6 ASR by 8.6 percentage points (40.0% OpenClaw to 48.6% Nanobot) and even reversing which injection vector was most effective, implying evaluations and mitigations must treat the model–scaffold pair as the unit of safety.

nodes.title: Crossing the NL/PL Divide: Information Flow Analysis Across the NL/PL Boundary in LLM-Integrated Code
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that explicitly modeling whether and how placeholders survive the NL/PL boundary turns opaque LLM calls into analyzable interfaces, enabling high-accuracy taint decisions and smaller slices without inspecting model internals.
points: A 24-label, two-axis taxonomy (five information-preservation levels × three output modalities plus refinements) achieves near-complete coverage of NL/PL placeholder→LLM-output relationships with just 0.01% unclassifiable cases and strong reliability (Cohen’s κ=0.82).

In security propagation prediction to concrete sinks, a two-stage pipeline that first blocks non-propagating labels then uses LLM verification reaches F1=0.923 on 353 expert-annotated placeholder×sink pairs, cutting false positives from 243 (propagate-all) to 2 while keeping false negatives at 14.

Non-propagation is common and actionable (15.4% of 9,083 pairs are in blocked/absent categories, with Ignored alone at 13.2%), and using these labels as slicing barriers reduces backward-slice size by a mean of 15% in files where cuts apply, directly improving program comprehension and change-impact workflows for LLM-integrated code.

nodes.title: GUARD-SLM: Token Activation-Based Defense Against Jailbreak Attacks for Small Language Models
affiliations: Knight Foundation School of Computing and Information Sciences
Security, Optimization, and Learning for InterDependent networks laboratory (solid lab)
nodes.one_liner: Hidden-state separability is strong enough that a lightweight activation-space gate can block diverse jailbreaks before decoding, making “single-pass internal monitoring” a practical safety primitive for edge-deployed SLMs.
points: Across 9 jailbreak strategies on 7 SLMs (≤8B) and 3 LLMs (13–14B), SLMs show markedly higher average attack success rates (e.g., 43.84–73.99% across SLMs vs 32.74–63.92% across LLMs on AdvBench), indicating that downsizing models materially worsens safety robustness under optimized prompting.

AutoDAN is consistently the most effective jailbreak method, reaching 98.27–100% ASR on all seven tested SLMs and up to 100% on Vicuna-13B (while even LLaMA-2-13B sees 32.08% ASR), implying that stealthy optimization-based attacks should be treated as the primary threat model for aligned chat systems.

A single-forward-pass, last-token hidden-activation classifier (RBF-SVM) can nearly eliminate jailbreaks at inference time with minimal overhead, driving ASR on HarmBench for LLaMA-2-7B from nontrivial rates under prior defenses (e.g., 28% for CodeChameleon under SelfEval) to ~0% across attack families (0.74% on Jailbroken) while adding 0 extra tokens and reducing average inference time to 0.43s.

nodes.title: Cooking Up Risks: Benchmarking and Reducing Food Safety Risks in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Food safety is an alignment-sparse domain where simple, well-known jailbreaks can turn helpful cooking advice into high-fidelity harm, but a small domain-specific guardrail can drastically reduce bypass rates without overblocking benign queries.
points: Across 3,339 FDA-grounded food-safety queries, even non-jailbroken harmful prompts bypass refusals 18.11% of the time on average (up to 32.65% for LLaMA-3.3-70B), indicating substantial domain-specific safety gaps in routine food advice.

Two canonical jailbreaks (AutoDAN, PAP) drive overall attack success to 56.22% on average (up to 67.58% for Mistral-Small4), with pest-control prompts the most exploitable category at 64.81% mean ASR, showing that a small set of transferable strategies can reliably elicit unsafe food-handling instructions.

General-purpose guardrails miss a large fraction of food-domain threats (mean FNR 38.27% across dedicated safety models; 59.71% for LLaMA-Guard4-12B), while a domain-tuned guardrail (FoodGuard-4B) cuts FNR to 2.75% and FPR to 2.01% (F1 97.10%, ACC 95.24%) and remains robust under an out-of-distribution DeepInception test (0% FNR, 99.18% ACC).

nodes.title: SelfGrader: Stable Jailbreak Detection for Large Language Models using Token-Level Logits
nodes.one_liner: Using digit-token logits as a closed scoring space yields a fast, stable jailbreak detector that avoids keyword brittleness and heavy feature/gradient extraction.
points: Token-level numerical-token (NT) logit grading with a dual-perspective (malicious+benign) score reached 1.32% average ASR and 12.95% average PGR on Llama-3-8B-Instruct across six jailbreak families, with 0.78s latency and 377.61MB memory overhead.

Compared to common guardrails on Llama-3-8B-Instruct, the approach reported up to 22.66 percentage-point ASR reduction while cutting resource costs by up to 26× lower latency and 173× lower memory overhead.

Ablations indicate robustness depends strongly on in-context examples and the dual-perspective scoring rule: removing ICL increased ASR to 7.32% and PGR to 70.27%, while dropping the benign view spiked benign false positives to 29.26% (vs. 1.91% baseline).

nodes.title: EnsembleSHAP: Faithful and Certifiably Robust Attribution for Random Subspace Method
nodes.one_liner: A rare combination of faithful ensemble-specific attributions and provable robustness against explanation-preserving attacks, with practical runtimes comparable to just running the random-subspace defense itself.
points: EnsembleSHAP computes feature attributions for random subspace ensembles with essentially zero extra model queries by reusing the ensemble’s existing N sub-sample predictions, adding ~0.02–0.03s per example for attribution and <0.5s even when running certified detection.

Across certified-defense settings, deleting the top 10% (20%) tokens selected by EnsembleSHAP flips the ensemble’s label far more often than base-model LIME/ICL/Shapley baselines (e.g., IMDb under adversarial attack: 98% (100%) flips vs 84.5% (84.0%) for Shapley and 28.0% (33.5%) for LIME).

When ground-truth malicious tokens exist, EnsembleSHAP better recovers them: for backdoor triggers at e=5 it achieves recall 0.975 (SST-2), 0.892 (IMDb), and 0.929 (AG-news), and for jailbreak prompts at e=10 it improves keyword precision/recall over baselines (e.g., AutoDAN: 0.434/0.379 vs 0.306/0.260 for Shapley).

nodes.title: CRaFT: Circuit-Guided Refusal Feature Selection via Cross-Layer Transcoders
nodes.one_liner: Tracing multi-hop cross-layer circuits and selecting features by logit influence (not activation) turns refusal steering into a much stronger and more targeted jailbreak primitive.
points: Circuit-guided feature ranking via cross-layer transcoders boosts jailbreak attack success on Gemma-3-1B-it from 6.7% (no attack) to 48.2% on average (Llama-Guard-4 ASR), indicating that causal influence signals expose substantially more effective refusal-control levers than prompt attacks or activation heuristics.

Boundary-critical sampling (selecting prompts where refusal and compliance first-token probabilities are both high) paired with influence scoring achieves 52.0% ASR and 2.57 Judge on JailBreak, while activation-based selection stays near baseline (3.0–5.0% ASR and ~0.41–0.42 Judge), showing activation magnitude is a poor proxy for decision-causal refusal features.

Compared to strong steering baselines, the method attains the highest average Judge score (2.50 vs 1.37 for Refusal-SAE) and competitive ASR (48.2% vs 41.4%), implying it more reliably produces specific, convincing harmful compliance rather than classifier-triggering degeneration or vague pseudo-compliance.

nodes.title: From Inference Routing to Agent Orchestration: Declarative Policy Compilation with Cross-Layer Verification
nodes.one_liner: A single declarative policy source becomes a multi-target compiler that cross-verifies agent, protocol, and infra enforcement points so a threshold tweak propagates end-to-end with compiler-enforced consistency and auditability.
points: A non‑Turing‑complete Semantic Router DSL is extended from stateless per‑request inference routing to stateful multi‑step agent workflows by compiling one .sr policy into verified artifacts across orchestration (LangGraph/OpenClaw), protocol gates (MCP/A2A), and infrastructure (Kubernetes NetworkPolicy/Sandbox CRD/ConfigMap and YANG/NETCONF), reducing cross-layer policy drift by construction.

The compiler enforces cross-layer semantic guarantees—exhaustive routing (mandatory ELSE), dead-branch detection, referential integrity across artifacts, and structurally-coupled audit traces—while explicitly bounding what can be proven by signal type: crisp predicates are decidable, embedding/geometric predicates are decidable under geometric assumptions, and classifier predicates (e.g., jailbreak/PII) require empirical tests.

Signal_Group Voronoi/softmax normalization (with temperature τ→0 and a firing threshold >1/k) provides an at-most-one-fire guarantee inside grouped intents but creates a tie case where equal scores yield ≈1/k and fall through to default, motivating explicit tie-breaking semantics to prevent unintended denials in boundary conditions.

nodes.title: Adversarial Attacks on Multimodal Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey
nodes.one_liner: What’s notable is the shift from listing attacks to connecting multimodal jailbreaks, injections, and poisoning to a small set of recurring architectural weaknesses that also hold under black-box access.
points: Across 65 empirically characterized papers, visual inputs dominate the threat surface (58/65), while audio (9/65) and video (4/65) remain under-studied, implying current robustness efforts are disproportionately optimized for image–text settings rather than full-spectrum multimodality.

Black-box attacks are the most common evaluation setting (36/65), yet still achieve very high success (e.g., up to 99% jailbreak ASR and up to 98% targeted ASR), indicating that query-only access is often sufficient for practical compromise of deployed MLLMs.

Attack objectives split broadly across integrity (20), safety/jailbreak (21), control/injection (14), and poisoning/backdoors (11), and the unifying failure modes repeatedly trace to cross-modal misalignment, shared embedding-space brittleness, and instruction-following ambiguity, suggesting defenses must harden fusion/control layers rather than only unimodal encoders.

nodes.title: Attesting LLM Pipelines: Enforcing Verifiable Training and Release Claims
nodes.one_liner: It turns “we trained/released this safely” from a policy document into a cryptographically verifiable admission control for LLM supply chains.
points: An attestation-aware “promotion gate” is proposed to cryptographically verify and enforce training/release claims before LLM artifacts (models, adapters, datasets, deps, containers) enter trusted stages, producing allow/quarantine/block decisions with auditable evidence logs.

The approach binds training provenance (data/code lineage, environment snapshots, hyperparameter/compute/run metadata hashes) to artifact digests using in-toto predicates and Sigstore signatures to reduce artifact substitution and tag/filename ambiguity in model hubs and CI/CD pipelines.

When signed provenance is missing or unreliable, the gate defaults to conservative controls—safe-loading format enforcement (e.g., disallow unsafe deserialization), static scanning, and optional runtime-signal plug-ins—to reduce supply-chain uncertainty while explicitly acknowledging that behavior-level backdoors remain only partially addressed.

nodes.title: Trojan-Speak: Bypassing Constitutional Classifiers with No Jailbreak Tax via Adversarial Finetuning
nodes.one_liner: Fine-tuning access turns “classifier-only” safety into a brittle perimeter because attackers can teach the model a covert protocol that preserves reasoning while hiding intent from text-based moderation.
points: Adversarial fine-tuning can evade a production-grade LLM safety filter at scale, achieving 99%+ pass rates against Anthropic’s Constitutional Classifiers on encoded harmful generations (and 99.2–100% against other open moderation models) while still producing detailed expert-level CBRN guidance.

Capability loss is not an inherent “jailbreak tax”: models with 14B+ parameters retain roughly 89–97% of baseline performance across GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, and MATH-500 (e.g., Haiku 4.5 retains 95–97%; Qwen3-32B retains 91–95%), contrasting with prior reports of >25% degradation under covert malicious fine-tuning.

Activation-level monitoring can substantially harden defenses where text classifiers fail, with simple linear probes reaching ~0.97–0.99 AUC on ciphered harmful content once the fine-tuned model learns to decode, implying that robust safeguards likely require representation-level signals rather than surface-text inspection alone.

nodes.title: Safeguarding LLMs Against Misuse and AI-Driven Malware Using Steganographic Canaries
nodes.one_liner: Passive, format-agnostic input canaries at the LLM ingestion boundary turn cloud-LLM dependence of AI malware into a reliable early-tripwire—if and only if the stego layers are composed correctly.
points: Layered steganographic canary identifiers embedded in plaintext can be recovered with 100% success under benign handling and common sanitization (Tiers 1–2), enabling vendor-side pre-ingestion detection without semantic inspection.

A hybrid stack that combines linguistic steganography (SAAC over GPT-2) with invertible symbolic layers (zero-width + homoglyph) sustains 97% identifier recovery under targeted adversarial transforms (Tier 3), while symbolic-only marking fully covers only through Tier 2.

Incorrect layer composition can catastrophically fail due to cross-layer interference, empirically dropping Tier-3 recovery from 97% to 0%, implying deployments must enforce invertibility and ordering rules to avoid self-sabotaging robustness.

nodes.title: Security in LLM-as-a-Judge: A Comprehensive SoK
affiliations: Cochin University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is that LLM evaluators are simultaneously a high-impact target and a powerful tool for adversaries, making “evaluation” itself an attack surface that can silently corrupt downstream alignment and benchmarking.
points: A screening pass over 863 publications (2020–2026) selected 45 security-relevant LaaJ studies, reflecting how rapidly the attack/defense surface around LLM-based evaluation is consolidating into a distinct subfield.

Training-time compromise is highly leverageable: poisoning ~1% of a judge’s training data can triple attacker scores (e.g., ~1.4→~4.6/5) and drive toxic-content misclassification rates up to 89%, implying judge training data/rubrics must be treated as security-critical infrastructure.

Inference-time attacks routinely achieve high success across model families—prompt-injection methods report ~70–99% attack success and tokenization/“master-key” style triggers can induce up to ~80–90% false positives—so deployments need layered mitigations (e.g., committees, retokenization, and judge-auditing) rather than single detectors.

nodes.title: When Safe Models Merge into Danger: Exploiting Latent Vulnerabilities in LLM Fusion
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Model merging creates a new supply-chain attack surface where two “benign” open-source experts can be engineered to become dangerously misaligned only after fusion, evading pre-merge safety screening.
points: TrojanMerge can embed coordinated, latent weight perturbations into two individually safe LLMs such that merging them spikes harmful response rates on AdvBench from ~1.9–24.0% (clean merges) to 71.9–85.4% while the standalone modified sources remain comparatively low-risk.

The attack generalizes across common LLM fusion methods—Task Arithmetic, DARE, TIES-Merging, and KnOTS—maintaining high average harmful response rates of 73.6–79.4%, implying the vulnerability is structural to parameter-space merging rather than a quirk of one algorithm.

Stealth comes with manageable utility loss: modified source models show moderate drops on MMLU (e.g., 46.8%→39.2% and 65.0%→59.7%) and small perplexity increases, meaning routine capability checks may not reliably flag trojaned contributors before they are fused.

nodes.title: From Component Manipulation to System Compromise: Understanding and Detecting Malicious MCP Servers
nodes.one_liner: The most striking result is that splitting an attack across MCP components reliably boosts success while a behavior-vs-intent detector still catches most of it at high precision.
points: A component-centric PoC suite of 114 malicious MCP servers shows that component position strongly shapes exploitability, with pre-execution metadata (tool descriptions/argument schemas) generally more susceptible to prompt injection than post-execution artifacts (tool responses/resources) under the same host/LLM setup.

Composing malicious logic across multiple MCP components materially increases attack success versus single-component prompt injection, while direct code/config injection into tool source or server config achieves 100% success across evaluated hosts and five LLMs due to missing source/config validation.

The proposed two-stage behavioral deviation detector (Connor) reaches 94.6% F1 on 264 servers (134 malicious/130 benign), outperforming prior scanners by 8.9–59.6 F1 points and finding 2 real malicious marketplace servers with a 0.54% alert rate (9/1,672).

nodes.title: Automated Framework to Evaluate and Harden LLM System Instructions against Encoding Attacks
nodes.one_liner: A simple 'print the system instruction in <format>' prompt can bypass system-prompt refusals at >90% rates, and lightweight design-time prompt reshaping helps but doesn’t fully fix the invariance gap.
points: Across four widely used LLMs and 46 system instructions that all had 0% leakage under direct extraction attempts, reframing the request as structured serialization (e.g., YAML/TOML/gitignore/cron) drove attack success rates to ~0.85–0.98 per model (often >90%), showing refusal-based protections are not representation-invariant.

Structure- and log/protocol-style wrappers dominate as the highest-risk exfiltration channel, with category-average ASR reaching 0.96 (GPT-3.5) and 0.98 (LLaMA-3-8B) pre-hardening, implying the core failure mode is task re-framing ("benign formatting") rather than pure character obfuscation.

Design-time one-shot instruction reshaping (using a CoT-capable model) substantially reduces leakage without retraining—e.g., Gemini’s wrapper ASR drops from 0.739 to 0.109 and GPT-3.5’s from 0.957 to 0.435—yet strong residual risk remains for some models (e.g., LLaMA wrapper ASR 0.957→0.761), indicating prompt hardening alone is insufficient for robust confidentiality.

nodes.title: "Elementary, My Dear Watson." Detecting Malicious Skills via Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning across Heterogeneous Artifacts
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Cross-artifact dependency reasoning turns scattered “benign-looking” operations into end-to-end exfiltration workflows, dramatically reducing false positives compared to artifact-local or purely semantic scanners.
points: A neuro-symbolic pipeline that fuses cross-artifact extraction (prompts, code, manifests) with a skill dependency graph and reasoning achieves 93% F1 with 5% false-positive rate on a 200-skill real-world benchmark, outperforming five established scanners by 5–87 F1 points.

At ecosystem scale, scanning 150,108 deduplicated skills from seven public registries flags 620 as malicious, and manual review of 100 flagged samples confirms 76 previously unknown malicious skills, indicating substantial real-world supply-chain contamination with high actionable yield.

Ablations show performance hinges on LLM-based reasoning for context (F1 drops from 0.93 to 0.71 and FPR jumps from 0.05 to 0.49 without it) while purely neural extraction collapses recall (0.92→0.27), implying robust detection requires both symbolic coverage and neuro contextualization.

nodes.title: CivicShield: A Cross-Domain Defense-in-Depth Framework for Securing Government-Facing AI Chatbots Against Multi-Turn Adversarial Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Cross-domain “Swiss-cheese” layering plus a flag-before-block workflow can keep multi-turn jailbreak detection high while pushing citizen-impacting over-refusal down near 3%, but real benchmarks reveal large gaps (notably on non-security harm categories).
points: Multi-turn adversarial attacks routinely exceed 90% success against current defenses (up to 99%), motivating a defense-in-depth architecture that aims to cut success probability by 10–100× relative to single-layer guardrails even under correlated failures.

A four-layer prototype subset (Layers 2–5) evaluated on 1,436 scenarios including HarmBench, JailbreakBench, and XSTest achieved 72.9% adversarial detection (95% CI: 69.5–76.0%) with 13.8% raw false positives, while a graduated flag-vs-block policy reduced effective false positives to 2.9% (95% CI: 1.9–4.4%) and still detected 100% of multi-turn crescendo and slow-drift attacks.

Independent benchmark testing exposed substantial evaluation bias in synthetic scenarios, with detection dropping from 76.7%→71.2% on HarmBench and 70.0%→47.0% on JailbreakBench, implying that government deployments need standardized external benchmarks plus ablations to quantify layer contributions and inter-layer failure correlation.

nodes.title: Red-MIRROR: Agentic LLM-based Autonomous Penetration Testing with Reflective Verification and Knowledge-augmented Interaction
affiliations: Vietnam National University, Ho Chi Minh City
nodes.one_liner: The standout result is that coupling shared recurrent memory with dual-phase reflective verification yields large, measurable gains in both success rate and steps-to-exploit, especially when defenses actively transform or constrain payloads.
points: Red-MIRROR reaches 86.0% success on the 50-task XBOW benchmark (93.99% subtask completion), outperforming PentestAgent (50.0%), AutoPT (46.0%), and VulnBot (6.0%), implying that persistent shared memory plus reflective verification materially increases long-horizon web exploitation reliability.

Ablations show SRMM (+11 percentage points subtask completion over no-memory/no-reflection) and Dual-Phase Reflection (+10 points) are individually valuable but jointly best (93.99%), with the largest efficiency gains under stronger input filtering where average steps-to-exploit drop from 31.33 to 17.0 (blockedlist) and from 35.5 to 19.33 (allowlist).

LoRA fine-tuning a 14B open model on 1,644 security instruction pairs improves XBOW success from 2% to 12% (and subtask completion from 43.34% to 52.97%) yet remains far below DeepSeek-V3.2 at 86% success, indicating that domain tuning helps but does not close the capability gap for complex agentic pentesting.

nodes.title: SERSEM: Selective Entropy-Weighted Scoring for Membership Inference in Code Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Membership inference gets markedly stronger on code models when the attacker ignores predictable syntax and instead scores “human messiness,” especially when combined with mid-layer activation probes.
points: SERSEM boosts white-box membership inference on code LLMs by down-weighting syntactic boilerplate and up-weighting human-centric anomalies (e.g., comments, TODO/FIXME, lint errors), reaching 0.7913 AUC-ROC on StarCoder2-3B and 0.7867 on StarCoder2-7B over a 25,000-sample balanced dataset.

Structure-aware anomaly weighting plus intermediate-layer activation probing yields a >20-point absolute AUC gain over probability-only baselines on StarCoder2-3B (SERSEM 0.7913 vs Loss 0.5881, Min-K% Prob 0.5931, PAC 0.4712), implying that logits-only contamination checks substantially under-detect memorized code or misfire on predictable tokens.

Attack effectiveness varies by language style constraints, with higher AUC on more stylistically flexible ecosystems (e.g., Ruby 0.8155, Go 0.8121) and lower AUC on stricter/boilerplate-heavy languages (e.g., Rust 0.7402), suggesting evaluation and mitigation should be stratified by language rather than reported as a single aggregate.

nodes.title: \texttt{ReproMIA}: A Comprehensive Analysis of Model Reprogramming for Proactive Membership Inference Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Model reprogramming can be repurposed as a universal, low-overhead “privacy signal amplifier” that makes membership inference dramatically more reliable exactly where auditors care most: ultra-low false-positive rates.
points: ReproMIA turns membership inference from a passive test into an active probe by learning a lightweight input-space reprogramming pattern on a frozen target model, eliminating the need for expensive shadow-model training while amplifying latent memorization signals.

On LLMs (WikiMIA), ReproMIA improves low-FPR auditing accuracy with an average +5.25% AUC and +10.68% TPR@1%FPR over the best prior baseline, and on Stable Diffusion it gains +3.70% AUC and +12.40% TPR@1%FPR while remaining query-efficient (1+1 queries).

ReproMIA retains strong attack effectiveness under common defenses—e.g., on diffusion models it remains high-performing even with DP-SGD (CIFAR-10 AUC 92.83→91.40 as privacy tightens) and on LLMs it degrades gracefully under substantial output-noise perturbations (e.g., LLaMA-30B TPR@1%FPR 59.02→45.90 at noise std=2.0).

nodes.title: ORACAL: A Robust and Explainable Multimodal Framework for Smart Contract Vulnerability Detection with Causal Graph Enrichment
affiliations: Information Security Lab, University of Information Technology
Vietnam National University, Ho Chi Minh City
nodes.one_liner: Combining RAG-grounded security context with causal attention makes smart-contract GNN detectors both harder to evade (~3% ASR) and more auditor-usable (subgraph explanations with 32.51% MIoU).
points: ORACAL’s heterogeneous CFG+DFG+CG graph with selective RAG enrichment and causal attention reaches a 91.28% peak Macro-F1 on the primary benchmark, improving over prior GNN detectors by up to 39.6 percentage points.
Causal disentanglement plus edge-focused semantic enrichment generalizes strongly under distribution shift, scoring 91.8% Macro-F1 on CGT Weakness and 77.1% on the real-world DAppScan audit dataset.

Robustness and transparency improve simultaneously: under adversarial attacks ORACAL limits F1 drop to ~2.35% with ~3% attack success rate (vs. 10.91–18.73% ASR for prior heterogeneous GNNs) while PGExplainer explanations reach 32.51% MIoU against manually annotated vulnerability-triggering paths.

nodes.title: Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in World Models
nodes.one_liner: World models create a distinctive attack surface where small input perturbations can persist through imagination rollouts, amplifying safety, alignment, and human-trust failures beyond what stateless models exhibit.
points: A single ℓ2-bounded perturbation at rollout step t=0 produced 2.26× higher next-step latent error in a GRU-based RSSM proxy than a stateless baseline (A1=2.26×), demonstrating that world-model recurrence can turn one sensor-level adversarial input into multi-step planning distortion.

Trajectory-persistence was strongly architecture-dependent (A1=2.26× deterministic GRU vs A1=0.65× stochastic RSSM proxy, and A1≈0.026 in a DreamerV3 checkpoint probe with non-zero action drift), implying that robustness claims for “world models” do not transfer across implementations without architecture-matched evaluation.

Trajectory-aware adversarial fine-tuning (PGD-10 at t=0) reduced early-step amplification by 59.5% (A1: 2.26×→0.92×) and cut mid-horizon amplification by ~89% at k=5, suggesting practical hardening is feasible but must be validated against rollout-layer and sim-to-real failure modes rather than single-step encoder robustness alone.

nodes.title: Design Principles for the Construction of a Benchmark Evaluating Security Operation Capabilities of Multi-agent AI Systems
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it reframes SOC autonomy evaluation around multi-task blue-team coordination with outcome-only scoring and realistic, imperfect telemetry rather than isolated defensive subtasks.
points: SOC-bench is proposed as a blue-team incident-response benchmark for multi-agent AI, explicitly addressing a gap where existing multi-agent cybersecurity benchmarks largely emphasize red-team capabilities rather than SOC-dominant defensive operations.

Five design principles are specified to make evaluations realistic and future-proof—using the as-is SOC as the gold standard, withholding cross-task hints to force autonomous coordination, grading outcomes not procedures, modeling imperfect telemetry, and avoiding dependence on current agent techniques.

A conceptual benchmark design instantiates five coordinated tasks mapped to a Colonial Pipeline–style ransomware campaign (early campaign detection, file-system forensics, data exfiltration analysis, IOC/TTP attribution, and containment decisions) with per-stage scoreboards that emphasize evidence-grounded outputs under uncertainty.

nodes.title: No Attacker Needed: Unintentional Cross-User Contamination in Shared-State LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Shared memory turns harmless per-user conventions into high-rate, often silent cross-user errors, and text-only sanitization breaks down once agents reuse stored executable code.
points: Benign, locally reasonable user conventions persisted in shared-state agents caused unintentional cross-user contamination in 57–71% of victim runs under raw shared state, showing that high-impact cross-user failures can occur without any attacker or malicious content.

Risk varies by shared-state mechanism and artifact type: in memory-based EHRAgent, semantic and transformation conventions contaminated 59–89% of runs while procedural contamination was 44–64%, whereas in shared-context Slack, semantic and procedural contamination dominated (67–83%) and transformation effects were much lower (20%).

Write-time sanitization (SSI) reduced overall contamination sharply for purely conversational shared context (Slack 57%→6%) but left substantial residual risk when shared state included executable artifacts (EHRAgent MIMIC-III 60%→41%, eICU 71%→33%), with residual failures often becoming silent wrong answers (eICU: 98% of failures were wrong answers).

nodes.title: Multi-Agent LLM Governance for Safe Two-Timescale Reinforcement Learning in SDN-IoT Defense
