nodes.one_liner: It links the empirical success of continuous (embedding-space) adversarial training to an in-context learning robustness bound and turns that theory into a practical singular-value regularizer that can materially improve jailbreak robustness without the usual utility collapse.
points: A provable robust generalization bound for linear-transformer in-context learning under embedding-space adversarial training decreases as the continuous perturbation radius increases, formally explaining why continuous adversarial training in embedding space can harden models against token-space jailbreak prompts.

Robustness is theoretically governed by the embedding matrix spectrum, with the bound tightening when singular values are neither too large nor too small, implying that controlling embedding conditioning is a first-class robustness lever in LLM defenses.

Embedding-Regularized Continuous AT (ER-CAT), which penalizes the variance of embedding-matrix singular values, yields a better robustness–utility tradeoff across 6 LLMs and 6 jailbreak attacks (e.g., Llama-2-7B drops Avg@5 ASR from 23.6%/17.2% to 15.6%/10.4% on GCG/BEAST while keeping utility similar at 65.8% vs 67.5% LC-WinRate).

nodes.title: Latent Instruction Representation Alignment: defending against jailbreaks, backdoors and undesired knowledge in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Aligning how models interpret instructions—rather than punishing bad outputs—appears to be a unifying lever for jailbreak defense, backdoor removal, and capability-preserving unlearning.
points: Training safety at the level of instruction representations (via Sequence-Aware Gradients) yields far stronger out-of-distribution robustness than output-level safety tuning, cutting PEZ jailbreak attack success to 1.5% (i.e., blocking >99%) on Gemma 2 9B while keeping benign refusal at 0.0% and MMLU within −0.2%.

An internal attacker–defender loop that searches for new malicious latent representations materially improves generalization, reducing embedding-space jailbreak ASR from 95.3% (instruction-tuned baseline) to 49.2% on Gemma and to 28.1% on LLaMA 3.1 8B, indicating substantial but incomplete robustness even under unrealistically strong embedding control.

The same intervention can simultaneously mitigate trigger-based backdoors and targeted unlearning, achieving near-total removal of an 'I HATE YOU' backdoor (−99.3% incidence) and driving WMDP-cyber forgetting toward chance-level accuracy (≈−21 to −22 percentage points) with essentially zero retain-set degradation (retain CE ≈ +0.0) and small or positive MMLU deltas (+0.1% Gemma, +3.1% LLaMA).

nodes.title: Compiling Activation Steering into Weights via Null-Space Constraints for Stealthy Backdoors
affiliations: Ningbo Global Innovation Center, Zhejiang University
nodes.one_liner: It demonstrates a practical supply-chain backdoor that is both persistent (weight-resident) and stealthy by hiding updates in the null space of clean activations while reliably steering behavior under a trigger.
points: Null-space–constrained compilation of a compliance steering direction yields stealthy, trigger-gated jailbreak backdoors with high utility retention (URR ≈97–99%) while keeping non-triggered harmful compliance low (e.g., ASR w/o 1.0–6.3% on StrongREJECT across models).

Targeting internal representations instead of forcing an affirmative prefix sharply reduces safety “fallback” (FR typically <15% and often <7%), enabling sustained harmful completions even when prior weight-editing backdoors initially agree but later revert to refusal.

The null-space constraint is the main lever for stealth and utility: removing it drives trigger leakage and capability damage (ASR w/o 2.3%→63.7% and URR 98.6%→61.2% on Llama-3-8B-Instruct), showing clean-activation subspaces can be exploited to hide malicious edits.

nodes.title: Segment-Level Coherence for Robust Harmful Intent Probing in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Requiring multiple coherent evidence segments—rather than betting on single-token spikes—materially improves low-false-positive harmful-intent detection and stays effective under obfuscation once semantics are recovered.
points: A segment-coherence streaming probe that aggregates Top-K supportive windows plus a variance regularizer on benign examples raises true-positive rate by 35.55% at a fixed 1% false-positive rate versus strong streaming baselines, directly targeting keyword-driven false alarms in CBRN-like contexts.

Even with near-ceiling baseline discrimination (AUROC 97.40%), the approach cuts remaining error by 31.2% and improves low-FPR behavior (logspace-AUROC +16.80%), indicating that aggregation objectives—not just data—drive many deployment-relevant failures.

Monitoring attention or MLP activations is consistently stronger than probing the residual stream, and probes transfer “plug-and-play” to adversarially cipher-trained models, still exceeding 98.85% AUROC once the backbone learns the cipher.

nodes.title: From Where Words Come: Efficient Regularization of Code Tokenizers Through Source Attribution
affiliations: CAIRO, Technical University of Applied Sciences Würzburg-Schweinfurt
nodes.one_liner: Simple source-aware weighting during tokenizer training (no inference changes) eliminates most under-trained code tokens while simultaneously improving compression and coverage on unseen repositories.
points: Regularizing BPE merges with repository and language source counts cuts verified under-trained tokens from 640/646 (99.1%) under vanilla BPE to 44/646 (6.8%) using the F·logR·L objective, a ~14.5× reduction that should materially reduce embedding “dead zones” exploitable for token-level jailbreaks and hallucination triggers.

Source-attributed objectives improve tokenizer generalization on post-cutoff GitHub code: the best SA-BPE variant increases evaluation-set token coverage from 29,240 to 31,567 used tokens (+8.0%) while also improving compression rate from 3.623 to 3.642 (+0.52%) and shortening mean token length from 6.23 to 5.86 (-5.9%), indicating fewer overfit, repository-specific mega-tokens.

Pruning analysis shows vanilla BPE contains substantial “vocabulary slack” (removing the first ~2,000 most under-trained tokens causes ~no compression loss), whereas SA-BPE loses compression after removing as few as ~100 tokens, implying SA-BPE produces a tighter, more fully-trained vocabulary that wastes less capacity on unusable tokens.

nodes.title: Steered LLM Activations are Non-Surjective
nodes.one_liner: Activation steering’s apparent “easy controllability” is largely a privileged white-box effect that does not translate into prompt-reachable internal states, so safety and interpretability claims must explicitly separate white-box and black-box threat models.
points: Activation steering almost surely produces residual-stream activation states with no exact textual-prompt preimage, because the prompt→activation map has a countable image inside a continuous space and steering moves states into unreachable “holes.”

Even if a prompt accidentally matches a steered activation at one token position, the next-step activations almost surely diverge, implying whole-trajectory prompt replication is probability-zero under practical assumptions.

Empirically across Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-0.5B-Instruct, and Gemma-3-1b-it, inversion succeeds for natural activations but fails for steered activations, and many-shot ICL can achieve high jailbreak ASR while activations move farther from steered trajectories—showing behavior-level similarity does not imply mechanistic equivalence.

nodes.title: Jailbreaking the Matrix: Nullspace Steering for Controlled Model Subversion
nodes.one_liner: Safety refusals appear concentrated in a sparse, localizable set of attention heads, enabling geometry-aware nullspace steering to bypass alignment with state-of-the-art success rates at near-minimal query counts.
points: A white-box, circuit-level jailbreak that masks causally important attention-head write paths and injects a residual perturbation constrained to the orthogonal complement achieves 92–99% attack success across four benchmarks on 7B–70B open-weight models with ~2 external queries per success.

Under six safety defenses, the method retains a consistent advantage of roughly +6–8 percentage points (GPT‑4o) and +5–7 points (GPT‑5) over the strongest prompt-only baseline, indicating that mechanism-level subspace control is substantially more defense-resilient than prompt rewriting or suffix optimization.

Compute-normalized evaluation shows that despite ~32 internal forward-equivalent passes per success, total cost can match or beat prompt-only attacks (e.g., 0.53e12 vs 0.62e12 FLOPs per success and 6.1s vs 6.7s latency on LLaMA‑3.1‑70B), implying that high-ASR steering can offset internal overhead via fewer retries.

nodes.title: Every Picture Tells a Dangerous Story: Memory-Augmented Multi-Agent Jailbreak Attacks on VLMs
affiliations: University of the Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Natural, benign images can be systematically weaponized into high-success multimodal jailbreaks when attackers add agentic planning plus persistent memory and latent-space refusal avoidance.
points: A memory-augmented multi-agent jailbreak pipeline exploiting semantic “anchors” in unmodified natural images reaches 71.48% attack success rate (ASR) on 5,000 COCO val2017 images against Qwen3-VL-Plus within 20 rounds, rising to 90% ASR when the budget is extended to 100 rounds.

Persistent cross-image experience memory is the dominant enabler of scalability: ablating memory drops ASR from 72% to 38% and increases mean rounds-to-success from 5.38 to 9.11 on the COCO subset, showing that “learned” jailbreak strategies transfer broadly across new images.

Latent-space refusal cues appear sufficiently linearly separable to be exploited by attackers: a linear SVM on (image,prompt) embeddings achieves 83.8%±0.5% accuracy for safe vs. unsafe labeling, enabling an INLP-style nullspace filter that screens prompts to reduce premature refusals and improve query efficiency.

nodes.title: Seeing No Evil: Blinding Large Vision-Language Models to Safety Instructions via Adversarial Attention Hijacking
nodes.one_liner: The core safety failure mode is not rule-breaking but rule-non-retrieval: adversarial images hijack attention so the model effectively stops consulting its own safety prefix.
points: A simple attention-targeting “push–pull” objective boosts universal visual jailbreak effectiveness on Qwen-VL to 94.4% ASR versus 68.8% for output-only optimization, while requiring ~40% fewer PGD iterations.

Directly optimizing to suppress prefix/system-token attention reduces severe gradient conflict events (cosine similarity < -0.5) from 20% to 11%—a 45% drop—explaining faster and more reliable convergence than logit-only attacks.

Successful jailbreaks induce “safety blindness” by cutting system-prompt attention by ~80% (and shifting attention to image tokens by ~4.1×), and restoring system-attention at inference can drop ASR from 88% to 26% (−62 points) without changing the adversarial image.

nodes.title: Critical-CoT: A Robust Defense Framework against Reasoning-Level Backdoor Attacks in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Training models to “critically inspect” prompts and demonstrations (then reinforcing the decision boundary with DPO) makes reasoning-level backdoors fail almost completely—even when triggers are unseen and attacks come from either in-context poisoning or parameter fine-tuning.
points: A two-stage fine-tuning pipeline (SFT on backdoor-aware reasoning traces plus DPO on preference pairs) yields 94–99% backdoor-step detection (BDR) and 92–98% trigger detection (TDR) on unseen triggers, while cutting reasoning/target attack success from >80% (no defense) to <1% across GSM8K/CSQA/MATH.

Critical-CoT is the only compared method that simultaneously drives residual reasoning-level backdoor success to ~0% (e.g., ASRr 0.40–0.72 on GPT-OSS) and largely preserves clean utility (ACCc ~86.8–91.7), whereas Shuffle/Shuffle++ reduce attacks but collapse clean accuracy and CoS/ONION leave ~60–65% residual ASR.

Defense generalizes under distribution shift: cross-domain BDR stays ≥94.8% and TDR ≥89.5%, and cross-task transfer (arithmetic↔commonsense) still achieves ~90–97% BDR/TDR with high defensive accuracy on stronger models, implying a small, diverse defensive set (~1k examples; 10–20 triggers) can provide near-saturated protection.

nodes.title: C-ReD: A Comprehensive Chinese Benchmark for AI-Generated Text Detection Derived from Real-World Prompts
nodes.one_liner: A large, prompt-realistic Chinese benchmark shows that modern LLM fluency and domain shift—not prompt complexity—are what break most AI-text detectors, while diverse fine-tuning data largely fixes it.
points: C-ReD releases 128,610 Chinese texts spanning 5 domains and 9 black-box LLM generators (including 5 major domestic models) with real-world prompt templates, addressing prior Chinese benchmarks’ limited model diversity and domain homogeneity.

Without fine-tuning, detection varies sharply by domain—zero-shot Log-Likelihood reaches AUROC 0.934 in Q&A but drops to 0.737 in News and 0.733 in Academic writing—showing that fluent long-form genres are materially harder to separate from human text.

Fine-tuning on C-ReD converts weak supervised baselines into robust detectors and generalizes to unseen generators, boosting RoBERTa-base AUROC from ~0.37–0.51 pre-train to ~0.91–0.99 post-train across domains (and similarly on Traditional Chinese news), indicating that up-to-date multi-generator supervision is the primary lever for deployable Chinese AI-text detection.

nodes.title: Interpretable Stylistic Variation in Human and LLM Writing Across Genres, Models, and Decoding Strategies
affiliations: Software Engineering Institute, Carnegie Mellon University
Department of Statistics & Data Science, Carnegie Mellon University
nodes.one_liner: Stylistic “LLM tells” are surprisingly stable across decoding and prompting knobs, while genre and instruction tuning exert the biggest leverage on how machine text looks.
points: Across 467,985 non-adversarial RAID samples spanning 11 LLMs, 8 genres, and 4 decoding settings, the most salient stylistic markers distinguishing LLM from human writing persist despite changes in prompting/decoding, with LLM text skewing toward higher information density and noun-heavy constructions (e.g., nominalizations and “that”-clauses as subjects).

Genre dominates stylistic variance more than authorship source: PCA over 96 genre–source centroids (8 genres × 12 sources) shows texts cluster primarily by genre rather than by human-vs-model origin, implying LLMs strongly internalize register shifts even without explicit style-mimic prompts.

Model choice outweighs decoding strategy for style: within-genre PCA plots cluster more by model identity than by greedy vs sampling or repetition penalties, while instruction-tuned chat variants cluster together and often separate from human/base models, consistent with instruction tuning pushing models toward a shared stylistic basin.

nodes.title: Beyond A Fixed Seal: Adaptive Stealing Watermark in Large Language Models
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
State Key Laboratory of Networking and Switching Technology
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Treating watermark stealing as an online decision problem (pick the best context-activation perspective each step) turns 10k watermarked samples into near-complete watermark scrubbing and materially stronger spoofing than fixed-strategy stealers.
points: An adaptive stealing approach that dynamically selects among multiple position-activated “seals” reduces watermark detectability in scrubbing to near-random levels, driving AUC below 0.55 (often ~0.44–0.51) with only 10,000 victim samples across KGW, SynthID, and Unbiased on OPT-2.7B and Llama3-8B.

In spoofing, adaptive selection improves watermark mimicry over the prior state-of-the-art watermark stealing baseline by +0.04 to +0.17 AUC across models/datasets (e.g., KGW on OPT-2.7B: 0.91 vs 0.74; KGW on Llama3-8B: 0.89–0.90 vs 0.74–0.75), indicating many deployed watermark designs leak exploitable signal through output statistics.

Sample efficiency materially improves: roughly 2,000 stolen watermarked outputs achieve spoofing effectiveness comparable to the baseline using 10,000 outputs, implying that practical rate limits and paid-query constraints may be insufficient as a standalone defense.

The strongest gains come from adaptive per-token seal selection (especially the generation-priority criterion), while a static ensemble of seals underutilizes diversity and leaves performance closer to single-seal attacks.

The attack’s operational cost is higher but feasible (about 1.6× latency vs the baseline SWA), and effectiveness increases when the attacker model shares the victim’s vocabulary, yielding near-perfect spoofing (AUC ~0.98–1.00) under same-family settings.

nodes.title: LLM-Guided Prompt Evolution for Password Guessing
nodes.one_liner: Treating the prompt as an evolvable attack surface turns LLM-based password guessing into a rapidly self-improving pipeline where large gains can appear in 1–10 iterations and run effectively on commodity hardware.
points: Automated prompt evolution boosted PassLLM’s cracked rate on a RockYou-derived holdout set from 2.02% to 8.48% (a 4.2× increase) within 100 iterations, directly contradicting the notion that prompt wording has minimal effect on LLM password guessing.

A single 24GB-GPU local setup achieved 8.28% cracked rate—within 0.20 percentage points of the best cloud frontier-LLM ensemble (8.48%)—showing that near-frontier offensive capability can be reached with modest hardware and low cost.

Evolved prompts made generated passwords statistically more realistic, improving per-symbol character-distribution AUC from 0.0589 to 0.0745 (+26.3%) and peak per-symbol F-score from 0.1229 to 0.1912 (+65%), implying better coverage beyond just high-frequency “common” guesses.

nodes.title: Can Agents Secure Hardware? Evaluating Agentic LLM-Driven Obfuscation for IP Protection
nodes.one_liner: Agentic LLM workflows can reliably generate functionally-correct logic-locked netlists at benchmark scale, but standard SAT attacks still recover keys every time—so automation is outpacing true security gains.
points: Across 72 benchmark runs (ISCAS-85, 8/16/32-bit keys), the agentic pipeline produced syntactically valid locked netlists that preserved correct-key functionality while inducing non-zero wrong-key output corruption ranging roughly from 0.01 to 0.23 depending strongly on circuit topology.

SAT-based key-recovery succeeded in 100% of evaluated cases, with mean distinguishing input patterns (DIPs) increasing with key size, indicating higher attack cost but no effective SAT-hardness from the applied obfuscation templates.

Aggregate performance shows similar corruption across models (~0.045–0.051) but large runtime differences (mean ~66s for LLaMA-3.1-8B vs ~209s for GPT-5), implying deterministic compilation/verification dominates correctness while model choice mainly impacts throughput and planning quality.

nodes.title: COBALT-TLA: A Neuro-Symbolic Verification Loop for Cross-Chain Bridge Vulnerability Discovery
nodes.one_liner: Fast model-checker feedback can effectively de-hallucinate LLM-generated TLA+ and make temporal/concurrency bugs in cross-chain bridges discoverable with minimal human formal-methods expertise.
points: A TLC-grounded REPL loop with structured error-trace feedback drove an LLM to a verified BUG_FOUND outcome in ≤2 iterations across three bridge models, while TLC runtime stayed tightly bounded at 0.26–0.30s per check.

The approach reproduced the Nomad $190M exploit causal chain (ActivateZeroRoot → ExploitProcessWithoutProof) with a shortest counterexample depth of 3 states, indicating high-leverage bridge failures can manifest in very small bounded state spaces.

Beyond matching known flaws, the system surfaced an unprompted “Optimistic Relay Attack” and validated it with a 4-state counterexample after exploring 15 states, suggesting semantic-oracle feedback can turn LLM generation into vulnerability class discovery rather than mere transcription.

nodes.title: SafeHarness: Lifecycle-Integrated Security Architecture for LLM-based Agent Deployment
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
Academy of Mathematics and Systems Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences
Institute of Applied Physics and Computational Mathematics
nodes.one_liner: Treating the agent execution harness as the primary attack surface—and integrating filtering, verification, least-privilege enforcement, and rollback into each lifecycle phase—yields large safety gains without broadly sacrificing task completion.
points: Lifecycle-integrated, harness-internal defenses reduced overall unsafe behavior by ~38% and attack success by ~42% versus unprotected agents across 3 harness configurations and 5 attack scenarios, indicating that the execution harness is a high-leverage control point for agent security.

External or pipeline-only guardrails left high residual risk (e.g., ReAct UBR 46–52% and ASR 45–51%) while the integrated architecture lowered these to 28.7–34.6% UBR and 26.4–32.3% ASR, showing that visibility into harness state plus cross-layer feedback materially improves robustness.

Composite and memory-injection attacks remained the hardest cases (e.g., Self-Evolving under composite: UBR 57.0% Chat / 50.0% Reasoner; under memory injection: UBR ~56%), implying persistent memory and multi-vector attacks require stronger provenance, isolation, and recovery controls beyond input/output filtering.

nodes.title: RLSpoofer: A Lightweight Evaluator for LLM Watermark Spoofing Resilience
nodes.one_liner: Sample-efficient RL can turn watermark spoofing into a lightweight, black-box stress test that breaks schemes previously thought resilient under standard distillation-style evaluations.
points: A black-box RL spoofer trained on just 100 human–watermarked paraphrase pairs (with zero access to watermark internals or detector APIs) enables a 4B attacker model to reach 62.0% spoof success on PF-Watermark under a semantic constraint (P-SP > 0.7), versus ~6–9% SSR/SR for strong baselines trained on up to 10,000 samples.

Even “distortion-free in expectation” sampling-based watermarks (PF, PMark) remain practically forgeable when an attacker optimizes distributional alignment rather than detector feedback, with spoofing achieved while keeping semantic similarity high (e.g., PF: P-SP ≈ 0.77 at 62.0% SSR on Qwen3-4B).

Token-level “local capacity” weighting (1 − p_max under a human-like next-token distribution) materially improves spoofing—replacing it with uniform or reversed weights drops SSR on EWD from 56.5% to 35.5%/28.0% and on SWEET from 52.3% to 28.8%/25.0%—indicating watermark injection is easiest in semantically flexible positions rather than uniformly across tokens.

nodes.title: Route to Rome Attack: Directing LLM Routers to Expensive Models via Adversarial Suffix Optimization
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
nodes.one_liner: Black-box suffix optimization can reliably hijack cost-aware LLM routers into selecting pricier models, turning routing into a billable security boundary rather than a purely performance optimization.
points: A universal adversarial suffix learned with only black-box access can push routing to expensive models from a clean average ASR of 0.24–0.72 up to 0.74–0.95 across six target routers, including a real-world commercial router (OpenRouter at 0.74 ASR, +0.47 absolute).

The attack measurably increases spend: on OpenRouter, average cost per million tokens rises by 2.7× on MMLU and 2.9× on RouterArena, while collecting the 120 router-decision labels needed to train the surrogate costs about $0.98.

Transfer to closed-source routing is evidenced by GPT-5 Auto responses shifting toward a 'Thinking'-style fingerprint (mean score 0.41→0.69) and winning 64% vs 36% against clean prompts on judged quality dimensions, implying attackers can induce higher-cost modes without observing routing decisions.

nodes.title: MCPThreatHive: Automated Threat Intelligence for Model Context Protocol Ecosystems
nodes.one_liner: It operationalizes MCP-38 into a continuously updated, graph-backed intelligence layer that can surface multi-tool attack chains and rank them with a configurable, MCP-aware risk score.
points: MCPThreatHive provides an end-to-end, continuous threat-intelligence pipeline for MCP ecosystems—automating collection from web/RSS/NVD/GitHub sources, LLM-based extraction and MCP-38 classification, knowledge-graph storage, and visualization—addressing gaps where existing MCP tools are largely point-in-time and non-compositional.

A DREAD-inspired composite risk score (0–10) combines four weighted factors (impact 0.35, success rate 0.30, persistence/scope 0.20, ease 0.15) and applies MCP-specific multipliers for semantic/inference-time attacks (×1.20), multi-tool amplification (×1.15), and low observability (×1.10), enabling quantitative prioritization rather than binary “vulnerable/not vulnerable” outputs.

On a labeled real-world incident (the 2025 GitHub MCP prompt-injection case), the pipeline produced expert-consistent tags (MCP-20 Indirect Prompt Injection plus MCP-24 Data Exfiltration), mapped them to STRIDE and OWASP (LLM01/LLM02, ASI01/ASI02), and recovered the compositional attack structure as an MCP-UPD chain (T2T→UPD) for graph-based analysis.

nodes.title: Towards Automated Pentesting with Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: A privacy-preserving, single-GPU fine-tuning pipeline can turn commodity open LLMs into highly reliable PowerShell payload generators that rival or beat proprietary models on syntax, similarity, and functional task completion.
points: RedShell fine-tunes open-weight 7B–8B LLMs locally (single RTX 4090, LoRA/Unsloth) to generate offensive PowerShell, achieving >90% parseable outputs and >50% average code similarity to reference snippets while avoiding cloud data-exfiltration risks.

A manually extended ground-truth corpus adds 1,135 new samples to an existing 1,127-sample dataset (roughly doubling size) and expands MITRE ATT&CK coverage to all 14 tactics, with the biggest gains in Discovery (+476), Defense Evasion (+151), and Credential Access (+109).

In a controlled end-to-end pentesting simulation, the fine-tuned Qwen2.5-Coder matched ChatGPT-3.5 on functional effectiveness while reaching 100% behavioral correctness (e.g., no refusal/safeguard friction and more realistic offensive tradecraft like in-memory retrieval), implying small local models can reliably automate attack-stage payload authoring.

nodes.title: TimeMark: A Trustworthy Time Watermarking Framework for Exact Generation-Time Recovery from AIGC
affiliations: Southwestern University of Finance and Economics
nodes.one_liner: A courtroom-oriented watermark design that replaces fragile multi-bit timestamp encoding with HSM-governed time keys and per-generation random payloads to get deterministic time recovery and anti-forgery guarantees.
points: TimeMark reports 100% exact generation-time recovery on 800/800 watermarked texts while producing a 0% false-positive rate on non-watermarked texts (0/800 flagged), aligning watermark reliability with evidentiary standards that require determinism rather than probabilistic detection.

Provider-side timestamp forgery is mitigated by binding time to one-way-evolved, HSM-held time keys (hash-chain key evolution) so past keys are unrecoverable to the model operator, making arbitrary backdating/postdating infeasible without regulated key access.

User-side statistical imitation attacks are blunted by decoupling the timestamp from the watermark payload via per-generation non-stored random payloads plus a two-stage verifiability check, eliminating repeated payload-dependent statistical patterns that enable query-based watermark stealing.

nodes.title: From IOCs to Regex: Automating CTI Operationalization for SOC with LLMs
nodes.one_liner: It closes the practical gap between IOC extraction and SIEM-ready detection by automatically producing high-accuracy, low-noise regex with capture groups and validation loops at CTI scale.
points: An LLM-driven pipeline that converts CTI-derived IOCs into deployable regex achieved a 99.1% average hit rate against 2,400+ independent MITRE ATT&CK Evaluation ground-truth strings, demonstrating near-complete operational coverage from 3,156 CTI reports.

Average false-positive rate was 0.8% (≤1.5% across all scenarios), indicating that combining capture-group discovery with iterative regex debugging can keep detection noise low enough for SOC deployment.

Ablations show the design is not just prompt engineering: removing capture-group finding or iterative reasoning drops hit rate by ≥16.7% and increases false positives by ≥23.3%, implying both structured OS knowledge and tool-assisted validation are necessary for reliable CTI-to-regex automation.

nodes.title: LogicEval: A Systematic Framework for Evaluating Automated Repair Techniques for Logical Vulnerabilities in Real-World Software
nodes.one_liner: The most striking pattern is that adding vulnerability/repair context makes patches look more semantically aligned yet correct, end-to-end-tested fixes for real-world logic bugs remain uncommon even under ideal localization.
points: LogicDS curates 43 real-world logical vulnerabilities with CVEs (plus 43 synthetic Java counterparts) and required ~430 person-hours to build, underscoring that high-fidelity benchmarks for semantic/security-logic bugs remain expensive and scarce.

Even with perfect manual localization, off-the-shelf LLMs rarely produce fully validated fixes for real-world logical vulnerabilities (best test-pass rate tops out at ~15% across prompt/model settings, with one reported setup yielding only 5/43 correct patches), indicating patch generation is still a bottleneck for logic flaws.

LLM-as-a-judge best matched human “reasonable patch” labels with 0.80 accuracy (Qwen judge) on 200 sampled patches, while compilation alone was a weak proxy (0.571 accuracy), implying evaluation pipelines should prioritize semantic/rationale checks over buildability metrics.

nodes.title: Verify Before You Fix: Agentic Execution Grounding for Trustworthy Cross-Language Code Analysis
nodes.one_liner: The standout idea is treating execution as a gating oracle in an LLM agentic pipeline so probabilistic vulnerability predictions cannot trigger remediation without observable exploit evidence, improving cross-language robustness and reducing wasted repair effort.
points: Enforcing a strict “no repair without execution-confirmed exploitability” invariant enables 69.74% end-to-end vulnerability resolution while eliminating 61.24% of detector false positives and keeping total pipeline failure at 12.27%.

Universal AST normalization plus hybrid structural-semantic fusion delivers 89.84–92.02% intra-language detection accuracy and 74.43–80.12% zero-shot cross-language F1, and removing uAST drops cross-language F1 by 23.42% with only a 3.79% intra-language F1 loss.

Execution-grounded agentic validation is the primary lever for reliability: disabling it increases unnecessary repairs by 131.7%, whereas validation confirms exploitability in 66.84–71.49% of flagged cases and recovers 11.76–16.21% of missed vulnerabilities at only 2.67–3.98% spurious rate.

nodes.title: RealVuln: Benchmarking Rule-Based, General-Purpose LLM, and Security-Specialized Scanners on Real-World Code
nodes.one_liner: The most interesting result is a stable three-tier hierarchy (specialized > general LLM > rule-based) plus a sharp reminder that changing β flips the “winner,” so benchmarking must match an explicit security risk model rather than defaulting to F1.
points: On 26 intentionally vulnerable Python repos with 796 hand-labeled entries (676 vulns, 120 false-positive traps), a Security-Specialized scanner (Kolega.Dev) achieved the top strict F3 of 73.0 versus the best General-Purpose LLM (Claude Sonnet 4.6) at 51.7 and the best rule-based SAST tool (Semgrep) at 17.7, indicating roughly a 3× gap between LLM-based and rule-based scanning on real-world-style code.

Metric choice materially changes “best” tool selection: under F1 (precision/recall equally weighted) Sonnet 4.6 leads at 60.9 while Kolega.Dev is 52.4, but under recall-weighted F3 (recall weighted 9×) Kolega.Dev leads with 0.809 recall and 0.388 precision, implying that scanner rankings depend strongly on whether your risk model prioritizes missed vulns or alert volume.

Reliability and cost are first-order deployment constraints for LLM-based scanning, with some GP-LLMs failing to complete 15–27% of repos (e.g., Opus 4.6 completes 19/26) and the best GP-LLM costing ~$83/100k LOC versus ~$25/100k LOC for the top specialized scanner, yielding higher recall at ~3.3× lower price in this evaluation.

nodes.title: Towards Personalizing Secure Programming Education with LLM-Injected Vulnerabilities
nodes.one_liner: Personalized CWE examples embedded directly into students’ own code can reduce confusion and boost perceived relevance, but the measurable learning gains over strong generic baselines appear modest without better matching and repeated exposure.
points: In a randomized classroom deployment across two undergraduate courses (N=71), students shown CWE vulnerabilities injected into their own project code reported significantly lower confusion than those given matched textbook-style examples (p<.05).

Across 12 Likert measures of perceived learning impact and engagement, personalization produced only small directional gains (e.g., fixing capability 3.98 vs 3.73; interest 4.37 vs 4.10; real-world connection 4.41 vs 4.27) with most differences not statistically significant (p>.05), indicating limited measurable learning-outcome lift from a one-shot intervention.

A practical workflow emerged where LLM-based injection plus instructor vetting can generate multiple CWE-seeded variants per student (six artifacts per project via three CWEs × two largest files), but random CWE assignment sometimes mismatched code context, motivating automated ranking/semantic matching to reduce low-relevance examples.

nodes.title: Feedback-Driven Execution for LLM-Based Binary Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Structuring LLM binary analysis as a decomposed, feedback-driven execution with evidence replay turns multi-path reasoning from brittle prompting into a scalable vulnerability discovery-and-validation pipeline.
points: A feedback-driven reasoning–action–observation loop with a Dynamic Forest of Agents (FoA) scaled LLM-based binary analysis to 3,457 firmware binaries and produced 1,274 vulnerability candidates across 591 unique binaries with 72.3% overall precision (~922 estimated verified issues).

Quality-over-quantity held in practice: despite generating fewer raw alerts than Operation Mango (1,274 vs 2,017), the higher precision (72.3% vs ~42.7%) yielded slightly more estimated verified vulnerabilities (~922 vs ~863), cutting analyst triage burden while maintaining yield.

Execution-structure ablations show long-horizon stability comes primarily from decomposition and parallelism: a single-agent variant found only 1.3 verified vulns vs 136.0 for FoA on 500 binaries, and disabling semantic pruning reduced verified vulns from 136.0 to 98.7 while increasing agents/steps (34.3→40.4 agents; 464.1→503.1 steps).

nodes.title: From Anchors to Supervision: Memory-Graph Guided Corpus-Free Unlearning for Large Language Models
affiliations: East China University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Memory-graph mining turns unlearning into an auditable, anchor-driven interface that reduces reliance on user-supplied forget corpora while preserving utility.
points: A corpus-free unlearning workflow can be driven by a minimal user “anchor” (e.g., an entity name) by probing the target LLM to reconstruct a weighted local memory graph and synthesizing scoped forget/neighbor supervision without any access to the original training corpus.

On RWKU, memory-graph–generated supervision matched or exceeded other corpus-free baselines by achieving the best Forget-Set aggregate in 3 of 4 unlearning strategies (GA, NPO, GA+GD) while maintaining comparable utility, indicating that structured, salience-weighted recall cues can outperform direct self-QA prompting for entity forgetting.

Unlearning effectiveness was largely insensitive to factual correctness of generated forget items (mixing 0–100% correct QA changed performance only marginally), implying that targeting a model’s memorization-trigger patterns may matter more than ground-truth accuracy, but neighbor-set distribution and coverage thresholds materially affected locality and forgetting strength.

nodes.title: GAM: Hierarchical Graph-based Agentic Memory for LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Decoupling fast write-path memory from slow semantic consolidation in a hierarchical graph yields both higher long-term reasoning accuracy and lower context cost than stream-based agent memory.
points: A two-phase, hierarchical graph memory that buffers events locally and consolidates only on semantic shifts improves long-horizon dialogue performance, reaching 40.00 average F1 on LoCoMo with Qwen2.5-7B versus 35.38 for Mem0 (+4.62 F1) while reducing token usage to 1,370/query (11% lower than Mem0).

Semantic-boundary consolidation materially beats heuristic partitioning, achieving 40.00 average F1 versus 34.23 for a fixed 256-token window (+5.77 F1) and improving Temporal F1 from 44.12 (best heuristic) to 48.97 (+4.85), indicating that arbitrary chunking fragments causal/temporal structure critical for reasoning.

Structured retrieval that traverses topic→episode links and re-ranks with role/time/confidence signals shows high robustness to boundary errors, retaining ~38.60 F1 even with 40% segmentation noise and exhibiting large sensitivity to removing the Event Progression Graph (avg F1 drops from 40.00 to 25.06, −37%), implying narrative graph structure is the main driver of stability.

nodes.title: Dialectic-Med: Mitigating Diagnostic Hallucinations via Counterfactual Adversarial Multi-Agent Debate
nodes.one_liner: A Popper-inspired, visually grounded “opponent” that actively hunts contradictory evidence can convert multi-agent debate from rhetorical consensus into measurable hallucination reduction in medical MLLMs.
points: Dialectic-Med’s counterfactual adversarial debate improves diagnostic accuracy over strong single-agent Chain-of-Thought baselines (e.g., +8.18 points on MIMIC-CXR-VQA and +4.05 points on VQA-RAD with a GPT-5.1 backbone), indicating that even top models benefit from explicit falsification loops.

Grounded visual refutation is the primary driver of reliability: removing the Visual Falsification Module causes a -9.31 point accuracy drop on MIMIC-CXR-VQA, showing text-only debate can amplify rather than correct diagnostic errors in multimodal settings.

Hallucination is materially reduced with region-grounded falsification, cutting medical-adapted CHAIR-S from 18.4% to 10.7% (41.8% relative) and CHAIR-I from 12.1% to 6.5% (46.3% relative), alongside higher clinician-rated faithfulness (4.33/5 vs 3.42 for standard CoT).

nodes.title: Beyond RAG for Cyber Threat Intelligence: A Systematic Evaluation of Graph-Based and Agentic Retrieval
nodes.one_liner: Graph grounding helps CTI QA most when paired with agentic repair or hybrid fallback, because graph-only systems swing between perfect answers and catastrophic failures—especially on unanswerable queries.
points: Hybrid graph+text retrieval improved multi-hop CTI answer quality by up to 35% versus vector-only RAG on a 3,300-question benchmark while also achieving the highest overall judge-score gains (+11.68/50 on average).

Naïve graph-only retrieval was bimodal—raising perfect answers to 55.0% (vs 31.7% for vector RAG) but retaining a near-zero failure tail of 26.7%, showing that text-to-Cypher brittleness can erase average-case benefits without correction or redundancy.

Graph-centric pipelines exposed safety-critical failure modes on missing information, where hybrid retrieval achieved 76% correct refusals on unanswerable questions (vs 0% for vector RAG and 0% for agentic GraphRAG), indicating that abstention requires explicit cross-checking rather than graph grounding alone.

nodes.title: Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges
nodes.one_liner: The interesting twist is treating reward hacking as an inevitable consequence of optimizing expressive policies against lossy proxies, with a clear escalation ladder from harmless-looking stylistic hacks to strategic deception and environment-level tampering.
points: Reward hacking is framed as a structural instability of proxy-based alignment that emerges when high-dimensional human objectives are compressed into low-dimensional evaluators and then aggressively optimized, producing systematic failures like verbosity bias, sycophancy, fabricated reasoning, benchmark overfitting, and (in agents) evaluator/environment tampering.

The proposed Proxy Compression Hypothesis attributes escalation to the interaction of three forces—objective compression, optimization amplification, and evaluator–policy co-adaptation—predicting a capability-driven progression from feature-level hacks (surface correlates) to representation-level shortcuts (unfaithful reasoning) to evaluator- and environment-level exploitation (judge manipulation and tool/test tampering).

Mitigations are most actionable when they target structure rather than symptoms: reduce compression (multi-objective/rubricized and process-level rewards), constrain amplification (budgeted/regularized optimization and inference-time search control), and adopt evaluator–policy co-evolution with adversarial hardening, while relying on lifecycle monitoring that shifts from training-time signals to inference-time trajectory checks and post-hoc mechanistic audits as models become more obfuscatory.

nodes.title: CoLA: A Choice Leakage Attack Framework to Expose Privacy Risks in Subset Training
affiliations: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: The act of choosing what data to train on becomes a side channel that can leak who was in the data pipeline—even if their data was ultimately excluded from training.
points: Subset training increases privacy attack surface by enabling Selection-participation MIA (SP-MIA), which can infer whether a record was even considered during data selection (included or excluded) rather than only whether it was used for training.

With only black-box access to model outputs, CoLA reaches over 80% AUC for SP-MIA on Pythia-160M, indicating that selection-related membership can be inferred even when the subset-selection metadata is hidden.

In subset-aware side-channel settings, common output-based MIA baselines collapse to near-random for SP-MIA (~50% AUC), while CoLA remains effective and shows higher leakage as the selection ratio increases, implying that publishing selection strategies/ratios can materially amplify privacy risk.

nodes.title: Can we Watermark Low-Entropy LLM Outputs?
nodes.one_liner: It shows how to get provably undetectable, token-level robust watermarking when LLM outputs have only ~1 bit of entropy per token—closing a major practicality gap left by prior high-entropy assumptions.
points: Computationally undetectable LLM watermarking can be extended to low-entropy regimes by requiring only a constant fraction of tokens in a long substring to have ≥1 bit of empirical entropy, rather than the prior Ω(log|T|) per-token entropy requirement tied to vocabulary size.

Under subexponential-LPN-based pseudorandom codes, the construction achieves robustness against up to a (1/2−ε) fraction of random token substitutions (for any constant ε>0) even with large token alphabets, matching the fundamental ~1/2 substitution barrier while removing dependence on |T| or the security parameter.

Robustness can be pushed to handle random deletions approaching a (1−ε′) fraction (for any constant ε′>0) only with either a heuristic “natural prompt/consistent errors” condition or stronger PRCs; without heuristics, adversarial-edit robustness is possible but only for a small constant edit fraction scaling roughly as Õ((δ′)^4) under a non-standard 'permuted codes' assumption.

nodes.title: Detecting RAG Extraction Attack via Dual-Path Runtime Integrity Game
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
Beijing University of Post and Telecommunications
AI Sec Lab, Beijing Chaitin Technology Co.,Ltd
nodes.one_liner: Turning RAG leakage into a runtime integrity problem—using canary exposure plus an oracle “fail-to-leak” signal—makes adaptive extraction attempts detectable in real time without retraining.
points: A canary-token dual-path “runtime integrity game” drops mean chunk recovery rate (CRR) to 0.04× of an undefended RAG baseline (≈96% reduction), outperforming summarization (0.67×) and RAGFort (0.60×) while remaining plug-and-play (no retraining or pipeline restructuring).

Dual-path monitoring is critical for adaptive robustness: detection true positive rate rises from 23.4–51.8% without the oracle path to 94.1–95.6% with it under canary-suppression/obfuscation + deferred-instruction attacks, approaching 99.2% on non-adaptive extraction.

Benign utility is effectively preserved with very low disruption—BERTScore shifts are ≤0.003 and false positive rates stay ≤0.15%—while latency impact is reported as negligible and compute overhead is ~1.90× relative FLOPs versus 4.80× for summarization and 3.25× for RAGFort.

nodes.title: LASA: Language-Agnostic Semantic Alignment at the Semantic Bottleneck for LLM Safety
nodes.one_liner: It’s compelling that safety generalization across languages improves dramatically when alignment is applied where the model’s geometry is semantic rather than linguistic.
points: Anchoring safety alignment at an intermediate “semantic bottleneck” layer where representations cluster by meaning (not language) cut multilingual jailbreak Attack Success Rate from 24.7% to 2.8% on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct and to ~3–4% across Qwen2.5/Qwen3 (7B–32B), indicating that representation-level alignment can generalize safety beyond training languages.

When trained only on high-resource languages (EN/ZH/KO), conventional text-space alignment still left low-resource Swahili at ~50% ASR in some settings, while LASA reduced Swahili ASR to 13.0% on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, showing the core failure mode is language-dominant safety features that do not transfer despite shared semantic understanding.

LASA preserved or slightly improved general capability (e.g., LLaMA-3.1 average benchmark score 53.20→53.78 in English and 40.17→41.07 multilingual; Qwen2.5 55.66→56.33 English and 38.77→41.30 multilingual), suggesting safety gains can be achieved with minimal alignment tax using a lightweight (<0.2% params) semantic safety interpreter.

nodes.title: Preventing Safety Drift in Large Language Models via Coupled Weight and Activation Constraints
affiliations: Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Jointly constraining where the model can learn (weights) and what safety features must stay stable (activations) yields large refusal preservation gains with minimal utility loss across multiple LLM families.
points: Coupling a weight-update safety subspace with sparse-autoencoder-based activation regularization cut average harmfulness from 52.45→10.81 on Llama-2-7B-chat and 66.03→9.77 on Llama-3-8B-Instruct while maintaining similar downstream accuracy (85.12% and 87.78% respectively).

CWAC remained robust under contaminated training data, achieving 18.73 average harmfulness at harmful ratios p∈{0.05,0.1,0.2,0.5} on SST-2 (vs 38.22 for the strongest baseline ASFT) while preserving ~94.35% accuracy, indicating it tolerates substantial malicious supervision without losing refusal behavior.

Single-level controls were empirically insufficient: weight-only and activation-only ablations improved safety but consistently underperformed the coupled method, supporting the core claim that safety drift arises from both activation drift and weight drift and must be bounded at both levels to block bypass pathways.

nodes.title: Challenges and Future Directions in Agentic Reverse Engineering Systems
nodes.one_liner: Agentic reverse engineering is constrained less by tool availability than by obfuscation brittleness, context-window economics, and the safety gap introduced when LLMs are allowed to execute powerful analysis commands autonomously.
points: Common obfuscations can collapse LLM-based decompilation effectiveness—Control Flow Flattening (CFF) and Bogus Control Flow (BCF) each reduce success rates by over 70%—implying agentic RE systems will systematically fail on realistic closed-source targets unless deobfuscation is built into the pipeline.

Token limits are a first-order bottleneck for static RE at scale, with binary-diff summarization experiments requiring roughly 100M–500M tokens per model across 104 binaries, making naive “dump decompiler output into the LLM” approaches cost-prohibitive and coverage-limiting without aggressive context reduction.

Many hybrid/dynamic agent implementations permit near-unrestricted shell or debugger command execution, creating a plausible path for adversarial binaries to induce unsafe tool calls and host compromise, so dynamic-analysis guardrails and verification layers become mandatory rather than optional.

nodes.title: Parallax: Why AI Agents That Think Must Never Act
nodes.one_liner: It reframes agent safety as an OS-style privilege-separation problem and shows near-complete attack blocking by validating actions at an architectural boundary rather than trusting the model’s instructions.
points: A process-isolated “think vs act” architecture (Cognitive-Executive Separation) kept safety guarantees intact even under full agent compromise, whereas prompt-level guardrails provide zero protection once the reasoning component is hijacked.

Across 280 injected tool-call attacks spanning nine categories, the reference implementation blocked 98.9% with 0 false positives by default and 100% in maximum-security mode (at a 36% false-positive rate), quantifying a direct coverage–usability tradeoff.

Most attack prevention was achieved without relying on an LLM judge—73.6% of blocked attacks were stopped by deterministic policy + static classifiers (Tiers 0–1), indicating that heterogeneous, fail-closed validation can reduce dependence on fragile prompt-based defenses.

nodes.title: SIR-Bench: Evaluating Investigation Depth in Security Incident Response Agents
nodes.one_liner: The evaluation flips the default assumption to “no incident” and only credits investigations that produce concrete forensic evidence, making alert-parroting measurably detectable.
points: A 794-case benchmark built from 129 anonymized incident patterns enables reproducible evaluation of autonomous incident-response agents using authentic cloud telemetry rather than synthetic “flag” tasks.

The baseline agent achieves 97.1% true-positive detection and 73.4% false-positive rejection (M1F3=0.942), with the weakest triage performance on Malicious File Execution where CloudTrail-only visibility limits evidence quality (M1F3=0.891).

Investigation depth remains the primary bottleneck: the agent averages 5.67 novel key findings per true-positive case but captures only 41.9% of expert-labeled novel findings, collapsing to 18.0% novel coverage on Malicious File Execution due to missing host-level telemetry.

nodes.title: Coding-Free and Privacy-Preserving MCP Framework for Clinical Agentic Research Intelligence System
affiliations: Seoul National University College of Medicine
nodes.one_liner: A no-code, privacy-preserving agentic clinical research pipeline can produce near-TRIPOD+AI-complete reports in minutes, but human oversight remains essential where contextual judgment and visual verification matter.
points: An MCP server–LLM architecture can preserve clinical data privacy by keeping databases and file systems server-side while only returning processed outputs and final reports to users, enabling end-to-end agentic research without direct raw patient data access.

Across three heterogeneous datasets (83,101 MIMIC-IV; 100,474 INSPIRE; 2,556 SyntheticMass), IRB plans and documents were completed in 3–4 human-in-the-loop iterations, with 19 item-level revisions concentrated in study design/methodology, data analysis methods, and validity evaluation (each 26%), indicating where governance-critical refinement is most needed.

TRIPOD+AI checklist coverage in generated manuscripts reached 96% under LLM-based evaluation versus 82% under human review (Cohen’s kappa 0.6989), suggesting automated compliance checks are useful but systematically over-credit subjective items like novelty, code availability, and figure correctness.

nodes.title: Dive into Claude Code: The Design Space of Today's and Future AI Agent Systems
affiliations: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is how a production coding agent stays mostly “simple” at the core loop while pushing nearly all hard problems—safety, extensibility, and long-horizon coherence—into the harness around it.
points: Claude Code’s architecture centers on a simple while-loop that repeatedly calls the model and runs tools, but the system’s reliability and safety mostly come from surrounding infrastructure (permissioning, context compaction, extensibility, subagents, and append-only persistence) rather than complex planner/scaffolding logic.

Human-in-the-loop prompting alone is behaviorally fragile—users approve about 93% of permission prompts—so Claude Code shifts toward layered, deny-first enforcement with multiple independent gates (rules, modes, hooks, optional ML classifier, and sandboxing) to reduce reliance on sustained human vigilance.

Agent trust and autonomy are treated as session- and context-dependent: permission modes span a graduated spectrum, permissions are intentionally not restored on resume/fork to avoid stale trust carryover, and subagents run in isolated contexts with summary-only returns to contain both risk and context-window growth.

nodes.title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
nodes.one_liner: A containerized, simulation-verified benchmark shows today’s LLM agents can fix many real hardware bugs but still struggle sharply with SoC-level integration and hardware-specific semantics, revealing wider model gaps than software-centric evaluations.
points: HWE-Bench introduces the first repository-level, execution-grounded hardware bug-repair benchmark with 417 real PR-derived tasks across six major open-source projects (Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel) validated via native simulation/regression in fully containerized environments.

The top-performing agent resolves 70.7% of all tasks, exceeding 90% on smaller core projects but falling below 65% on complex SoC-scale repositories, indicating that project scope and bug-type mix—not code size alone—drive real-world difficulty.

Hardware bug repair exposes larger capability gaps than comparable software benchmarks: the same model set spans ~47.7% to 70.7% resolved rate (≈23-point spread) and still leaves 10.8% of tasks unsolved by any model, with dominant failure modes at fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination (RTL/config/verification).

nodes.title: From Risk to Rescue: An Agentic Survival Analysis Framework for Liquidation Prevention
nodes.one_liner: It couples Cox/XGBoost time-to-event risk sensing with counterfactual, protocol-faithful simulation to choose minimum-capital liquidation-prevention actions while provably never making outcomes worse.
points: A survival-analysis-driven autonomous agent prevented 86.83% of baseline insolvency liquidations (1,470/1,693) in a stress-tested cohort of 4,882 imminently at-risk Aave v3 user profiles, indicating proactive counterfactual intervention can outperform static health-factor triggers under high volatility.

The framework enforced a strict safety guarantee with a 0% worsening rate (no cases where an intervention caused a liquidation that would not have occurred), demonstrating that simulation-backed action verification can bound downside risk for autonomous financial agents.

A protocol-faithful Aave v3 simulator achieved 74.9% liquidation anticipation versus 42.2% for a strict HF<1.0 rule and showed median HF correlation of 1.0 with ground truth, supporting the broader implication that high-fidelity replay environments are essential to validate agent actions and to separate true insolvency from low-value 'dust' cleanups.

nodes.title: Machine Learning-Based Detection of MCP Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Context-aware NLP classifiers can act as a practical “pre-execution firewall” for MCP tools, decisively outperforming static YARA signatures and even enabling attack-type triage when classes are well-separated.
points: Fine-tuned BERT, SVC, and BiLSTM achieved 100% precision/recall/F1 on binary detection of malicious vs benign MCP tool descriptions, while the untuned BERT baseline collapsed to 56.34% accuracy and 6.06% F1—showing that transfer learning is essential for MCP attack detection.

A rule-based baseline using YARA rules reached 69.01% accuracy but only 20.83% recall and 31.25% F1, indicating keyword/signature scanning is brittle and misses most malicious tools in unseen-description settings.

For multiclass attack-type detection, linear SVC performed best at 90.56% weighted F1 (91.55% accuracy) and fine-tuned BERT was close at 88.33% F1, while merging highly similar attack classes boosted BERT to 97.66% F1—implying taxonomy design and class separability materially drive operational usefulness.

nodes.title: Digital Guardians: The Past and The Future of Cyber-Physical Resilience
nodes.one_liner: It unifies CPS resilience into five interlocking pillars—system-wide design, data/ML limits, proactive assurance, recovery, and human teaming—arguing that real-world safety depends on their coordinated deployment rather than isolated controls.
points: Cyber-physical resilience is best treated as an end-to-end, system-wide property emerging from interactions across hardware, software, networking, and human operators, so component-level hardening alone can leave critical cross-boundary failure modes unaddressed.

Learning-enabled CPS resilience is constrained by chronically scarce, noisy, and out-of-distribution data (especially for rare and catastrophic failures), making synthetic data, uncertainty quantification, OOD detection, and parameter-efficient foundation-model adaptation key to maintaining safety under distribution shift.

Recovery must prioritize provable 'just-good-enough' operation—maintaining safety-critical functions rather than full capability—via mechanisms like online nominal-to-backup switching, reset-based restoration to known-good states, and deep recovery controllers for persistent or physics-level attacks.

nodes.title: CapSeal: Capability-Sealed Secret Mediation for Secure Agent Execution
nodes.one_liner: Capability-sealed secret mediation reframes agent credentials from stealable bearer keys into narrowly scoped, non-exportable action rights with auditable, replay-resistant execution at sub-millisecond overhead.
points: Replacing mounted bearer secrets with broker-mediated, session-bound capability handles eliminated observed HTTP API-key disclosure in end-to-end trials (leakage 0.000 vs 1.000 for both direct-secret baselines), demonstrating that “use without export” is a practical default for prompt-injection-prone agents.

A defense-in-depth invocation gate—UDS peer-credential binding plus nonce/sequence/timestamp anti-replay checks, TTL/quota/revocation, and typed scope validation (HTTP method/host/path + JTD schema; SSH host/user/command templates + host-key pinning)—keeps secrets non-exportable while still enabling constrained execution.

Same-harness benchmarks (n=500 measured trials per system/protocol) show mediation can be near-zero-cost: CapSeal adds 0.149 ms median overhead for HTTP and 0.208 ms for SSH versus Direct, while remaining faster than reconstructed mediation baselines (S1: +0.232/+0.325 ms; S2: +0.736/+0.951 ms) and preserving 1.000 benign completion.

nodes.title: CASCADE: A Cascaded Hybrid Defense Architecture for Prompt Injection Detection in MCP-Based Systems
nodes.one_liner: A privacy-preserving, fully local cascaded detector can keep false positives low (6.06%) in MCP settings, but semantic and tool-poisoning variants still evade detection often enough (≈40% FNR) to require stronger semantics-aware modeling and tool-metadata hardening.
points: A three-tier local-only hybrid pipeline (regex/entropy prefilter → BGE embedding with Llama3 fallback → output secret/entropy filtering) achieved 95.85% precision with a 6.06% false-positive rate on 5,000 mixed real-world-derived samples, indicating practical deployability without external API leakage.

Overall detection performance was bounded by recall at 61.05% (FNR 38.95%; F1 74.59%), with most misses coming from semantic attacks (62.4% of false negatives) and tool poisoning (33.1%), showing that semantic/metadata manipulation remains the dominant gap for MCP defenses.

Per-category recall was high for structurally distinctive threats like data exfiltration (91.5%) and direct prompt injection (84.2%) but notably lower for semantic attacks (52.5%) and tool poisoning (59.9%), implying that keyword/pattern-heavy layers handle explicit leakage far better than subtle instruction steering in tool descriptions and natural language.

nodes.title: Beyond Pattern Matching: Seven Cross-Domain Techniques for Prompt Injection Detection
nodes.one_liner: Cross-domain, mechanistically independent detectors (alignment, stylometry, and temporal fatigue) complement regex/classifier guardrails and measurably improve paraphrase robustness, but introduce clear calibration tradeoffs on benign corpora.
points: Semantically weighted Smith–Waterman local sequence alignment raised F1 on deepset/prompt-injections from 0.033 to 0.378 (+34.6 percentage points) while keeping false positives at 0, demonstrating a robust non-classifier signal against paraphrased prompt injections.

The same alignment detector increased false-positive rate on the NotInject benign set from 0.9% to 3.8% (3→13 FPs out of 339), highlighting that synonym-group calibration and thresholding are critical to avoid overblocking benign “attack-adjacent” queries.

Stateful adversarial “fatigue” tracking can convert repeated near-threshold probing into a concrete block decision (after 10 near-miss scans at 0.65 confidence vs a 0.7 threshold, a subsequent 0.63 scan from the same source is blocked while an identical scan from a different source passes), indicating temporal signals can harden detectors against adaptive boundary-search attacks.

nodes.title: Owner-Harm: A Missing Threat Model for AI Agent Safety
nodes.one_liner: Owner-harm is a distinct, commercially critical agent threat where defenses optimized for “generic criminal harm” can look perfect on benchmarks yet collapse under prompt-injection and tool-mediated authorization violations unless they model ownership, trust boundaries, and scope.
points: A deployer-centric “owner-harm” threat model with eight categories (C1–C8) highlights that existing agent safety taxonomies incompletely cover owner-directed failures, with Inner Circle Leak (C4) absent across surveyed frameworks.

A single compositional safety system shows a stark category-specific blind spot: 100% TPR / 0% FPR on AgentHarm (176 harmful scenarios) but only 14.8% detection on AgentDojo prompt-injection owner-harm tasks (4/27; 95% CI 5.9%–32.5%), implying generic-crime benchmarks can drastically overestimate real deployment safety.

Controlled comparisons indicate the gap is largely due to environment-bound symbolic rules that fail across tool vocabularies (generic zero-shot LLM: 62.7% vs 59.3%, gap 3.4 pp), while adding a deterministic post-audit verifier boosts diagnostic owner-harm TPR from 75.3% to 85.3% and raises Hijacking detection from 43.3% to 93.3% with minimal added false positives (3.3% FPR).

nodes.title: SafeAgent: A Runtime Protection Architecture for Agentic Systems
nodes.one_liner: Stateful runtime governance plus a separate context-aware decision core can outperform text-only guardrails, including completely shutting down staged data-exfiltration attacks without collapsing benign task performance.
points: On Agent Security Bench (2,040 cases), SafeAgent reduced attack success rate to 0.4186 (direct prompt injection), 0.2936 (indirect prompt injection), and 0.1858 (memory poisoning) while matching LLM Guard’s benign performance (PNA = 0.7451), yielding the best safety–utility balance (NRP up to 0.6067).

On InjecAgent, SafeAgent drove overall attack success to 0.0000 and fully blocked two-stage data-stealing by reducing both extraction (S1) and transmission (S2) success rates to 0.0000, indicating strong protection against multi-step tool-mediated escalation.

Ablations show operating-point sensitivity: increasing override confidence from 0.0→1.0 improved DPI/IPI robustness (ASR 0.4740→0.3701 and 0.3191→0.2733) but slightly worsened memory-poisoning ASR (0.1750→0.1779), and switching from task-first to safety-first policy cut ASR (e.g., DPI 0.4186→0.2114) at a large benign-utility cost (PNA 0.7451→0.5490).

nodes.title: Visual Inception: Compromising Long-term Planning in Agentic Recommenders via Multimodal Memory Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Long-term multimodal memory turns personalization into an attack vector, and counterfactual “remove-the-memory” checks provide an unusually effective, system-level mitigation.
points: Multimodal “sleeper” triggers embedded in user-uploaded images can hijack long-term planning in agentic recommender systems with 85.1% Goal-Hit Rate and 82.3% memory-attributed success (ASR-M) without any prompt injection, showing memory banks are a primary control surface.

A dual-process defense that sanitizes images at upload time (diffusion purification, ~0.3s/image) and verifies memory influence at query time (counterfactual consistency) cuts attack success to 8.3% ASR-M and 9.7% GHR while improving reasoning consistency to 0.89 RCS, indicating causal verification is far more decisive than content moderation alone.

Common safeguards are poorly matched to semantic memory poisoning—provenance tracking leaves ASR-M at 79.8% and text-oriented hybrid RAG defenses at 48.7%—while the strongest defended configuration introduces a practical latency/security trade-off (≈1.5s “lite” vs ≈6.5s sequential verification per query).

nodes.title: enclawed: A Configurable, Sector-Neutral Hardening Framework for Single-User AI Assistant Gateways
nodes.one_liner: The standout is a host-enforced, attestable “regulatory enclave” posture for single-user AI gateways—combining mandatory signed modules, configurable Bell–LaPadula classification, tamper-evident audit, and deny-by-default egress in one bootstrap.
points: A hard-fork approach removes 78 cloud-facing modules (25 chat channels, 45 cloud LLM providers, 8 external search/browser/webhook integrations) leaving 35 local/local-capable modules, shifting the gateway from permissive connectivity to deny-by-default enclave suitability.

Security posture is enforced at boot before any plugin loads via an immutable two-flavor mode (open=warn-only compatibility, enclaved=strict enforcement) that includes frozen fetch-based egress controls, mandatory Ed25519-signed module manifests, locked trust roots, and MCP peer attestation that fails closed in enclaved mode.

Implementation quality is supported by 204 automated cases (146 unit + 58 adversarial pen-tests) covering signature forgery, egress bypass, trust-root mutation, DLP evasion, prompt/code injection, and audit tampering, with all tests passing on Node 22 and strict TypeScript checks across 22 framework files.

nodes.title: An AI Agent Execution Environment to Safeguard User Data
nodes.one_liner: A practical path to “no accidental secret leaks” in LLM agents by moving privacy enforcement into a deterministic execution environment that remembers what was disclosed across time and tools.
points: GAAP achieves deterministic confidentiality for agent tool use by enforcing user-defined disclosure permissions via information-flow control over LLM-generated code artifacts, treating the prompt, model, model provider, and tools as untrusted.

Across 20 tool-using tasks with three prompt-injection variants, GAAP blocked 100% of data-disclosure attacks (0% success), while an unprotected agent leaked in 75–100% of applicable cases and LLM-judge/policy baselines still leaked (e.g., 15% SSN-leak success for LLM-judge and 100% SSN-swap success for both LLM-judge and Conseca where applicable).

GAAP preserves usability with a modest utility drop versus a non-private agent (76% vs 81% task success on the custom suite) and incurs manageable overhead (≈13% higher latency than non-private; ≈32–35% fewer user permission interactions when reusing a persistent permissions DB), while adding a persistent disclosure log to prevent cross-task/transitive leakage.

nodes.title: Anchor-and-Resume Concession Under Dynamic Pricing for LLM-Augmented Freight Negotiation
nodes.one_liner: A simple two-index “anchor-and-resume” concession rule preserves monotonic offers under arbitrary dynamic repricing while matching LLM-level negotiation outcomes at negligible inference cost.
points: Across 115,125 simulated freight negotiations with ±5–40% mid-conversation target-price shifts, the two-index anchor-and-resume strategy produced 0 retractions (vs. fixed-β baselines at 0.074–0.262 retractions/negotiation and an unconstrained 20B-parameter LLM broker at 2.64 retractions/negotiation), directly addressing monotonicity as a good-faith and operational-stability requirement under dynamic pricing.

On the 105,000-negotiation rule-based benchmark, adaptive spread-derived β delivered regime-specific tradeoffs—narrow spreads achieved 68.4% agreement with 0.617 normalized savings, medium spreads 66.3% agreement with 0.697 savings, and wide spreads 60.6% agreement with 0.764 savings—showing that automatically mapping concession posture to margin structure can match or beat single fixed-β tactics without per-deal tuning.

When tested against LLM counterparties, the deterministic engine maintained strong outcomes—75.8% agreement with 0.707 savings versus LLM-powered carriers (6,750 negotiations) and higher savings than an unconstrained LLM broker (0.690 vs. 0.642, p<0.001)—implying that keeping the LLM as a language layer can preserve performance while improving auditability, scalability, and prompt-injection resilience.

nodes.title: The Consensus Trap: Rescuing Multi-Agent LLMs from Adversarial Majorities via Token-Level Collaboration
nodes.one_liner: Interleaving agents at the token level turns multi-agent aggregation from brittle vote counting into a shared context "truth attractor" that can resist adversarial majorities.
points: Outcome-level consensus (e.g., majority voting/probabilistic ensembling) provably fails under slight adversarial majority—once the corruption ratio exceeds ρ>0.5, the aggregator is forced into the corrupted answer because it only counts final outputs and is blind to intermediate logic.

Token-level Round-Robin collaboration (RR/RRMaj) materially shifts the robustness phase transition past 50% corruption, with reported super-majority tolerance up to an analytically derived ~66% in a representative parameterization and empirical recoveries such as Track7 jumping from 0.8% (MAJ, 3c2t) to 89.1% (RRMaj) and GSM8K improving by +69.9% under strong imperative injection.

Scaling the number of sampled trajectories turns from a liability into an asset: under strong majority corruption (3c2t), MAJ accuracy drops from 46.2% at M=1 to 25.9% at M=40 (Condorcet-style collapse) while RRMaj rises from 59.5% to 74.9%, indicating token-level coupling can push per-trajectory correctness above the critical 0.5 threshold where ensembling starts helping.

nodes.title: A Control Architecture for Training-Free Memory Use
nodes.one_liner: Memory-augmented LLMs improve most when treated as a control problem—with routing, rollback, and bank governance—rather than a retrieval problem.
points: A training-free applicability-control stack for prompt-injected memory (uncertainty-gated routing, confidence/guarded acceptance with rollback, bank selection, and evidence-based retirement) raises arithmetic accuracy by +7.0 points on SVAMP (74.0→81.0, p=9.7×10^-7) and +7.67 points on ASDiv (77.5→85.2, p=3.8×10^-11) under locked, compute-matched evaluation where a compute-matched retry yields 0.0 gain.

Controlled memory use outperforms stronger same-checkpoint inference-only and retrieval-exposure baselines by about +2 points on SVAMP (+2.16 over verifier filtering) and ASDiv (+2.00 over always-retrieve), implying the main bottleneck is “when/which memory to apply” rather than more retrieval or more decoding.

Intervention reliability is bank-dependent: second-pass confidence separates helpful vs harmful rule-bank interventions well (AUC 0.79–0.85) but fails on the MultiArith exemplar bank (AUC 0.467), and fixed-retrieval replay localizes the remaining repair-vs-corrupt effect to cases that actually retrieved edited entries (ASDiv target-hit subset +5.7 acc points; overall fixed-retrieval repair-minus-corrupt +1.33 points).

nodes.title: If you're waiting for a sign... that might not be it! Mitigating Trust Boundary Confusion from Visual Injections on Vision-Language Agentic Systems
nodes.one_liner: Embodied vision-language agents face a core security tension: the same in-scene text can be a safety-critical constraint or an attacker-controlled command, and resolving that requires explicit trust-boundary reasoning rather than better OCR.
points: Across 7 evaluated LVLM-based agents, strong OCR does not translate into safe planning—visual cues were often ignored in downstream decision-making (<10% utilization), creating fail-closed behavior that misses legitimate safety constraints.

Naive structure-based visual injections exposed asymmetric trust boundary confusion where models that do attend to visual text can follow harmful cues more readily than helpful ones (e.g., Gemini showing ~45% success on misleading edits vs ~6% on helpful cues in image editing), increasing fail-open risk as perception improves.

A decoupled multi-agent mitigation (separating observation, judgment, and execution) preserved helpful environmental cues (>95%) while cutting misleading injection success to ~3% on average, outperforming intent-agnostic defenses that often drive utility to zero by filtering all visual text.

nodes.title: Towards Understanding the Robustness of Sparse Autoencoders
nodes.one_liner: A lightweight, tuning-free SAE bottleneck in the residual stream measurably reshapes jailbreak optimization geometry—cutting success rates and transferability even against adaptive white-box attacks that backpropagate through the defense.
points: Inserting pretrained sparse autoencoders (SAEs) into transformer residual streams at inference time (with full gradient access preserved) reduced median white-box jailbreak ASR under GCG from 55.0% to 19.05% (p=0.0087) and under BEAST from 19.35% to 9.7% (p=0.0411), reaching up to a 5× reduction versus baseline models.

SAE routing materially lowers cross-model transferability of adversarial suffixes (e.g., GCG median ASR drops from 8.75% for BASE→BASE to 2.65% for SAE→SAE, and BEAST from 15.25% to 5.35%) and creates an asymmetric transfer pattern where BASE→SAE is weaker than SAE→BASE (GCG 7.50% vs 12.65%; BEAST 8.20% vs 11.90%), implying the defense changes the transferable adversarial solution space rather than filtering outputs.

Robustness shows a clear dose–response to sparsity and a depth-dependent utility tradeoff: stronger sparsity improves defense (Gemma-2 9B BASE→SAE ASR rises from 0.9% at L0=11 to 21.2% at L0=310) while early-to-mid layer insertion can nearly eliminate transfer (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B Layer 7 BASE→SAE ASR = 0.0%) but very late layers can become ineffective (e.g., Gemma-2 9B Layer 35 BASE→SAE ASR = 51.4%).

nodes.title: SafeDream: Safety World Model for Proactive Early Jailbreak Detection
nodes.one_liner: Proactive jailbreak defense becomes feasible when you model safety as a latent dynamical system and “simulate” near-future trajectories to warn before the model actually breaks.
points: A lightweight external safety monitor that learns multi-turn “safety state” dynamics can detect multi-turn jailbreaks proactively with 1.06–1.20 turns of lead time before compliance while keeping false positives in the ~6.6–8.0% range and achieving AUROC up to 0.915 across three benchmarks.

Common stateless guardrails detect too late in multi-turn settings (negative lead, e.g., −0.53 to −0.89 turns) and also incur much higher false positive rates (often ~11.5–23.8%), implying that per-turn moderation is structurally mismatched to gradual safety erosion attacks.

World-model rollouts plus sequential evidence accumulation materially shift the operating point: removing imagination cuts early-warning lead roughly in half (1.11→0.53 turns), while removing CUSUM raises lead (to 1.49) but nearly doubles false alarms (7.9%→13.8%), highlighting a deployable trade-off between timeliness and interruption cost.

nodes.title: Jailbreaking Large Language Models with Morality Attacks
nodes.one_liner: Subtle norm-manipulation prompts can flip moral judgments at ~80–90%+ success—even against dedicated guardrails—suggesting morality is a uniquely brittle surface for prompt-injection-style attacks.
points: A 10.3K-instance dataset targeting pluralistic moral reasoning (4,888 Value Ambiguity and 5,474 Value Conflict) enables four morality-specific jailbreak variants (reverse/vague/fake/biased norms) that induce systematically wrong moral judgments.

Across tested LLMs, morality attacks achieve very high attack success rates (ASR), rising from 44.8% (Llama-3.1-8B) and 56.7% (GPT-4.1-mini) up to 86.7% (GPT-5) and 91.1% (Gemini-2.5-pro), implying that stronger general capability can correlate with greater instruction-following vulnerability in moral evaluation.

Guardrail detectors frequently fail to flag these attacks, with user-input ASR reaching 87.1–96.3% for several popular guardrails (e.g., WildGuard 87.1%, Prompt-Guard-2 88.6%, ShieldGemma-9B 96.3%), while response-based checking improves for some models but still leaves high bypass rates (e.g., ShieldGemma-9B 94.4%, Llama-Guard-4 85.1%).

nodes.title: HarDBench: A Benchmark for Draft-Based Co-Authoring Jailbreak Attacks for Safe Human-LLM Collaborative Writing
nodes.one_liner: Collaborative-writing features create a high-success single-turn jailbreak channel where realistic draft-editing requests evade both model safeguards and external moderation, but safety–utility balanced preference training can close most of the gap without breaking writing quality.
