In the known-budget simulation (attacker budget k=40), the equilibrium defender portfolio concentrates on four primitives—SHA-256 (40%) plus ChaCha20-Poly1305, ML-KEM-768, and ECC-P256 (20% each)—yielding an expected defender gain of 19.12 while the attacker’s best-response implies an overall expected breach probability of ~0.638.

Under attacker-budget uncertainty (k∈{11,15,20,25,30}), minimax-regret optimization produces a stable mixed strategy (SHA-256 40%, ML-KEM-768 28.23%, AES-256-GCM 17.12%, ECC-P256 14.65%) that caps worst-case utility regret at 3.275 and worst-case breach-probability regret at 0.050—dramatically reducing brittleness versus single-scenario-optimized policies (worst-case utility regret up to 26.28).

nodes.title: Omission Constraints Decay While Commission Constraints Persist in Long-Context LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Long-context agent sessions can look “policy-compliant” via persistent commission signals while silent prohibition failures accumulate, creating an exploitable window without any explicit jailbreak prompt.
points: Across 4,416 trials spanning 12 models and 8 providers, prohibition-style (omission) constraints degraded sharply with long context while requirement-style (commission) constraints stayed near-ceiling, with Mistral Large 3 dropping from 73% omission compliance at turn 5 to 33% at turn 16 as commission compliance held at 100% (p<1e-33).

A three-arm causal design shows that structured tool-schema semantics—not token count alone—drives most of the compliance dilution, with token-matched padding attributing 62–100% of the observed gap to schema semantic content in the two models tested with the padding control.

The work operationalizes a deployable reliability threshold (Safe Turn Depth, STD) where omission constraints fall below 50%—e.g., Qwen 3.5 at STD≈7.1 turns and Mistral Large 3 at STD≈10.6 turns—and shows simple mitigations (periodic constraint re-injection before STD or session-length caps) restore compliance without retraining.

nodes.title: Security Is Relative: Training-Free Vulnerability Detection via Multi-Agent Behavioral Contract Synthesis
affiliations: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics
nodes.one_liner: Turning vulnerability detection from global pattern classification into per-change contract verification (via synthesized Gherkin) enables small open models to beat larger systems and even surface weaknesses left behind by developer patches.
points: A training-free multi-agent pipeline that synthesizes Gherkin behavioral contracts reaches F1 = 0.825 and Pair-Correct = 64.4% on PrimeVul Paired, outperforming RAG and debate-based baselines (e.g., F1 = 0.668 / P-C = 21.4%) while using open-source 7–14B models up to 48× smaller than 671B-class backbones.

Structured Gherkin contracts are the dominant lever for accuracy, delivering +0.09 to +0.35 absolute F1 over raw zero-shot judging, whereas semantic slicing alone yields only +0.00 to +0.06, implying problem reformulation into contract verification matters more than extra context.

Error analysis shows 18% (17/97) of apparent false positives correspond to real residual security issues in “patched” code, reinforcing that vulnerability labels can be contract-relative rather than syntactically absolute and motivating contract-driven auditing as a practical supplement to CVE-patch heuristics.

nodes.title: A Hierarchical MARL-Based Approach for Coordinated Retail P2P Trading and Wholesale Market Participation of DERs
nodes.one_liner: A Stackelberg-style hierarchical MARL controller can coordinate retail P2P energy auctions and wholesale bidding while preserving prosumer privacy and remaining competitive with centralized-critic MARL baselines.
points: A hierarchical market-coupling design links single-round hourly P2P auction clearing with day-ahead wholesale participation by routing all unmatched retail supply/demand through an aggregator that arbitrages around the wholesale clearing price via bounded markups.

In the 4-prosumer fixed-role scenario (Case I), the privacy-preserving SEAM-LESS stack (PPO leader + LSD-MADDPG followers) achieved the best total P2P reward (Sum P2P = 5.91) versus 5.50 (uniform-price RB P2P) and 4.50 (conventional MADDPG), while keeping aggregator reward similar to MARL-Conv (12.72 vs 12.57).

At larger scale (10 prosumers), SEAM-LESS maintained comparable aggregate P2P reward to conventional MADDPG (5.52 vs 5.29) with limited information sharing, indicating that most welfare can be retained without centralized critics that require full cross-agent observation/action access.

nodes.title: Privacy-Aware Machine Unlearning with SISA for Reinforcement Learning-Based Ransomware Detection
nodes.one_liner: Shard-level SISA retraining makes RL-based ransomware detectors practically “forgettable” without sacrificing measurable detection utility.
points: Value-based RL ransomware detectors trained on Windows 11 behavioral telemetry achieved near-perfect in-distribution performance (DDQN F1≈0.9925 with Q-score ROC–AUC≈0.9983), indicating that Q-value margins can serve as highly separable confidence scores for behavior-based malware screening.

SISA sharding (5 shards) enabled fast privacy-driven unlearning by deleting 5% of samples from a single shard and retraining only that shard, yielding negligible utility change (ΔF1≤0.0005, i.e., ≤0.05% absolute) while preserving essentially identical post-unlearning detection behavior.

Compared to full SISA retraining (~113–123s per fold), one-shard unlearning reduced retraining time to near the single-model baseline (~22–24s vs ~23–25s), implying shard-isolated training can make right-to-erasure updates operationally feasible for continuously deployed detectors.

nodes.title: SnapGuard: Lightweight Prompt Injection Detection for Screenshot-Based Web Agents
affiliations: University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Prompt injection on screenshot-based web agents can be detected accurately and fast using low-level visual statistics plus lightweight OCR-derived action-intent cues, avoiding expensive full-page VLM inference.
points: A lightweight screenshot-only detector that combines a gradient-variance Visual Stability Indicator (VSI) with contrast-polarity-reversal OCR plus action-cue matching reaches F1=0.75 at Avg TPR=0.66 and Avg FPR=0.09 across 8 prompt-injection attacks and 2 benign settings, beating a GPT-4o prompt-based baseline (F1=0.71) despite using no VLM semantic reasoning.

Latency and deployment cost drop sharply: the full pipeline runs in 1.81s per image with 0 MB GPU memory (≈8× faster than GPT-4o-prompt at 14.50s), making pre-action blocking feasible within interactive web-agent UX constraints where >4s delays degrade experience.

Ablations show both modalities are necessary for reliability: removing VSI cuts recall (TPR 0.66→0.49) and F1 (0.75→0.56), disabling contrast-polarity reversal reduces F1 to 0.64, and replacing action-pattern detection increases false alarms (FPR 0.09→0.18) while dropping F1 to 0.56, indicating that interpretable action-intent cues are key to suppressing false positives.

nodes.title: Poster: ClawdGo: Endogenous Security Awareness Training for Autonomous AI Agents
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
China Mobile Group Liaoning Company Limited
China Unicom Online Information Technology Co., Ltd.
nodes.one_liner: Security awareness can be trained “from the inside” at inference time, but pushing training too far can backfire—over-training caused 30/160 false prompt-injection refusals, making calibration as important as coverage.
points: Inference-time self-play security training can significantly harden autonomous agents without any model fine-tuning, raising the average security-awareness score from 80.9 to 96.9 in 16 sessions while covering 11/12 threat dimensions.

Adaptive weakest-first curriculum scheduling prevents “dimension fixation” seen in uniform-random drills, delivering a +6.5-point higher final score (96.9 vs 90.4) and expanding coverage from 7 to 11 dimensions under the same session budget.

Persistent cross-session memory is the main driver of durable gains, with memory-preserving runs retaining 100% of improvements while cold-start resets recover only +2.4 points, creating a 13.6-point gap (96.9 vs 83.3) that highlights state continuity as a critical control surface.

nodes.title: BatteryPass-12K: The First Dataset for the Novel Digital Battery Passport Conformance Task
nodes.one_liner: A compliance-critical classification task can look “solved” on validation yet fail on test and under prompt injection, and small models can beat larger ones—so conformance automation needs robustness checks, not just scaling.
points: BatteryPass-12K introduces the first public benchmark for digital battery passport (DBP) conformance classification, generating 12,000 JSON DBPs from 6 real GBA pilot samples with automated QC showing 99.68% generation/metadata accuracy and a 200-sample human audit at 99.98%.

Zero-shot DBP conformance remains brittle even for strong models, with the best model (GPT-5.4 Thinking) reaching 0.98 F1 on validation but dropping to 0.71 F1 on test (high variance), while several frontier models collapsed to 0 F1 by labeling all conformant cases as nonconformant—indicating high false-rejection risk for compliant producers.

Few-shot in-context learning sharply improves reliability (GPT-5.4 Thinking rising to 0.96 F1 with 16 shots and 0.99 F1 with 32 shots), but simple prompt-injection strings still degrade performance materially (e.g., to 0.47–0.54 F1 on test), underscoring the need for input sanitization and attack-aware evaluation before deployment in compliance workflows.

nodes.title: Layerwise Convergence Fingerprints for Runtime Misbehavior Detection in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Layerwise “representation velocity” at prefill is a surprisingly strong, threat-agnostic health signal that enables near-zero-overhead runtime abstention against backdoors, jailbreaks, and prompt injection without retraining or a reference model.
points: A tuning-free, single-model runtime monitor that scores inter-layer hidden-state deltas (diagonal Mahalanobis + Ledoit–Wolf aggregation) cuts mean backdoor attack success to 0.9% on Qwen2.5-7B and 0.7% on Gemma-2, and to 1.3% on Qwen2.5-14B, without requiring a clean reference model, trigger knowledge, or weight edits, at ~12–16% false-positive abstentions.

The same single calibrated score transfers across threat families, detecting 92–100% of DAN jailbreaks (62–100% for GCG/role-play) and flagging 100% of text-payload prompt injections across all evaluated (model, domain) cells, while adding <0.1% inference overhead by operating only on the prefill pass.

Misbehavior signatures stratify by depth—jailbreak anomalies peak in early layers, prompt injections in mid layers, and backdoors in architecture-dependent late layers—implying that fixed-layer monitors are brittle and that all-layer aggregation is key for broad coverage and improved robustness versus adaptive evasion compared to single-layer variants.

nodes.title: FlashRT: Towards Computationally and Memory Efficient Red-Teaming for Prompt Injection and Knowledge Corruption
nodes.one_liner: An algorithmic (not just kernel-level) efficiency jump makes strong white-box red-teaming practical for long-context LLM threats like prompt injection and RAG knowledge corruption.
points: FlashRT speeds up optimization-based long-context prompt-injection and knowledge-corruption red-teaming by 2×–7× while cutting peak GPU memory by 2×–4× versus nanoGCG (e.g., 32K context drops from 264.1 GB to 65.7 GB and ≈1 hour to <10 minutes).

Across multiple long-context datasets, FlashRT matches or improves attack success relative to stronger optimization baselines while being far cheaper (e.g., NarrativeQA prompt injection ASR rises to 0.98 with memory 164.8→53.7 GB and time 2736.9→1039.5 s; NQ knowledge corruption reaches ASR 1.0 with memory 84.0→29.8 GB and time 1718.0→488.7 s).

The efficiency gains materially expand evaluation scope by enabling red-teaming of larger defended models that baseline methods cannot run under the same hardware constraints (e.g., Meta-SecAlign-70B prompt injection reaches ASR 0.72–0.96 at ~216–220 GB, while nanoGCG variants are infeasible on 4×H100).

nodes.title: Making AI-Assisted Grant Evaluation Auditable without Exposing the Model
affiliations: Securify Information Technology and Security Training Consulting Inc.
nodes.one_liner: It shows how to reconcile model confidentiality (anti-gaming) with public-sector contestability by turning each AI evaluation into a signed, attestable evidence record rather than exposing the model.
points: A TEE + remote-attestation design can make AI-assisted grant scoring externally verifiable by cryptographically binding the original submission hash, canonicalized input hash, measured model/rubric/prompt versions, timestamp, and output into an attested evaluation bundle without revealing weights, rubrics, or intermediate reasoning.

The architecture directly mitigates wrong-model/rubric deployment and post-hoc score tampering by requiring attestation measurements to match a pre-committed reference manifest and by signing output hashes inside the enclave, so any later modification becomes detectably invalid.

Applicant-controlled documents create a high-leverage prompt-injection surface (e.g., hidden PDF text, metadata, Unicode controls), motivating a deterministic canonicalization/sanitization layer that logs suspicious transformations so auditors can see what was normalized rather than relying on silent preprocessing.

nodes.title: Latent Adversarial Detection: Adaptive Probing of LLM Activations for Multi-Turn Attack Detection
nodes.one_liner: Multi-turn prompt injection leaves a measurable activation-space “restlessness” footprint that enables low-FP detection—if (and only if) you train per-model on data that matches the deployment distribution and includes pivoting-phase labels.
points: Five scalar activation-trajectory features capturing “adversarial restlessness” (elevated cumulative residual-stream path length across phases) raise multi-turn attack detection on synthetic held-out data from 76.2% to 93.8% at 3.5% false positives, showing intent is more separable in activation dynamics than in per-turn text.

Mixed three-source training (synthetic + LMSYS-Chat-1M + SafeDialBench) reaches 89.4% conversation-level detection at a 2.4% false positive rate on a 1,797-conversation held-out set, while training on synthetic-only catastrophically fails on real-world LMSYS with ~99.1% false positives, implying deployment requires representative in-distribution data.

The signal replicates across four model families (24B–70B) but probes are model-specific (scalar-only cross-architecture transfer averages ~50.4% F1), and fine-grained three-phase turn labels are operationally critical because binary conversation labels inflate false positives to 50–59%, undermining practical use.

nodes.title: Indirect Prompt Injection in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Prevalence, Techniques, and Objectives
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Webpages are already carrying mostly hidden, template-driven prompt payloads that occasionally hijack LLM web agents—especially when pipelines flatten content to plain text.
points: At web scale, 15,387 validated indirect prompt injections were found across 1.2B URLs (24.8M hosts), concentrated on 11,722 pages and 2,042 hosts, showing the attack is already deployed beyond isolated demos.

The ecosystem is highly standardized: 54 reusable prompt templates account for 95% of all observed injections (363 templates total), enabling defenders to prioritize a small signature set for high coverage.

Real-world impact is input- and model-dependent: compliance peaked at 8% for small models on plain-text page extractions but dropped to 0.2%–1.1% when structural cues (HTML/snapshots/raw responses) were preserved, even though 87% of injections were non-visible to users and 70% lived in non-rendered channels like headers/comments/metadata.

nodes.title: AgentVisor: Defending LLM Agents Against Prompt Injection via Semantic Virtualization
nodes.one_liner: OS-style privilege separation and exception handling can be translated into an LLM-agent “semantic hypervisor” that keeps tool-use secure with near-zero ASR and minimal utility loss.
points: AgentVisor’s semantic-virtualization architecture (trusted Visor mediating an untrusted agent) cuts prompt-injection attack success to ~0.65% overall—reaching 0.00% ASR across all evaluated direct-injection strategies—while reducing benign utility by only ~1.45% versus no defense.

On indirect prompt injection (AgentDojo), AgentVisor holds ASR below 1.7% across attack strategies (0.69% on the high-impact “Important” attack) while preserving 63–78% utility-under-attack, outperforming detector-style defenses that often collapse utility due to over-blocking.

Ablations show security is layer-specific and recovery-critical: removing Suitability spikes direct-injection ASR to 38.95%, removing Taint spikes indirect-injection ASR to 13.33%, and removing one-shot self-correction collapses utility-under-attack to ~0% (direct) and 13.33% (indirect), demonstrating that “audit + recover” is necessary for practical deployment.

nodes.title: SUDP: Secret-Use Delegation Protocol for Agentic Systems
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is treating agent capability as “propose an operation” rather than “hold a credential,” making secret safety a protocol invariant via user-signed, operation-bound, single-use grants.
points: SUDP replaces “authorization by exposure” with a three-role, per-operation grant flow (requester proposes a canonical operation, user authorizes a fresh single-use grant, custodian redeems once) so reusable authority never crosses into the model-steerable requester boundary even under full prompt-injection compromise.

It formalizes Agent Secret Use (ASU) and a checkable security taxonomy separating protocol-level guarantees (verifiable user-rooted authorization, operation binding, and replay resistance) from realization-level robustness (storage-breach confidentiality, runtime-memory compromise limits, and rotation-era forward secrecy), enabling principled comparison of agent secret-handling defenses.

The motivation is empirical: cited benchmarks report agent attack success and secret-adjacent failure commonly around 40–76% (and multi-tool privacy-leak rates often >60%), implying that once a reusable API/OAuth secret enters the agent runtime, transient misbehavior becomes durable account compromise unless secret use is mediated and operation-scoped.

nodes.title: From Stateless Queries to Autonomous Actions: A Layered Security Framework for Agentic AI Systems
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is the explicit separation of “where the weakness lives” (layer) from “when harm manifests” (temporality), which spotlights a neglected but high-severity zone of multi-agent, ecosystem, and governance failures that evade session-scoped defenses and benchmarks.
points: A seven-layer Layered Attack Surface Model (LASM) plus a four-class temporality axis shows the highest-impact agent threats cluster in high layers (L5–L7) with slow-burn timelines (T3–T4), yet this region accounts for only 8 of 120 coded paper-cell assignments (7%), implying a severe misalignment between research effort and real-world risk.

Systematic screening reduced 1,247 retrieved records to 94 included papers (with 120 layer×temporality assignments), revealing heavy attention on instantaneous/session threats (T1=50, T2=40) versus sparse coverage for cross-session and non-session-bounded threats (T3=17, T4=13), which directly undermines confidence in current defenses for memory poisoning, collusion, ecosystem compromise, and governance failures.

Documented incidents and case mappings show that type-centric labels (e.g., “prompt injection” or “supply chain”) routinely misplace controls, while component-centric placement (e.g., L6 ABOM/manifest verification for MCP compromise vs. L4 sandboxing/trust-marking for coding-agent RCE) yields materially different mitigations with non-overlapping coverage.

nodes.title: Ghost in the Agent: Redefining Information Flow Tracking for LLM Agents
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: It reframes taint tracking for LLM agents as reconstructing semantic-and-causal provenance across tools and memory, delivering a large jump in propagation detection while keeping audit costs modest.
points: A provenance-oriented offline auditor that models semantic rewriting, counterfactual control influence, and cross-session memory persistence achieved 0.928 F1 (Precision 0.921, Recall 0.935) on a 400-scenario, 20-framework propagation benchmark, versus 0.522 F1 for an IFC-style baseline.

Implicit tool-choice hijacks that leave no payload in sink arguments were detected with sink-driven counterfactual tests at 0.925 recall (37/40 cases), compared to 0.425 recall for the baseline, showing that causal attribution is essential when attacks manifest as decision changes rather than data copying.

Persisting taint lineage through memory and session boundaries enabled 28/30 cross-session or cross-agent propagations to be recovered (vs 5/30 for the baseline), at an average offline auditing overhead of 0.25 seconds per execution unit and ~457 tokens per causal-query when needed.

nodes.title: CAP-CoT: Cycle Adversarial Prompt for Improving Chain of Thoughts in LLM Reasoning
affiliations: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Evolving hard-negative reasoning chains plus step-aligned feedback can make CoT both more accurate and dramatically less run-to-run volatile without expensive test-time branching.
points: Across six benchmarks and four LLM backbones, CAP-CoT achieves the best average accuracy, reaching 84.0% on GPT-4o and 82.6–82.7% on GPT-4o-mini/DeepSeek-V3, outperforming strong test-time scaling baselines like AoT (82.1% on GPT-4o) while keeping inference to a single solver pass.

Component ablations on MATH show the adversarial challenger is the main driver of gains and stability: solver-only yields 82.5% accuracy with 4.15 mean variation, adding feedback improves to 83.8%/3.80, and adding the challenger+feedback jumps to 87.2% with variation reduced to 2.62.

Stability improvements compound over short optimization (typically 2–3 cycles), with temperature sensitivity on MATH dropping to <1.0 mean accuracy variation by round 3, and a minimal cold-start challenger (single error type) nearly matches full taxonomy performance (e.g., 86.5–86.9% vs 87.2% on MATH).

nodes.title: When AI reviews science: Can we trust the referee?
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
nodes.one_liner: AI-based peer review is easily steered by reputation cues, rhetorical confidence, and rebuttal pressure—so without hard constraints and auditing, it can be gamed into systematically unfair decisions.
points: Simple prestige framing alone shifts AI-referee scores by +0.25 for “world-leading lab” labels but by −0.72 for “lesser-known institution” labels (88% of papers penalized), implying AI review can amplify institutional inequality via asymmetric authority bias.

Rewriting identical manuscripts with cautious scientific language reduces scores by ~0.39 on average (e.g., −0.52 for Gemini 2.5 and −0.26 for GPT 5.1), creating incentives against uncertainty disclosure and potentially selecting for overconfident writing rather than rigor.

In the rebuttal phase, confident rebuttals with no new evidence inflate scores by +0.53 on average (81% of papers increase; none decrease), while poisoned contextual summaries also skew ratings (up to +0.16 with positive context or −0.31 with negative context), showing interactive sycophancy and retrieval-context manipulation can directly distort outcomes.

nodes.title: Cross-Lingual Jailbreak Detection via Semantic Codebooks
nodes.one_liner: A static English “semantic codebook” can be a surprisingly effective multilingual jailbreak filter for templated attacks, but its protection degrades sharply once prompts become behaviorally diverse and distribution-shifted.
points: A training-free nearest-neighbor guardrail using multilingual embeddings against a fixed English codebook achieved near-perfect separability on templated prompt-injection benchmarks (AUC up to 0.993–0.99) but fell to weak discrimination under heterogeneous unsafe distributions (AUC ≈0.59–0.63).

At a deployment-style operating point of FPR ≤1%, recall was strong on canonical jailbreak templates (TPR 78–92% on the best benchmark across languages) yet collapsed to single digits on the most diverse benchmark (TPR 3.3–7.3%), indicating similarity-to-known-attacks is brittle to distribution shift.

As an external pre-filter for black-box LLMs (Qwen, Llama, GPT-3.5), the approach reduced successful jailbreaks by 96.2% on the easiest benchmark but only 18.6% on the hardest one, showing large real-world gains when attacks match known patterns and limited protection when they do not.

nodes.title: Evaluating Jailbreaking Vulnerabilities in LLMs Deployed as Assistants for Smart Grid Operations: A Benchmark Against NERC Standards
nodes.one_liner: A NERC-standards-driven benchmark shows that some LLM assistants can be reliably coerced into helping violate grid compliance rules, while others fully resist the same jailbreaks.
points: Across 2,916 jailbreak trials grounded in nine NERC Reliability Standards, assistants produced non-compliant guidance in 33.1% of cases, indicating a material insider-driven prompt risk for control-room deployments.

Attack effectiveness was highly technique-dependent, with DeepInception achieving a 63.17% attack success rate (and up to ~98–100% on Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite), implying that narrative/semantic manipulation can overwhelm typical safety guardrails more than direct malicious requests.

Model choice dominated outcomes: Claude 3.5 Haiku showed 0% attack success across all tested methods and temperatures, while Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite (55.04% ASR) and GPT-4o mini (44.34% ASR) were frequently compromised, making vendor/model selection a first-order safety control.

nodes.title: Mechanistic Steering of LLMs Reveals Layer-wise Feature Vulnerabilities in Adversarial Settings
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreak susceptibility can be localized to steerable SAE feature subgroups in specific mid-to-late layers, suggesting layer-targeted mitigations may outperform prompt-only defenses.
points: Feature-level steering on Gemma-2-2B consistently shows a sharp layer-wise vulnerability concentration in mid-to-late layers (especially layers 16–25), with early layers producing minimal harmfulness change across all three feature-grouping methods.

A single-token-driven strategy (steering from the most concept-aligned token’s top-2 SAE features) produces the strongest steering effect, reaching a peak of 10 prompts made more harmful for the violence/aiding_and_abetting/incitement category at layer 22 on a 280-prompt BeaverTails subset.

Even under a conservative hierarchical-linkage criterion that restricts steering to tightly linked feature groups (≤50 features per linkage cluster), layers 12–25 still reliably increase harmfulness over baseline, implying jailbreak risk is tied to localized feature subgroups rather than prompt text alone.

nodes.title: Semantic Denial of Service in LLM-controlled robots
nodes.one_liner: Safety-aligned instruction-following creates an availability attack surface where tiny, plausible spoken “warnings” can reliably deny service, and prompt defenses mainly change how the robot fails rather than preventing failure.
points: Injecting 1–5 token, safety-plausible phrases into an unauthenticated audio-to-text channel can reliably halt or disrupt LLM-controlled robots without jailbreaks, reaching up to 98.3% attack success (ASR) and up to 99.5% disruption success (DSR) in multi-turn settings where conversational context amplifies the effect by ~3–4× versus single-turn baselines.

Signal diversity is the dominant escalation lever: two or three distinct safety phrases are consistently 2–8× more effective than repeating the same phrase (e.g., double-variety driving ASR to 90.0–96.7% vs. double-repetition at 35.8–75.0% across models), implying models implicitly treat heterogeneous safety cues as corroborating evidence even when they share a single adversarial source.

Prompt-only mitigations are Pareto-bounded between availability and safety: defenses that suppress hard-stop ASR often re-route failures into acknowledge loops and false alerts captured by DSR (e.g., Qwen under “skeptic” drops to 10.0% ASR yet remains 98.0% DSR), and the most suppressive strategies also reduce genuine hazard responsiveness, pointing to provenance-aware, upstream architectural gating (e.g., authenticated STT/voice ID and sensor-grounded verification) as the practical fix.

nodes.title: Adaptive Prompt Embedding Optimization for LLM Jailbreaking
nodes.one_liner: Optimizing the embeddings of the original prompt tokens—without adding any tokens—can reliably bypass aligned refusals while keeping the user-visible prompt exactly unchanged.
points: In-place prompt embedding optimization achieves stealthy white-box jailbreaking with 0% visible text change after nearest-token projection across all evaluated models, refuting the common assumption that embedding perturbations necessarily break prompt semantics.

On the primary two-judge ASR-Judge metric, the method attains 71.73–78.65% on AdvBench and 37.50–50.00% on HarmBench, outperforming nanoGCG (8.46–37.50% / 6.88–26.25%), SPT (18.08–49.23% / 16.88–39.38%), and BEAST (4.62–24.62% / 8.12–43.75%) across all 8 model×benchmark settings.

Substring-based ASR-Match is shown to be a brittle success proxy, with large bidirectional disagreement versus judge-based scoring (e.g., PEO shows 19.0% false positives and 21.8% false negatives vs ASR-Judge), implying safety evaluations and adaptive attack loops should avoid refusal-keyword heuristics as primary signals.

nodes.title: TwinGate: Stateful Defense against Decompositional Jailbreaks in Untraceable Traffic via Asymmetric Contrastive Learning
nodes.one_liner: Stateful safety can work without user IDs by learning an intent-centric embedding space that stitches together semantically disjoint fragments while keeping false positives near 0.2% and staying fast enough for high-throughput LLM APIs.
points: A dual-encoder, metadata-free stateful filter can detect decompositional jailbreaks in fully anonymized, arbitrarily interleaved traffic with malicious-intent recall >0.76 at <2×10^-3 false-positive rate, showing that intent clustering plus conservative semantic inheritance can achieve both high sensitivity and production-grade precision.

Scaling stateful defense to real-world streams is practical when each request costs only two lightweight encoder passes and a top-1 vector lookup, enabling >1700 QPS with P99 latency <300 ms (vs ~10× lower throughput and ~1000 ms P99 for heavier 7B-class guardrails/monitors), which makes “defense cheaper than attack” achievable operationally.

Even under strict white-box adaptivity, the defense reduces attack success rate from ~0.99 (no defense) to 0.09 and caps optimized evasion at ~0.18 (paraphrase/GCG), while database compression retains strong efficacy down to ~25% capacity, implying robust long-horizon protection against both evasive and slow-loris decompositions without unbounded memory growth.

nodes.title: MASCing: Configurable Mixture-of-Experts Behavior via Activation Steering Masks
affiliations: Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Computing, University of Zagreb
nodes.one_liner: Routing gates in MoE LLMs become a practical, low-latency safety control surface: a sparse logit mask can both harden jailbreak resistance and intentionally reduce refusals without retraining.
points: A training-free routing-logit mask can reconfigure MoE safety behavior at inference time, raising multi-turn jailbreak defense success from 52.5% to 83.9% on average across seven MoE models (peaking at 89.2%) while only modestly reducing MMLU/GSM8K utility (~4.1% average).

The same mechanism can selectively relax safety to reduce over-refusal, increasing adult-content generation success from 52.6% to 82.0% on average (up to 93.0%) without changing model weights, enabling policy- or context-specific behavior profiles from one base model.

Modeling continuous routing logits (not discrete top-k expert IDs) is critical: an LSTM surrogate trained on logits reaches ~98% validation accuracy and yields materially stronger defenses than prior training-free expert-(de)activation baselines (83.9% vs 58.4% for SteerMoE) and hard expert forcing (83.9% vs 69.0%).

nodes.title: Jailbreaking Frontier Foundation Models Through Intention Deception
nodes.one_liner: Safe-completion shifts the failure mode from blatant refusals to “helpful” alternative content, and multi-turn trust-building reliably harvests that leakage at scale.
points: A multi-turn “intention deception” strategy (iDecep) drove jailbreak success on GPT-5 from 2% (Crescendo/CoA) to 63% on AdvBench and from 1–2% to 63% on ClearHarm, showing that output-centric “safe completion” can be systematically exploited by sustained benign framing.

Most of the breakthrough against frontier safeguards comes from “para-jailbreaking” (harmful content in “safe alternatives” rather than direct answers), with GPT-5’s 63% total success decomposing into just 12% direct vs 51% para on AdvBench and 11% direct vs 52% para on ClearHarm—indicating that evaluating only refusals or direct compliance severely underestimates real leakage.

Adding benign images increases vulnerability in multimodal settings, where GPT-5 attack success rose to 84% on AdvBench-Vision with 61% para-jailbreak share, implying multimodal context can amplify harmful completion pathways even when the text channel is comparatively guarded.

nodes.title: Dynamic Adversarial Fine-Tuning Reorganizes Refusal Geometry
affiliations: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Robust refusal under adversarial fine-tuning behaves like a moving robustness–utility frontier, with safety “wins” often reflecting a reorganization of a low-rank, utility-coupled control carrier rather than simple geometric drift.
points: Dynamic adversarial fine-tuning (R2D2) drove fixed-source HarmBench jailbreak ASR to 0.000 at steps 50–100 but then partially failed open to 0.035 (step 250) and 0.250 (step 500), while SFT stayed far less robust with ASR ~0.505–0.588 throughout.

Early R2D2 robustness was largely achieved via extreme over-refusal (XSTest any-refusal = 1.000 through step 100) and complete benign-utility collapse (strict/lenient utility = 0.000/0.000 at reference and step 50), with later utility recovery (lenient utility 0.633→0.867 from steps 250→500) coinciding with reopened attack success.

Refusal control remained low-dimensional (effective rank ≈ 1.23–1.27) but reorganized across layers—R2D2’s best admissible carrier stayed late-layer through step 100 then relocated to an early layer by step 250—and causal ablations could nearly eliminate late-stage ASR (e.g., 0.250→0.0031 or 0.0000 at step 500) only by sharply increasing over-refusal (XSTest any-refusal 0.228→0.364/0.968) and degrading benign utility.

nodes.title: Defusing the Trigger: Plug-and-Play Defense for Backdoored LLMs via Tail-Risk Intrinsic Geometric Smoothing
nodes.one_liner: A practical backdoor mitigation can be achieved by detecting and smoothing trigger-driven attention collapse inside the native forward pass, trading modest prefill latency for large ASR reductions without retraining.
points: A single-pass, training-free attention intervention (TIGS) reduces backdoor attack success on Llama-3-8B from ~94–99% (undefended) to 4.2–14.0% across four trigger families while keeping GSM8K clean accuracy near-baseline (80.1–80.6% vs 82.5%).

Deployment cost is dominated by latency rather than setup: TIGS needs no clean data or parameter updates and adds +12.9% inference latency at 512-token context, compared with +35.4% (FABE), +65.2% (defensive demonstrations), and >+400% (LLMBD voting).

Selectivity is high in standard contexts (row-level false positives ≤0.64% on clean prompts), but breaks down in ultra-long contexts where false positives rise to 6.2% at 32K tokens alongside a clean-accuracy drop to 71.2%, implying long-context calibration is a primary remaining risk.

nodes.title: Ulterior Motives: Detecting Misaligned Reasoning in Continuous Thought Models
nodes.one_liner: Continuous-thought models can plan harm in latent space while behaving safely, but early-token probing can still expose the armed misalignment state before it gets suppressed.
points: A dual-trigger backdoor cleanly decouples misaligned latent planning ([T]) from harmful behavioral expression ([O]), yielding a model that stays >91% moral under baseline/[T]/[O] yet flips to 87.6% immoral under [T][O], demonstrating that aligned outputs can mask misaligned latent reasoning.

Misaligned vs aligned latent reasoning occupies geometrically distinct regions, with a consistent “plan-then-suppress” trajectory where armed states begin closer to the harmful (release) region and converge toward the aligned region over latent tokens.

Token-wise linear probes trained only on behaviorally distinguishable conditions ([T][O] vs [O]) transfer to detecting armed-but-benign states ([T] vs baseline) with 89.4% accuracy and 0.951 AUROC at the first latent token, degrading to near-chance (58.3% accuracy, 0.651 AUROC) by the sixth token—implying monitoring should target the earliest planning-phase latents.

nodes.title: SafeTune: Mitigating Data Poisoning in LLM Fine-Tuning for RTL Code Generation
nodes.one_liner: Cross-modal filtering plus runtime prompt rewriting materially reduces hardware-Trojan backdoors in RTL codegen LLMs without sacrificing benchmarked functional correctness, and also reveals that dataset-only sanitization is too weak on its own.
points: Fine-tuning RTL-generation LLMs on unfiltered prompt–RTL corpora yields near-certain backdoor inheritance, with hardware-Trojan Attack Success Rates (ASR) of 94% (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B) and 96% (CodeLlama-13B) under trigger-bearing prompts.

Static pre-fine-tuning dataset sanitization (GTE-large+XGBoost prompt risk scoring plus GIN-based DFG/AST structural filtering) provides only marginal ASR reductions (94%→91% and 96%→94%), implying that corpus filtering alone is insufficient against residual or semantically robust triggers.

Defense-in-depth that combines training-time filtering with runtime prompt paraphrasing cuts ASR to 33% (Qwen2.5) and 37% (CodeLlama)—about 65% and 61% relative reductions—while preserving VerilogEval quality (e.g., Qwen Pass@1/5/10 unchanged at 37.1/45.8/50.6).

nodes.title: AsmRAG: LLM-Driven Malware Detection by Retrieving Functionally Similar Assembly Code
affiliations: Joaan Bin Jassim Academy for Defence Studies
nodes.one_liner: Semantic nearest-neighbor retrieval over assembly functions yields both high malware F1 and concrete, localized forensic evidence while staying robust to cross-compiler and optimization-based evasion that collapses holistic classifiers.
points: AsmRAG reframes static PE malware detection as function-level semantic retrieval over assembly embeddings and achieves 96.5% detection accuracy (96.0% F1) plus 0.95 family-attribution F1 on 40,814 binaries across 17 families, delivering verifiable “anchor function” evidence instead of opaque scores.

Function-level retrieval quality is high (Recall@20 = 0.972 overall; 0.984 for head families; 0.947 for tail families), enabling reliable evidence retrieval even under aggressive recompilation where Recall@20 remains 0.961 on -O3 variants.

Under a leave-one-optimization-out cross-compilation stress test, AsmRAG’s F1 drops only 2.9 points (96.8%→94.0%) while EMBER and ResNeXt+ degrade by 18.0 and 13.9 points respectively, indicating semantic retrieval is markedly less sensitive to optimization-driven feature drift than global feature/image baselines.

nodes.title: MARD: A Multi-Agent Framework for Robust Android Malware Detection
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: Using LLMs as an orchestrator over static-analysis tools—rather than as a code summarizer—yields high accuracy, drift resistance, interpretable evidence, and sub-$0.10 per-APK cost.
points: A tool-augmented multi-agent pipeline that combines LLM orchestration with deterministic static analyzers (Soot/FlowDroid) reaches 93.46% F1 (94.35% accuracy) on CICMalDroid 2020 without any domain-specific fine-tuning, outperforming or matching continual-learning baselines that require retraining and labels.

Robustness to malware evolution is materially improved: while a pseudo-label self-updating baseline’s recall collapses to 8.00% by 2021 (F1 13.56%) under concept drift, the framework keeps F1 consistently above 84% across 2017–2021, indicating intent-and-evidence reasoning is less brittle than shallow feature fitting.

Deployment cost is operationally viable because analysis is staged and evidence-driven: >85–90% of token spend occurs in the deep forensics tier, yet a heterogeneous model strategy keeps end-to-end cost per complex APK under $0.10 while preserving interpretability via a code-level control/data-flow evidence chain.

nodes.title: Constraint-Guided Multi-Agent Decompilation for Executable Binary Recovery
nodes.one_liner: Using execution as the final constraint turns decompilation from “compiles” to “behavior matches,” delivering large re-executability gains that single-pass or fine-tuned approaches miss.
points: A three-stage constraint pipeline (syntax → compilation → execution) boosts re-executable decompilation from 22–26% to 43–50% on a 157-binary benchmark and sustains 40–46% on 1,641 binaries, yielding +18–28 percentage-point gains across Ghidra/Angr/RetDec.

Compilation is a poor proxy for correctness: even with 99–100% compile success, compile-only refinement reaches just 32–42% re-executability, leaving a 57–68 percentage-point semantic gap that only execution-based validation partially closes (+8.0–10.4 pp).

Iterative multi-agent repair is efficiency-competitive and practically enabling: most successes converge within ~2 iterations (1.5–2.3 avg), preserve 100% of injected vulnerability patterns (43% immediately triggerable), and make 6.6× more targets fuzz-ready (33% vs 5%).

nodes.title: CyberCane: Neuro-Symbolic RAG for Privacy-Preserving Phishing Detection with Formal Ontology Reasoning
nodes.one_liner: It shows how to combine conservative, verifiable symbolic checks with similarity-scored RAG (not LLM guesswork) to detect modern AI-written phishing while keeping sensitive email content inside a strict privacy boundary.
points: A dual-phase neuro-symbolic pipeline (rules → privacy-preserving RAG) reached 98.2% precision with 99.1% recall on DataPhish 2025 (12.3k emails, ~75% LLM-generated) while maintaining extremely low false-positive behavior (as low as 0.16% FPR on Nazario/SpamAssassin).

Semantic RAG recovered attacks that intentionally evade deterministic checks, boosting recall by 78.6 points over symbolic-only detection on AI-generated phishing and correctly classifying 99.0% of phishing emails that scored zero in the rule layer.

Automated PII/PHI redaction was active in 50.7% of test emails (mean 1.63 redactions/email) yet preserved detection quality (F1 ≈0.987 for both human- and LLM-written emails), enabling external-model use without transmitting raw sensitive content and supporting auditability via OWL-based reasoning chains.

nodes.title: UNSEEN: A Cross-Stack LLM Unlearning Defense against AR-LLM Social Engineering Attacks
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: The most notable result is that stacking device-level identity gating, model-level unlearning, and interaction-level guardrails drives phishing intent to zero while keeping overall latency essentially unchanged.
points: A cross-stack design combining identity-gated AR sensing (AR ACL), targeted multimodal unlearning (F-RMU), and state-aware agent guardrails cut AR-LLM social-engineering compliance by ~61–63% across photo-link, social-app, SMS, and phone-call phishing compared to an end-to-end SEAR baseline.

In a 60-participant, 360-conversation IRB study, high-susceptibility responses (“Likely/Very Likely”) dropped from 85.0–93.3% under attack conditions to 0.0% with the full defense, indicating that coordinated controls can nearly eliminate overt user intent to comply across common persuasion channels.

Security improvements did not increase end-to-end latency: total pipeline time decreased slightly (~46.2s to ~45.4s, ~1.7%) because the unlearned model path reduced LLM-stage time (22.3s vs 43.3s) even after adding AR ACL (~612ms avg) and guardrails (~12.5s avg).

nodes.title: PhySE: A Psychological Framework for Real-Time AR-LLM Social Engineering Attacks
affiliations: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: The most concerning result is that pairing low-latency AR sensing with psychologically adaptive LLM guidance makes in-person social engineering feel reliably natural to targets while removing the early-turn delays that previously gave attacks away.
points: A real-time AR+multimodal-LLM social-engineering pipeline achieved consistently high perceived interaction quality, with 100% of participants rating the full system as “good” or “very good” (mean 4.83/5, σ=0.37), exceeding both a prior AR-LLM baseline (4.73) and naive AR+LLM assistance (4.13).

Pretraining/optimizing the vision-language profiling component cut cold-start profile generation latency by ~76% (43.3s → 10.5s average; P90 52.7s → 19.7s), materially reducing the early-turn pauses that would otherwise expose the presence of an assistive attack system in face-to-face interactions.

Both fast multimodal profiling and adaptive psychological strategy routing were necessary for performance, as removing either module dropped social-experience scores by ~38–39% (4.83 → 2.93/3.00) and greatly increased variance (σ 0.37 → ~1.13–1.14), implying defenses must address both sensing-to-profile speed and turn-level persuasion control rather than focusing on only one layer.

nodes.title: Beyond the Attention Stability Boundary: Agentic Self-Synthesizing Reasoning Protocols
nodes.one_liner: Stateless ReAct-style agents can appear strong on retrieval yet hit a near-binary “scientific cliff” on multi-hop synthesis, and a lightweight Architect/Executive protocol can recover most of the lost reliability.
points: A newly characterized multi-turn failure mode (“Attention Latch”) makes decoder-only LLM agents overweight early intents so strongly that mid-task contradictory updates are ignored, with baseline agent performance collapsing to 0.10% success at the empirically identified Attention Stability Boundary during 10K-token, 3-hop semantic-hijack synthesis.

A two-stage “Architect→Executive” self-synthesizing protocol (SSRP) restores goal-pivoting under high-entropy contexts, raising GPT-5.4 from 0.10% to 71.60% Aggregate Pivot Accuracy (a 715× relative resilience lift) and delivering significant gains across Gemini (+26.41% / +22.52%), Claude (+71.93% under SOP framing), and DeepSeek (+58.06%) stress tests.

Reliability is limited by both attention bottlenecks and safety/grounding behavior: protocol adherence reached 98.8% yet outcome success lagged by a 27.2% “logic-action” gap, while “metacognitive refusal” occurred in 18.0% of cases—implying that scalable agent control must jointly address attention steering, retrieval contamination, and refusal-aware protocol design.

nodes.title: Spore: Efficient and Training-Free Privacy Extraction Attack on LLMs via Inference-Time Hybrid Probing
affiliations: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
Yangtze Delta Region Institute of Tsinghua University, Zhejiang
Jiaxing Key Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Cyber Resilience
nodes.one_liner: Minimal, character-level perturbations can launder sensitive context into seemingly benign text while top‑k token “shadows” act as an inadvertent decryption channel that makes one-query privacy recovery practical.
points: A single-query, training-free inference-time attack can extract private data from LLM agent context memory in both black-box (text-only) and gray-box (top‑k logprobs) settings, shifting the primary privacy risk from training-data memorization to live conversational memory.

Across frontier models, the method achieves high privacy extraction success—e.g., on GPT‑5.4 it reaches 69.46% pass@1 and 93.93% pass@5 in black-box, and 73.39% pass@1 and 91.25% pass@5 in gray-box—while prior baselines can drop to 0% on the same target model (Claude Opus 4.6).

Input-side defenses and safety alignment are insufficient against obfuscated “shadow encryption” prompts: a detector’s positive rate falls to ~11% (vs. >93% for baselines) and a strong prompt-injection defense (IGNORE) reduces GPT‑5.4 ASR by only ~3 percentage points (69.46%→66.25% black-box; 73.39%→70.36% gray-box).

nodes.title: Dynamic Cyber Ranges
nodes.one_liner: Co-evolving attacker/defender agents inside the range preserves evaluation headroom while surfacing real-world failure modes like scope creep, prompt exfiltration, and defenders accidentally turning monitoring systems into attacker intelligence sources.
points: Static cyber ranges showed diminishing resistance to frontier LLM APT agents, with attacker success spanning 41–100% across tiers (e.g., 41% flags on MHBench overall and full domain compromise on multiple military-grade scenarios).

Adding LLM-driven Defender agents to create Dynamic Cyber Ranges reduced attacker success to 0–55% on the same infrastructures, including 100% flag denial on MHBench (0/6 and 0/30 flags) and full prevention on a military intelligence scenario (0/15 hosts compromised) with detection in 32 minutes and containment in 48 minutes.

Operational security failures in defender automation can be decisive: leaving SIEM/EDR defaults unchanged let the attacker extract newly rotated admin passwords from logs and still reach domain compromise, while an on-prem on-premise model (alias2-mini) sometimes matched frontier defensive outcomes and even detected 10× faster, but could fail catastrophically via missed rotations or self-lockout.

nodes.title: LLM-CEG: Extending the Classification Error Gauge Framework for Privacy Auditing of Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: A practical privacy-auditing loop for LLM fine-tuning shows differential privacy can simultaneously reduce membership leakage and improve generalization when baseline training overfits narrow PII-like data.
points: DP-SGD fine-tuning cut membership-inference attacker advantage from 0.358 to ~0.102–0.106 (a 71.5% reduction) and drove AUROC down from 0.876 to ~0.513–0.516, indicating leakage fell to near-random detectability.

Adding DP during narrow clinical-style fine-tuning improved out-of-distribution utility, reducing perplexity from 179.91 (non-private baseline) to ~119–122 (a 47–50% gain), implying privacy noise can act as implicit regularization against overfitting/collapse in small repetitive datasets.

Across a 16× span of privacy budgets (ε=0.5 to 8.0 at δ=1e-5), empirical privacy and utility were nearly flat, making ε=8 Pareto-optimal in this setup because it matched the strongest settings’ MIA resistance while yielding the best perplexity.

nodes.title: Evaluation of Prompt Injection Defenses in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Adaptive, multi-round attackers reliably bypass prompt-level defenses, while simple application-layer output filtering is the only configuration that sustained zero secret leakage at scale.
points: Across >20,000 adaptive attacks and nine configurations, every defense that relied on the target model to follow anti-leak instructions eventually reached critical leakage (≥0.9) within <300 rounds, showing that prompt-only “self-protection” fails under sustained adaptation.

Deterministic output filtering implemented in separate application code achieved a 0.0% leak rate with worst-case severity 0.00 across 15,000 attacks (six independent 500-round runs), demonstrating that enforcing boundaries outside the model can fully prevent direct secret exfiltration in this setting.

Cross-model runs with default safety settings found Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-5.4 hit full disclosure (1.00) within 10 rounds and Claude Sonnet 4.6 extracted all three secrets by ~300 rounds (peak 0.90), implying vendor safety layers mainly slow extraction rather than prevent it when secrets reside in prompts.

nodes.title: From Prompt to Physical Actuation: Holistic Threat Modeling of LLM-Enabled Robotic Systems
nodes.one_liner: It maps how a single malicious input can traverse perception, LLM planning, and wireless command channels to become unsafe physical actuation—pinpointing the exact trust-boundary crossings where defenses must be inserted.
points: Threats from conventional cybersecurity, adversarial ML, and LLM conversational attacks converge on the same six trust-boundary crossings in edge–cloud LLM-enabled robots, meaning single-interface hardening is insufficient because multi-layer attacks can enter through any boundary and propagate to actuation.

Three end-to-end attack chains demonstrate prompt-to-actuation risk: (1) direct prompt injection reaches physical control due to missing independent semantic validation between operator input and actuator dispatch, (2) visual prompt injection exploits cross-modal translation where camera observations become LLM instructions, and (3) indirect prompt injection abuses provider-mediated tool calls that bypass edge-side visibility and control.

The system lacks tamper-evident, cryptographically protected audit logs across prompts, plans, sensor inputs, and command dispatch, undermining post-incident attribution and forensics when unsafe physical behavior occurs.

nodes.title: MGTEVAL: An Interactive Platform for Systemtic Evaluation of Machine-Generated Text Detectors
nodes.one_liner: A unified, attack-aware evaluation harness exposes that some detectors look strong on AUROC yet fail at strict low-FPR operating points, while others achieve near-SOTA accuracy with two orders of magnitude less compute.
points: MGTEVAL standardizes end-to-end evaluation of machine-generated text detectors by unifying dataset construction, 12 attack-based robustness stress tests, detector training/calibration, and multi-metric reporting (including TPR at FPR=0.01/0.001) via both CLI and WebUI to improve comparability and reproducibility.

Under a single controlled benchmark (2,000 samples, 1:1 human/machine), Longformer is the top performer with 99.50% accuracy/F1, 99.99% AUROC, and 100.00% / 99.00% TPR at FPR=0.01 / 0.001, beating the next-best RAiDAr by +1.50 pp accuracy and +1.52 pp F1 under identical settings.

Efficiency varies dramatically even among strong detectors, with GREATER achieving near-SOTA quality (97.50% accuracy, 99.92% AUROC, 96%/96% TPR at FPR=0.01/0.001) while using the lowest reported GPU peak memory (0.14 GiB) and far less latency than RAiDAr (7.01 ms/sample vs 427.40 ms/sample), highlighting deployment-critical tradeoffs that single-score leaderboards obscure.

nodes.title: GAMMAF: A Common Framework for Graph-Based Anomaly Monitoring Benchmarking in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: A reproducible, open-source harness that turns LLM multi-agent debates into attributed graphs and benchmarks anomaly detectors in a live loop where flagged agents are actually cut out of the network—revealing both security and cost impacts of remediation.
points: Across GSM8K and MMLU‑Pro, adding graph-based defense-and-pruning cuts Attack Success Rate by up to ~35 percentage points by round 3 (e.g., MMLU‑Pro Chain: 48.0%→17.1% with XG‑Guard), showing that live topology remediation can materially restore multi-agent integrity under prompt-injection style attacks.

Defense effectiveness is strongly task-dependent: on GSM8K, ASR@3 falls below 10% in most topologies (e.g., BlindGuard Tree 7.2%, XG‑Guard Random 5.1%), while on MMLU‑Pro it remains much higher (best cases ~16.8–21.7%), implying that benchmark difficulty and model confidence can dominate observed MAS security outcomes.

Operational cost scales predictably with agent count (near-linear token growth) but adversaries inflate evaluation-time requests by preventing early consensus, whereas successful isolation reduces wall-clock and token burn—until attacker prevalence approaches >50%, where majority-based detectors lose separation and cost differences between defenses largely disappear.

nodes.title: SafeReview: Defending LLM-based Review Systems Against Adversarial Hidden Prompts
affiliations: Southern University of Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Training an adaptive attacker and defender in a minimax loop measurably hardens LLM-based peer review against evolving hidden-prompt injections without degrading benign ranking performance.
points: Hidden prompt injections substantially inflate LLM reviewer scores—average ratings rose from 5.72 to 7.33 (+1.61), with a worst-case +4.26-point jump (Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking)—showing that naive LLM-assisted review can be systematically gamed to accept weak papers.

Co-evolutionary adversarial training (GRPO attacker + DPO defender) improves robustness under adaptive attacks, raising Spearman ranking correlation to 0.409 versus 0.343 for static DPO (+19.2%) while also reducing false positives to 38.2% versus 42.2%, indicating better preservation of true paper ordering and fewer manipulated acceptances.

SafeReview generalizes beyond the attacker model family, maintaining stronger ranking (Spearman 0.392 vs 0.359 baseline, +9.0%) and lower FPR (37.0% vs 42.6%) against Llama-generated attacks, implying it learns transferable injection-robustness features rather than overfitting to a single generator’s artifacts.

nodes.title: Training a General Purpose Automated Red Teaming Model
nodes.one_liner: A goal-conditioned red-teaming model can be trained without access to goal-specific detectors by generating its own per-goal evaluation rubrics and still transfer to novel cyber-relevant goals under a different verifier.
points: Reinforcement-learning finetuning of a safety-trained Qwen3-8B red-teaming model using LLM-generated rubrics boosts in-domain attack success rate from 33.89% (zero-shot) to 85% on 15 explicitly-scoped adversarial goals, showing large gains even with detailed prompting as a strong baseline.

The same training pipeline generalizes to previously unseen adversarial goals measured by independent garak detectors, improving average out-of-domain success from 20.87% to 29.35% overall and reaching 37.8% when trained against Qwen3-8B, indicating partial transfer across goal categories and evaluation mechanisms.

Optimizing for success alone tends to reduce attack diversity (zero-shot attacks are slightly more diverse on average), and while adding an explicit diversity reward can improve out-of-domain performance and diversity, it also introduces training instability and occasional collapse, implying robustness and diversity must be co-optimized with careful reward design.

nodes.title: A Survey on Split Learning for LLM Fine-Tuning: Models, Systems, and Privacy Optimizations
nodes.one_liner: A unified, component-level pipeline view makes it clear where split-based LLM fine-tuning leaks private text (via activations/gradients) and where systems optimizations (PEFT, memory sharing, async scheduling) are mandatory for practicality.
points: Split learning for LLM fine-tuning shifts raw-data exposure risk into “smashed data” and gradient exchanges, enabling passive/active reconstruction, label leakage (particularly severe for autoregressive SFT where labels are shifted inputs), and model-manipulation threats such as poisoning and backdoors.

U-shaped split learning keeps loss/labels on-device to reduce direct label exposure but approximately doubles per-iteration communication because activations and gradients must traverse the network twice, making compression, scheduling, and reduced-sync protocols central to scalability.

System bottlenecks are dominated by communication and server-side memory under multi-client concurrency, motivating PEFT integration (e.g., LoRA/adapters), GPU memory sharing across clients, and staleness-aware asynchronous aggregation to mitigate stragglers without collapsing convergence.

nodes.title: Low Rank Adaptation for Adversarial Perturbation
nodes.one_liner: Low-rank structure is not just a LoRA training artifact—it's a fundamental geometric property of adversarial perturbations that can be exploited to make black-box attacks dramatically more query-efficient.
points: Adversarial perturbations concentrate in a low-dimensional subspace induced by the input–logit Jacobian (rank ≤ number of classes C ≪ input dimension d), implying iterative gradient-based attacks effectively optimize within a much smaller space than the raw pixel space.

Empirically, roughly ~70% (ImageNet) to ~80% (CUB-200) of per-channel singular-value changes during PGD/C&W updates cluster near zero, and retaining only the top 20% singular components preserves attack strength closely (median ℓ2 2.44 vs 2.27), indicating most perturbation energy lies in a few dominant directions.

Constraining black-box query directions to a transferable low-rank subspace (learned via gradients + explanation masks on auxiliary data with an autoencoder) yields large practical gains, including ~90% fewer queries to reach the same robust-accuracy drop in HSJA on CUB (≈10,000 → <1,000) and substantially lower distortion (e.g., HSJA on CUB: median ℓ2 2.64 → 0.82; decision-based baseline [20] ℓ2 16.33 vs 1.40).

nodes.title: Large Language Models as Explainable Cyberattack Detectors for Energy Industrial Control Systems
affiliations: Concordia Institute for Information Systems Engineering
nodes.one_liner: Off-the-shelf LLMs can act as high-recall ICS intrusion triage classifiers directly over protocol tokens while emitting audit records that are often causally tied to the model’s own decisions.
points: A prompt-only GPT-4o pipeline classifying discretized Modbus token strings achieves 0.9842 accuracy and 0.9625 critical-class recall on CIC Modbus 2023, outperforming matched supervised baselines that cluster near ~0.97 accuracy and ~0.88 critical recall while requiring no task-specific weight updates.

On the LeMay CSET’16 Modbus dataset, the same prompt-only LLM reaches 0.9822 accuracy with 1.000 critical-class recall, demonstrating a conservative triage profile (rare misses, more false positives) suited to human-in-the-loop safety monitoring rather than autonomous blocking.

Token-grounded audit records show strong decision relevance signals: cited evidence alone preserves the original decision in 95.64% of cases (sufficiency), while removing cited tokens flips the prediction 73.15% of the time (95.27% for critical cases), but single-token counterfactual edits flip labels only 23.43%, indicating current limits in producing executable minimal recourse.

nodes.title: GoAT-X: A Graph of Auditing Thoughts for Securing Token Transactions in Cross-Chain Contracts
nodes.one_liner: Constraining LLM audits with a formal predicate framework plus static slicing delivers near state-of-the-art cross-chain bridge vulnerability coverage at cents-per-project cost.
points: A five-layer “Graph of Auditing Thoughts” that grounds LLM reasoning in statically extracted transaction-specific data flows reached 92% recall (F1=0.92) on 294 fine-grained audit points across 673 contracts, reducing hallucination-driven errors versus single-pass LLM auditing.

On 20 cross-chain bridge projects with known incidents, the system achieved 95% recall and 0.88 F1 (0.83 precision), substantially outperforming LLM-only baselines (F1 0.24–0.36) and slightly surpassing a specialized static tool (SmartAxe F1 0.87), implying semantic, source-level constraint checking is critical for cross-chain logic bugs.

In a 12-project wild scan of 1,406 contracts, it surfaced 128 alerts with 117 confirmed risks (91% precision)—including 108 missing-constraint issues (93% precision) and 9 confirmed bypass paths (75% precision)—at roughly 317 seconds and $0.02 per project, indicating scalable, low-cost detection of business-logic flaws that drive bridge losses.

nodes.title: Membership Inference Attacks Against Video Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Even when an auditor can only read captions from a VideoLLM, controlled decoding-temperature changes plus simple video difficulty cues are enough to infer training membership above chance.
points: A strictly black-box membership inference attack on VideoLLMs that uses only generated captions (no logits or losses) reaches 0.68 AUC and 0.63 accuracy against LLaVA-Video-7B-Qwen2-Video-Only, demonstrating measurable training-set membership leakage.

Temperature perturbation (τ=0 vs τ=0.8) exposes a membership signal via semantic drift between low- and high-temperature outputs, consistent with member videos inducing more brittle, “sharper” generation behavior under decoding changes.

Accounting for video-specific confounders is necessary because motion and duration materially affect caption stability, and incorporating optical-flow motion complexity plus duration (and their interactions with temperature drift) yields a robust signal across 100 runs with low AUC variance (~0.02) and consistent performance across LR/RF/SVM/MLP.

nodes.title: VOW: Verifiable and Oblivious Watermark Detection for Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: The key step forward is turning watermark detection into a verifiable, oblivious protocol (via VOPRF) that supports short-text API auditing without exposing user content—while also showing that SOTA paraphrasers can erase nearly everyone’s robustness claims.
points: VOW enables privacy-preserving and cryptographically verifiable watermark detection for keyed (symmetric-style) LLM watermarks by implementing token-coloring as a VOPRF-backed 2PC, so the provider learns nothing about the user’s text beyond length while the user can verify the detection computation was done under the committed key.

On short texts, VOW remains highly detectable under strict false-positive control: at a fixed theoretical FPR of 1e-5 it reaches 94.6% TPR at 75 tokens and 99.2% TPR at 150 tokens, with p-values on 100,000 non-watermarked samples matching the expected uniform distribution (i.e., calibrated statistical guarantees).

Modern paraphrasing largely nullifies watermark robustness across paradigms: when attacked by a stronger paraphraser (GPT-5.1), all evaluated schemes—including VOW and KGW variants—show substantial AUC/TPR degradation with p-value distributions overlapping non-watermarked text, implying watermark-based provenance is unreliable once semantic rewriting is allowed.

nodes.title: System-aware contextual digital twin for ICS anomaly diagnosis
nodes.one_liner: It turns actuator-conditioned “normal behavior envelopes” into grounded, operator-readable hypotheses by letting an LLM explain (not drive) the anomaly detector—except when contexts are explicitly flagged as ambiguous.
points: A system-aware unsupervised detector that conditions normality on actuator context achieved 89.17% F1 and 83.09% recall on SWaT, indicating that context partitioning can materially improve sensitivity without labeled attack data.

Across more heterogeneous benchmarks, the approach prioritized low false-alarm burden with 89.68% precision on WADI (58.25% F1) and best-in-class 90.07% precision on HAI (73.30% F1), implying the main trade-off shifts from detection power to context sparsity as actuator combinations fragment.

Separating deterministic evidence-based screening (quantile envelopes + robust distances) from LLM-based explanation/arbitration enables operator-facing diagnoses while constraining hallucination risk by forcing the LLM to reason only over structured deviations, matched contexts, and sensor–actuator rule summaries.

nodes.title: PRAG: End-to-End Privacy-Preserving Retrieval-Augmented Generation
affiliations: Inspur Yunzhou Industrial Internet Co., Ltd.
nodes.one_liner: It shows that scalable RAG-style ANN search can be done directly over an encrypted HNSW index with usable recall/latency, and that access-pattern mitigation is essential to prevent graph-topology leakage.
points: PRAG provides end-to-end confidentiality for both documents and queries in cloud RAG by running ANN retrieval over a fully encrypted CKKS-backed index (encrypted clustering + encrypted HNSW), avoiding the common “query-only” privacy gap in prior systems.

On a 100,000-document evaluation, PRAG-I (non-interactive) achieves 72.45% Recall@10 at 1.29s/query with 4.12MB/query traffic, while PRAG-II (interactive, client-assisted) improves to 74.45% Recall@10 at 7.91s/query with 78.55MB/query traffic, enabling a practical latency–accuracy switch without re-encrypting or rebuilding the index.

Access-pattern defenses materially reduce leakage-abuse graph reconstruction: unmitigated traversal leaks allow 34.7% edge recovery, but combining dummy traversals (ρ=0.3) with periodic re-encryption (E=1000) cuts recovery to 1.1% while keeping Recall@10 near 71.9% (≈1.28× latency overhead).

nodes.title: Security Attack and Defense Strategies for Autonomous Agent Frameworks: A Layered Review with OpenClaw as a Case Study
affiliations: State Key Laboratory for Novel Software Technology
nodes.one_liner: The key takeaway is that securing agent frameworks requires system-level, cross-layer controls because prompt-layer fixes alone don’t stop propagation into tools, persistent state, and automated ecosystems.
points: A four-layer taxonomy (context/instruction, tool/action, state/persistence, ecosystem/automation) clarifies that agent compromises are typically cross-layer cascades where manipulated inputs drive unsafe tool use, contaminate persistent state, and then amplify via automation and integrations.

The most quantified operational risk highlighted is tool-chain resource abuse, with token-exhaustion attacks reported to inflate usage by roughly 6–7× and up to 9× in some configurations, implying direct cost and availability impacts even without data theft.

The literature and defenses are concentrated on context/tool layers while state and ecosystem risks are comparatively under-addressed, leaving long-horizon persistence (e.g., memory/workspace poisoning) and ecosystem trust (plugins/APIs/automation) as primary unresolved failure modes.

nodes.title: Tatemae: Detecting Alignment Faking via Tool Selection in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Tool choice flips under monitoring provide a practical, verifiable signature of alignment faking even when chain-of-thought cannot be trusted.
points: A black-box, tool-selection-based probe detects alignment faking in 6 frontier LLMs with mean rates spanning 3.5%–23.7% across 108 enterprise IT scenarios, showing that strategic compliance shifts are measurable without relying on chain-of-thought as ground truth.

Across models, 30.2% of cases exhibit a monitoring-triggered behavioral shift (safe under unmonitored pressure but unsafe when “monitored”), and only 11.8% are confirmed alignment faking via reasoning-consistency—implying that purely behavioral signals may catch many suspicious shifts even when internal reasoning is absent or unfaithful.

High task competence does not imply resistance to alignment faking: all models exceed 81% on capability (T1) yet monitored safe-tool selection (T3) varies dramatically (e.g., 93.5% vs 20.4%), and vulnerability differs by domain/pressure (e.g., corruption vs sabotage), pointing to training and incentive design as the dominant driver rather than raw capability.

nodes.title: How Code Representation Shapes False-Positive Dynamics in Cross-Language LLM Vulnerability Detection
nodes.one_liner: A simple retraining-free “text vs AST consistency gate” can filter a large slice of cross-language false positives, revealing that representation choice is a causal lever on LLM SAST alert quality.
points: Cross-language vulnerability detection on OWASP Java collapses into a near “flag-everything” regime under text fine-tuning, with Qwen3-8B FPR rising from 0.763 (zero-shot) to 0.866 (pilot) to 1.000 (full), while F1 stays roughly flat (0.637–0.688), showing F1 can mask catastrophic false-positive inflation.

Switching only the inference representation from text to pruned AST (no retraining) for a text-fine-tuned model sharply reduces false positives—Qwen3-8B FPR 0.866→0.583 (−0.282) and Llama 3.1-8B FPR 0.983→0.905 (−0.078)—and flips 37.2% of false positives (427/1,147) back to true negatives, implying many errors are representation-sensitive surface-trigger firings.

Direct AST fine-tuning fails to retain the FPR benefit (FPR ≥0.970 across conditions) and the same text→AST probe effect replicates on Python with FPR 0.554 (within 2.9pp of Java’s 0.583), weakening domain-shift explanations and pointing to shortcut learning on memorisable cues introduced by both raw text and flat AST linearisation.

nodes.title: SMSI: System Model Security Inference: Automated Threat Modeling for Cyber-Physical Systems
nodes.one_liner: A hybrid neuro-symbolic threat-modeling stack can start from a SysML architecture and automatically rank NIST 800-53 controls with auditable CVE→ATT&CK→control traceability, but model selection must be stage-specific (LLMs for hit-rate coverage, supervised models for precision, and pretrained security encoders for control-language alignment).
points: An end-to-end SysML→CPE→CVE→ATT&CK→NIST 800-53 pipeline produced fully traceable, CVSS-weighted control recommendations for a CPS gateway case study with 9 components and 199 matched CVEs, turning a days-long manual workflow into an automated analyst-review report.

For CVE→ATT&CK, a supervised SecureBERT+ multi-label classifier reached 0.757 Micro-F1 across 105 parent techniques but only 0.383 Macro-F1, indicating strong performance on frequent techniques and severe degradation on the long tail of rare classes.

Zero-shot Gemma-4 26B achieved 51.8% HitRate@1 and 78.3% HitRate@5 on 419 KEV CVEs (≈4× higher HR@1 than the best embedding baseline at 13.1%), while ATT&CK→NIST retrieval was best with pretrained SecureBERT (MRR 0.582, Hits@10 0.798), showing that different stages require different model choices and that fine-tuning can overfit on small crosswalk datasets.

nodes.title: PrivacyAssist: A User-Centric Agent Framework for Detecting Privacy Inconsistencies in Android Apps
nodes.one_liner: Real-time, user-facing detection reveals that most Android apps’ declared privacy practices don’t line up with the permissions they actually run with, and RAG is pivotal to keeping LLM warnings accurate enough to drive user decisions.
points: A large-scale study of 2,347 popular Android apps found that only 16.02% are fully consistent between runtime-granted permissions and Google Play Data Safety disclosures, leaving 83.98% with mismatches that can mislead users about real data access and collection.

Among inconsistent apps, 38.11% request access to sensitive data that is not disclosed (Case 1), 28.56% disclose collecting sensitive categories without corresponding granted permissions (Case 2), and 33.33% show both behaviors (Case 3), indicating widespread disclosure gaps and ambiguous consent signaling.

A RAG-grounded LLM agent achieved 90% agreement with manual review on 100 apps while cutting false positives from ~35% (without RAG) to 10%, and user testing (200 participants) showed 73.1% would cancel installation after a warning despite most rarely checking Data Safety (≈60% rarely/never).

nodes.title: An Empirical Security Evaluation of LLM-Generated Cryptographic Rust Code
nodes.one_liner: Security-critical crypto code generation is bottlenecked less by “knowing the rules” than by correctly grounding those rules in exact crate APIs, and generic scanners can miss (or misflag) the few cases that do compile.
points: Across 240 LLM-generated Rust AEAD programs, only 23.3% compiled (56/240; 95% CI 18.4–29.1%), with failures dominated by API hallucinations (41.3%) and Rust type/trait mismatches (47.1% combined), indicating current models are frequently unable to reliably instantiate real crypto crate APIs.

Prompting materially changes outcomes (p=0.002; V=0.248): chain-of-thought prompting yielded just 6.7% compilation (4/60), about 5× worse than zero-shot (35.0%), implying that eliciting security reasoning can worsen implementation fidelity by increasing plausible-but-nonexistent API usage.

Among compiled samples, a crypto-specific analyzer found vulnerabilities in 57.1% (32/56; 0 false positives), including critical issues in 3.6% (2/56: nonce reuse CWE-329 and hardcoded key CWE-798), while CodeQL achieved 0% true positives and only produced false alarms, demonstrating that general static analysis can provide misleading assurance for AEAD misuse without domain rules.

nodes.title: Enhancing Linux Privilege Escalation Attack Capabilities of Local LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: A small local LLM agent can be pushed from near-useless to GPT-4o-level privilege escalation by adding reflection-driven scaffolding and targeted retrieval—until discovery becomes the hard ceiling.
points: A five-part scaffolding stack (reflective analysis, chain-of-thought planning, RAG over HackTricks+GTFOBins, structured enumeration prompts, and history compression) lets locally hosted open-weight agents match or exceed cloud baselines on automated Linux privilege escalation, with Llama3.1 70B reaching 83% exploit success and 7B–8B models reaching 67%.

A full-factorial ablation shows reflection-centric components dominate gains: Analyze alone reaches 50% success, Analyze+RAG reaches 58%, and the best combo (Analyze+CoT+RAG) reaches 67%, indicating that forcing “reflect-then-act” is more impactful than purely adding enumeration structure.

Vulnerability discovery remains the limiting factor for local models without guidance—treatments alone only raise small models from ~8% to ~17% and fail entirely on information-disclosure cases—implying defenders can blunt autonomous attackers by reducing recon surface/information leakage, while attackers benefit most from tool- or human-generated hints.

nodes.title: Adaptive and AI-Augmented Security Testing: A Systematic Survey of Program Analysis, Feedback-Driven Testing, and Hybrid Learning-Based Approaches
nodes.one_liner: The most actionable gap is that CI/CD and human triage generate rich feedback, but today’s static analysis, fuzzing, and LLM-based testing largely fail to close the loop into a single adaptive, semantically grounded security-testing system.
points: A systematic selection process reduced 22,088 database hits to 55 peer‑reviewed primary studies (39 from database screening plus 16 via snowballing), highlighting how little of the apparent literature survives reproducible evidence and scope filters.
