A temporal re-entry pattern—tainted write → exposed read into an authority-bearing context → high-risk action—makes reads an integrity-critical event in LLM agents, and enforcing runtime temporal re-entry constraints plus capability attenuation is claimed to provably prevent persistent worm propagation without disabling normal workflows.

nodes.title: A Sentence Relation-Based Approach to Sanitizing Malicious Instructions
nodes.one_liner: A non-generative NLI-based sanitizer can beat LLM-judge and fine-tuning defenses by surgically removing instruction-incompatible spans while keeping latency low and robustness high under adaptive attacks.
points: SONAR’s sentence-relation (NLI graph) pruning drives prompt-injection attack success to near-zero across multiple RAG/agent benchmarks, including 0.0–1.7% ASR on several Open-Prompt-Injection classification tasks and 2.0%/15.0%/0.0% ASR on BIPIA email/code/abstract respectively.

Compared to nine established defenses, the strongest baselines still exhibit large residual risk under distribution shift (e.g., SecAlign reaches 82.0% ASR on BIPIA code vs 83.0% undefended), while SONAR remains substantially lower (15.0% ASR) without any fine-tuning of the target model.

Utility impact is bounded: on clean inputs SONAR reduces task fidelity by ~4.3 points on average (e.g., Llama-3-8B TF 95.9%→90.9%), while avoiding denial-of-service behaviors like DataSentinel’s trivial 0% ASR that collapses TF to 0 on Open-Prompt-Injection.

nodes.title: APIOT: Autonomous Vulnerability Management Across Bare-Metal Industrial OT Networks
nodes.one_liner: Autonomous agents can now purple-team bare-metal OT by reasoning directly over industrial protocol bytes, and lightweight runtime guardrails—not model tuning—are the biggest lever for dependable long-horizon execution.
points: An LLM-agent framework executed the full discovery→exploit→patch→verify cycle on bare-metal Zephyr RTOS OT devices with a 90.0% end-to-end mission success rate across 70 runs, demonstrating that protocol-field reasoning can replace scarce human OT exploit expertise as the main scaling factor.

A runtime governance “overseer” materially improved reliability and efficiency: success rose from 90.0% (27/30) to 100% (30/30) and average turns-to-completion dropped 20.5% (27.3→21.7), while degenerate behaviors persisted without it (e.g., 100% of overseer-OFF runs exploited without crash verification and 66.7% entered repetitive loops).

Capability generalized across models and adverse networks but revealed a concrete boundary: across 150 cross-model runs overall success was 85.3% (128/150), heavy impairment still yielded 100% success for CoAP and Modbus in tested settings, yet CoAP in a 3-tier Edge–Fog–Cloud topology collapsed to 30% (3/10) due to multi-hop planning stalls, indicating stateless UDP lateral-movement is a primary near-term weakness.

nodes.title: Heimdallr: Characterizing and Detecting LLM-Induced Security Risks in GitHub CI Workflows
nodes.one_liner: LLM prompt injection becomes materially more dangerous in CI because model outputs can cross the boundary into privileged repo operations and shell execution, and nearly half of observed LLM-enabled workflows are externally triggerable.
points: Across 16,818 content-unique LLM-integrated GitHub Actions workflows (50,354 instances), 43.5% include at least one externally triggerable LLM node, indicating that untrusted issue/PR/comment text often reaches LLM calls in security-critical CI contexts.

A hybrid static+LLM analysis approach (graph-normalized workflow modeling, triggerability reasoning, and cross-language taint propagation through LLM prompt/response boundaries) achieved F1=0.994 for LLM-node identification, 99.8% exact triggerability-mode accuracy, and micro-F1=0.917 (macro-F1=0.874) for detecting six concrete LLM-induced CI threat vectors.

Verified real-world exploitation potential is substantial, with 802 vulnerable workflow instances disclosed across 759 repositories (71 acknowledged/fixed), showing LLM-mediated command execution, agent hijacking, and attacker-steered repository changes are not theoretical but present in widely used projects.

nodes.title: LeakDojo: Decoding the Leakage Threats of RAG Systems
nodes.one_liner: Better instruction-following and more faithful RAG answers can unintentionally make chunk exfiltration easier, and a simple multiplicative model (SLT×ARC) predicts leakage surprisingly well.
points: Leakage in RAG can be modeled as two largely independent factors—adversarial retrieval coverage (ARC) from the query generator and successful leak triggering (SLT) from the instruction—where cumulative leakage (CCL) is closely approximated by SLT×ARC, enabling modular attack/defense evaluation.

Models with stronger instruction-following show higher leakage risk, with SLT positively correlated with IFEval instruction-following scores (Pearson r=0.578, p=0.039), implying that capability gains can directly increase exfiltration susceptibility.

RAG quality improvements can raise exposure: a rewriter increases and stabilizes retrieval coverage (making weak queries reliably hit more chunks), while higher faithfulness correlates with higher leakage across datasets (e.g., FIQA r=0.83, p=4.88e-7), creating an inherent utility–privacy trade-off.

nodes.title: Stable Agentic Control: Tool-Mediated LLM Architecture for Autonomous Cyber Defense
nodes.one_liner: Constraining both defender and adversary to catalog-bounded, tool-computed moves makes stability a property of the closed loop—so even nondeterministic LLM controllers can yield deterministic, formally certified outcomes.
points: A tool-mediated LLM control loop with finite action catalogs and deterministic game/observer tools achieved mechanically-verified closed-loop guarantees (controllability, observability under asymmetric sensors, and ISS robustness to best-responding adversaries) via a composite Lyapunov certificate checked in Lean 4 with zero-sorry proofs.

Across 282 real enterprise attack graphs, the defender-only loop produced non-increasing attacker game value in 282/282 cases and reduced mean S from 0.509 to 0.408 (19.7%) in ~3 rounds, while under adaptive edge-injecting adversaries all single-round spikes remained within the ISS bound (max ΔS=0.74 vs γ≤1.0) with per-graph max excursion <0.60 (40% margin).

On paired offensive/defensive telemetry, a tool-mediated Claude Sonnet 4 controller cut final game value from 0.8367 (deterministic greedy) to 0.3427 (59% lower) with σ=0 across 40 runs at four temperatures, while a weaker Claude Haiku 4.5 remained catalog-bounded (0 off-catalog actions) but converged to suboptimal values (mean 0.603, σ=0.249), demonstrating stability is enforced by architecture whereas optimality depends on controller capability.

nodes.title: Exposing LLM Safety Gaps Through Mathematical Encoding:New Attacks and Systematic Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Mathematical “word problems” that force the model to reconstruct intent can evade modern safety stacks at ~50% success, and proof-based encodings appear to generalize the weakness beyond set-theory jailbreaks.
points: Deep mathematical reformulation via a helper LLM bypasses safety at 46–56% average attack success across eight models and two benchmarks, while rule-based “math formatting” stays near baseline (9–11% vs 7–10%), indicating most defenses still key on surface semantics rather than abstract intent.

A new Formal Logic proof-style encoding matches Set Theory overall (≈50% HarmBench ASR; 64–70% on JailbreakBench) and is stronger on newer GPT-5-family models (42.1% vs 37.1% average; e.g., GPT-5-Mini 57% vs 44% on JailbreakBench), suggesting proof-structured abstractions are harder for updated safety training to recognize.

Prompt repetition post-processing is largely ineffective as either a defense or amplifier (85.7% of repeats change ASR by ≤5 percentage points; mean ΔASR −0.46%), implying the vulnerability is stable and driven by the initial abstraction/encoding quality rather than fragile formatting tricks.

nodes.title: PragLocker: Protecting Agent Intellectual Property in Untrusted Deployments via Non-Portable Prompts
affiliations: Innovation and Management Center, School of Software Technology (Ningbo), Zhejiang University
University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: Model-conditioned, noise-tuned prompts behave like non-textual triggers: they keep utility on the intended LLM yet break even across closely related variants (e.g., quantized siblings).
points: Black-box prompt obfuscation can make stolen agent system prompts largely non-reusable across models, cutting cross-LLM portability to 0.20× of the unprotected baseline while keeping target-task performance essentially unchanged at 1.01×.

A two-stage design is critical: code/symbol rewriting alone is almost fully portable (0.99×), optimization alone is only mildly effective (0.82×), but combining both collapses transferred performance to near-zero on multiple agent benchmarks.

Adaptive recovery is economically unattractive because querying the target LLM to “recover” text yields limited improvement (3.49× relative gain) and direct deobfuscation barely helps (1.03×), while simply inducing a fresh prompt from I/O examples is far more effective (4.78×), implying IP protection should be complemented with defenses against behavior cloning/distillation.

nodes.title: LoopTrap: Termination Poisoning Attacks on LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Indirect prompt injection can target an agent’s control-flow (termination) rather than its outputs, turning “helpful” self-evaluation into a scalable resource-exhaustion primitive.
points: Termination poisoning reliably drives agentic non-termination, with 10 simple injection strategies causing a mean 2.33× step amplification across 8 mainstream LLM agents and reaching 3.93× token overhead in the automated setting.

LoopTrap’s adaptive red-teaming (profiling + context-aware synthesis + skill library) achieves 86.3% attack success rate with 3.57× average step amplification and a 25× peak, outperforming the strongest static baseline by +14.6 percentage points ASR and +0.96× SAF.

Vulnerability is strongly model- and task-dependent: Grok-4 and GPT-4o-mini show high susceptibility (≈3.36× and 3.50× average SAF under static attacks), while more objectively verifiable tasks (e.g., math/logic) resist authority-style traps (e.g., 3.58→1.68 SAF drop for an authority strategy), implying termination should be guarded by provenance-aware context separation and external progress validation rather than self-evaluation alone.

nodes.title: When Embedding-Based Defenses Fail: Rethinking Safety in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.one_liner: Stealthy “near-benign” messaging breaks embedding-only MAS safety, while token-level confidence buys a short, topology-dependent window for effective mitigation.
points: Embedding/graph anomaly defenses in LLM multi-agent systems can be provably bypassed when attackers keep message embeddings within a benign acceptance neighborhood (dist(h,SB)≤γ/L), causing threshold-based detectors to accept malicious messages by construction.

Near-benign attacks materially reduce embedding separability (benign–malicious cosine distance drops from 0.236 in “obvious” attacks to 0.176 with Slow Drift and 0.156 with Benign Wrapper), which correlates with sharp robustness collapses for embedding-based defenses (e.g., GUARDIAN falls to 30.2–35.0% accuracy across near-benign attacks vs 67.0% clean on MMLU with LLaMA-3.1-8B).

Token-level confidence (entropy-based) provides complementary early-round signal—AUROC under near-benign attacks is 0.84 at round 1 vs 0.62 for embedding scores with pruning—and a confidence-guided defense recovers substantial accuracy under attack (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B MMLU Acc-Avg 52.7% vs 39.3% G-Safeguard and 32.6% GUARDIAN), but effectiveness decays rapidly in dense topologies (signal half-life drops to 1 round in fully connected graphs with ASR rising to 0.48).

nodes.title: MAGE: Safeguarding LLM Agents against Long-Horizon Threats via Shadow Memory
nodes.one_liner: A “shadow stack”-inspired, safety-only memory layer makes long-horizon agent attacks visible early and blockable without sacrificing normal task completion.
points: MAGE’s shadow-memory guardrail cut sequential tool-attack chaining (STAC) attack success from 100.0% to 8.3% while preserving 94.4% benign utility using only a small local Qwen3-4B defender model.
Against long-horizon persistent indirect prompt injection (PI2), MAGE achieved 0.0% attack success while maintaining 73.0% benign utility—matching the no-defense utility ceiling in that setting.

Turn-wise RL with joint training of the memory manager and judge is pivotal: without RL, the same architecture collapses to indiscriminate blocking (0.0% ASR but 0.0–8.1% BU), while full training yields early detection (mean latency −0.06 turns for STAC and 0.05 turns for PI2) at modest overhead (~7.0K extra tokens/task for STAC; ~2.9–4.8K for prompt-injection tasks).

nodes.title: Sealing the Audit-Runtime Gap for LLM Skills
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: It turns skill vetting from a soft, stage-bound process into an end-to-end, verifiable supply-chain guarantee by making audited bytes the only bytes that can be loaded.
points: An on-chain skill registry plus a mandatory Skill Verification Loader (SVL) cryptographically binds “what was audited” to “what is loaded,” blocking post-approval rug-pulls (95.1% detection) and local tampering (100% blocked) before any skill content reaches the agent context.

Across 1,023 in-the-wild skills and six attack classes, the system is the only evaluated approach to cover the full lifecycle (submission→anchoring→invocation), achieving 97.6% accuracy on explicit injection/implicit poisoning and 90.2% on cross-skill interaction—highlighting that runtime-only or scan-only defenses leave systematic gaps.

A DAO audit committee with reputation-weighted voting and stake-and-slash incentives remains robust under collusion (stable accuracy with 20–40% malicious auditors) while keeping deployment practical, with batched on-chain verification completing in under 86 ms and audit token costs under ~3% of a typical $20/month LLM subscription quota.

nodes.title: Misrouter: Exploiting Routing Mechanisms for Input-Only Attacks on Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
nodes.one_liner: MoE routers are a practical, transferable jailbreak surface: steering expert selection—rather than token probabilities—produces significantly stronger input-only attacks against real API deployments.
points: Input-only routing manipulation can jailbreak Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLM services by transferring prompts optimized on open-source surrogate MoEs to related public APIs, expanding routing attacks beyond settings that require model modification.

Routing-aware prompt optimization composed with an existing jailbreak (Misrouter+FFA) raised average black-box attack success rate from 20.8% to 39.7% across six major MoE LLM API services, while output-only optimization (e.g., GCG) often transferred with near-zero success.

Exploiting MoE safety gaps requires shaping routing toward weakly aligned experts while anchoring to high-capability general-purpose experts and using a two-phase optimization to stabilize routing–generation coupling, and simple defenses (retraining weak experts or biasing routing toward safety experts) reduced but did not eliminate high ASR (e.g., 96%→30–44% in evaluated settings).

nodes.title: One Turn Too Late: Response-Aware Defense Against Hidden Malicious Intent in Multi-Turn Dialogue
nodes.one_liner: The key twist is treating multi-turn safety as a timing problem—block exactly at the first harm-enabling turn, using the model’s would-be response as part of the evidence.
points: A response-aware, turn-level monitor (TURNGATE) that inspects the candidate assistant reply before delivery cuts harmful miss rate from 37.9% to 17.7% versus a strong turn-level SFT baseline while increasing exact “closure-turn” blocking accuracy from 34.3% to 41.4%, yielding a substantially better safety–utility trade-off on MTID (F1ϕ2: 0.610→0.699).

Off-the-shelf guardrails that score turns largely in isolation preserve benign pass-through (e.g., Llama Guard benign score 0.998) but fail to detect distributed multi-turn intent (harmful score ≈0.002), implying that single-turn or output-only safety filters can be near-blind to gradual capability buildup.

Under closed-loop adaptive tree-search attacks, TURNGATE achieves the lowest attack success rate across budgets—0.26 at i=1 and staying below 0.68 at i=5—while query-only or intent-only monitors degrade toward high ASR (e.g., Intention Analysis ≈0.77 at i=5), indicating response-aware intervention degrades more gracefully against adaptive probing.

nodes.title: Generating Proof-of-Vulnerability Tests to Help Enhance the Security of Complex Software
nodes.one_liner: An agentic pipeline can turn dependency CVEs into executable, app-specific PoV tests with >90% compile success, but only ~55% of tests truly demonstrate exploitability and RCE remains a major gap.
points: Across 33 Java application–library pairs spanning 20 CVEs, the system identified 207/216 valid source-to-sink call paths (96% precision) and recovered 25/31 known qualifying entry points (81% recall; 88% F1), showing LLM-guided call-path discovery can be accurate enough to map supply-chain exposure at scale.

From 152 entry-point tasks, 141 generated JUnit tests compiled (≈92–93%) and 84 tests actually triggered vulnerable behavior (55% overall), with strong performance on directory traversal (77%) and “other” bug classes (84%) but very weak results on RCE (10%), indicating exploit synthesis is still heavily vulnerability-class dependent.

Automated judging remained unreliable—agent self-critique was 88% accurate for compilability but only 62% for true PoV demonstration, while a separate LLM log-based assessor reached 68–79% accuracy—implying build-and-run feedback loops help but still require human verification before driving remediation decisions.

nodes.title: MEMSAD: Gradient-Coupled Anomaly Detection for Memory Poisoning in Retrieval-Augmented Agents
nodes.one_liner: Persistent external memory turns one-time injections into compounding, session-spanning compromises, and the core remaining gap is discrete synonym-level near-isometries that bypass continuous-space guarantees.
points: Correcting the triggered-query evaluation protocol increases measured memory-poisoning success by 4× (ASR-R 0.25→1.00), showing that small benchmarking mismatches can fully mask worst-case persistent compromise.

A write-time semantic anomaly detector based on max/mean cosine similarity can be made provably coupled to retrieval gradients, implying any continuous evasion that reduces detection score must also reduce retrieval rank, and calibration is minimax-optimal up to log factors with a sample lower bound of Ω(1/ρ²).

Across a 3×5 attack–defense matrix with rigorous statistical validation (20 trials, n=1,000), composite defenses achieved TPR=1.00 and FPR=0.00 for all studied attacks, but discrete synonym substitutions evaded detection with ~0 retrieval impact (ΔASR-R≈0), exposing a hard boundary for embedding-only defenses.

nodes.title: Toward a Principled Framework for Agent Safety Measurement
nodes.one_liner: Agent safety evaluation becomes more deployment-realistic when it systematically searches the agent–environment trajectory tree under a likelihood budget instead of reporting a single greedy safe/unsafe outcome.
points: Trajectory-search-based evaluation exposes long-tail unsafe agent behaviors that greedy decoding and a few sampled rollouts miss, producing a likelihood-weighted safety score that better reflects deployment risk under stochastic decoders (top-p/top-k).

On 72 Agent-SafetyBench prompts with a 600s/prompt budget on H100s, the highest mean safety scores were 0.53 (Qwen2.5-32B) and 0.52 (Llama-3.1-70B), while small-model performance lagged sharply (Llama-3.1-8B: 0.31 overall; 0.18 on 'greedy-mixed'), implying substantial safety robustness gains from scale that binary greedy labels obscure.

Across 7 prompt-injection cases, an injection-specific instructional defense improved mean safety by +0.127 to +0.502 depending on model (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B: 0.396→0.825), while removing an explicit 'ignore previous instructions' marker strengthened attacks by lowering safety scores by 0.133–0.337, indicating that overt override phrases can trigger learned refusal cues and underestimate real-world stealthy injection risk.

nodes.title: SafeHarbor: Hierarchical Memory-Augmented Guardrail for LLM Agent Safety
affiliations: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: A hierarchical memory of prohibition+exemption rules yields a sharper, context-dependent decision boundary that measurably reduces over-refusal while staying fast enough for production agent loops.
points: SAFEHARBOR improves the safety–utility trade-off by reaching 63.6% benign utility on GPT-4o while still refusing >93% of harmful requests, indicating that context-aware, rule+exemption guardrails can cut over-refusal without materially weakening protection.

On AgentSafetyBench, SAFEHARBOR raises GPT-4o environment-interaction defenses to 62.05% Refusal-Env (vs 47.35% for A-Mem and 45.31% for vanilla RAG), showing that hierarchical rule retrieval plus reasoning is notably better at catching tool-mediated harm than flat retrieval or static prompts.

Efficiency results show SAFEHARBOR keeps deployment to a single 7B model (14 GB VRAM) with 306.67 ms latency—~20× faster than GuardAgent (6433 ms) and faster than LlamaGuard’s two-model setup (379.30 ms, 30 GB)—making robust agent guardrails feasible under tight latency/VRAM budgets.

nodes.title: Architecture Matters: Comparing RAG Systems under Knowledge Base Poisoning
nodes.one_liner: Architecture alone creates a ~58-point robustness spread against N=1 RAG poisoning, and most of CorruptRAG-AK’s edge comes from persuasive framing that breaks content-level credibility reasoning rather than retrieval.
points: Under realistic single-document (N=1) knowledge-base poisoning on 921 Natural Questions, clean-conditioned attack success rate varies dramatically by RAG architecture—from 81.9% (vanilla) to 24.4% (Recursive Language Models)—despite ~92% clean accuracy for vanilla/agentic/RLM, implying system design choices can outweigh many add-on defenses in practical robustness.

Decomposing CorruptRAG-AK’s advantage over naive contradictions shows the dominant failure point is content reasoning rather than retrieval for 3/4 systems (content-effect share ≈64% vanilla, ≈88% agentic, ~100% RLM), indicating that meta-epistemic framing primarily exploits credibility assessment and should be countered with generation-stage defenses (e.g., contradiction arbitration and source-trust calibration), not just retrieval filtering.

Multi-agent debate (MADAM-RAG) shows high indecision even when unpoisoned (56.6% clean accuracy driven by a 41.4% non-answer rate) and gains little additional vulnerability from adversarial framing once the poison is retrieved (content effect −1.8 pp, CI crosses 0), suggesting debate-style pipelines may dampen rhetorical framing yet still fail to reliably resolve conflicts into correct answers.

nodes.title: Needle-in-RAG: Prompt-Conditioned Character-Level Traceback of Poisoned Spans in Retrieved Evidence
nodes.one_liner: RAG poison forensics becomes materially more actionable when it can pinpoint the exact responsible characters inside an otherwise benign retrieved chunk instead of quarantining whole passages.
points: A two-pass black-box traceback design (prompt-anchored logging plus replay-based counterfactual masking) enables character-level attribution of RAG misgeneration causes without gradients or model internals, making fine-grained RAG forensics feasible in closed-source deployments.

Across two QA corpora, five poisoning attack families, and six LLM backends, character-level localization improved from near-noise baselines (e.g., NQ Char IoU ≈0.016–0.078 and Char FPR ≈0.977–0.985 for passage-level methods) to high-fidelity spans (RAGCharacter NQ Char IoU 0.844–0.970 with Char FPR 0.003–0.131), showing that passage-level traceback systematically over-attributes while span-level causal tests can sharply reduce collateral removal.

Dataset and model behavior dominate event traceability: open-weight models produced high rates of traceable poisoning events on NQ (e.g., Gemma EVT 0.86–0.95), whereas a more guarded model largely abstained (gpt-5-mini UNK 0.93–0.97 with EVT 0.03–0.10), implying that operational monitoring should expect many untraceable outcomes (abstentions/confusions) depending on backend and corpus noise.

nodes.title: LLM Ghostbusters: Surgical Hallucination Suppression via Adaptive Unlearning
nodes.one_liner: A closed-loop, token-surgical unlearning objective can materially harden code LLMs against package-confusion attacks by suppressing fabricated dependencies while preserving benchmark performance.
points: Adaptive Unlearning cuts LLM-generated Python package hallucinations from 21.2% to 2.6% on DeepSeek-Coder-7B (≈88% reduction), directly shrinking the slopsquatting/software-supply-chain attack surface without requiring retraining or human labels.

Compared to baselines, AU achieves the best joint tradeoff—e.g., on 7B it beats gradient ascent (7.2% hallucination rate), NPO (14.2%), and PMC (11.8%) while keeping coding utility essentially intact (68.6% average pass@1 vs 67.0% baseline on EvalPlus).

Distributional drift is highly localized: AU’s KL divergence on package generations is ~1.42 nats while code KL is ~0.08 nats (≈17× smaller), indicating updates largely avoid collateral damage to general code generation behavior.

nodes.title: Autonomous Adversary: Red-Teaming in the age of LLM
nodes.one_liner: Structured governance (expert-defined plans + explicit validation predicates) materially improves LLM-agent red-teaming outcomes, but raw autonomy still collapses on credential/state handling and tool-execution reliability.
points: In a live multi-host Windows Active Directory lateral-movement testbed, expert-defined task chains produced the highest reliability—achieving 100% completion in Scenario-1 (9/9 tasks) for Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Opus 4.5, and GPT-5.1—while the same models frequently failed under self-scaffolded and fully autonomous operation due to execution and state brittleness.

Autonomy sharply increased variance and cost: in Scenario-1 fully autonomous mode, the best run reached 80% task completion (16/20) but consumed ~1.25M tokens and ~199 minutes, indicating that partial progress can be achieved at high operational expense and with unstable convergence.

Failures clustered around concrete operational bottlenecks—command/remote execution fragility, credential and identity transition errors, and weak evidence-gating—driving loss-of-control indicators like long retry loops that burned on the order of 10^5–10^6 tokens without producing judge-verifiable artifacts.

nodes.title: AgentTrust: Runtime Safety Evaluation and Interception for AI Agent Tool Use
nodes.one_liner: Semantics-aware, fail-safe, millisecond-latency tool-call interception plus deobfuscation can outperform zero-shot LLM judging on agent safety while keeping deployment practical.
points: A real-time interceptor for AI-agent tool calls can reach 95.0% verdict accuracy and 73.7% risk-level accuracy on a 300-scenario benchmark while keeping rule-only median latency in the low-millisecond range, making safety enforcement feasible inline with agent execution.

Shell-command deobfuscation is a primary driver of safety coverage: removing the normalizer increases false negatives on a 630-scenario adversarial suite from 6.4% to 15.7% (≈2.5× worse), while still achieving ~93% accuracy on obfuscated payloads when enabled.

Adding an LLM “second-opinion” judge sharply trades off safety and usability: it cuts false negatives from 5.4% to 0.8% but raises false positives from 2.3% to 9.0% and adds ~1.6s latency on judged actions, implying LLM escalation should be targeted to ambiguous cases rather than applied universally.

nodes.title: EvoPoC: Automated Exploit Synthesis for DeFi Smart Contracts via Hierarchical Knowledge Graphs
nodes.one_liner: Turning exploit synthesis into grounded reasoning over protocol semantics—with formal reachability and profit checks—shifts LLMs from plausible code to executable, economically valid DeFi PoCs at near-human effectiveness.
points: A hierarchical knowledge-graph memory plus SMT reachability checks and asset-level profit simulation enables end-to-end DeFi exploit PoC generation with a 96.6% exploit success rate on 88 historical attacks, reproducing 85 exploits and recovering $116.2M in simulated revenue.

On 72 audited projects (2,573 contracts), the system achieves 98% recall and 0.90 F1 for vulnerability detection, indicating that structuring protocol semantics and failure modes materially reduces missed bugs compared with prompt-only LLM auditing.

Against prior art, exploit success is up to 5× higher than state-of-the-art fuzzers and about 2× higher than an LLM exploit generator, while yielding up to 300× more recoverable value, showing that validation of both executability and economics is the main lever for practical exploit synthesis.

nodes.title: Safety Anchor: Defending Harmful Fine-tuning via Geometric Bottlenecks
affiliations: Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: A small, cached “safety anchor” at the unembedding bottleneck can outperform broad parameter/gradient/drift constraints by removing the attacker’s ability to hide in redundant internal dimensions.
points: Existing parameter-, gradient-, and representation-constrained defenses can be bypassed under persistent harmful fine-tuning because overparameterized LLMs exploit redundant degrees of freedom to move along largely orthogonal optimization trajectories while still satisfying the imposed constraints.

Safety Bottleneck Regularization (SBR) anchors the final-layer hidden state at the unembedding bottleneck to a cached safe reference on a small set of high-risk “safety anchor” prompts, achieving an average Harmful Score of 5.68 (vs 70.70 for standard SFT) while keeping average benign-task performance essentially unchanged (78.17 vs 78.07).

SBR is data-efficient and robust: a single anchor can drop HS from 67.8 to ~6, and across poison ratios up to 0.3 it keeps HS <10 on Llama3.1-8B (avg 6.35), Qwen2.5-7B (avg 6.10), and Gemma1.1-7B (avg 7.28), including backdoor-triggered settings where HS falls to 4.7 vs 65.68 for SFT.

nodes.title: DECKER: Domain-invariant Embedding for Cross-Keyboard Extraction and Recognition
nodes.one_liner: Domain-invariant audio modeling plus LLM post-processing makes passive, cross-device acoustic keystroke eavesdropping far more practical than per-key accuracy alone suggests.
points: Cross-keyboard domain shift is severe for standard acoustic encoders, with top-1 accuracy dropping ~37–41 points when moving from seen keyboards (~88.5–95.3%) to unseen keyboards (~47.9–58.1%).

DECKER substantially improves cross-keyboard keystroke inference to 81.3% top-1 on unseen keyboards (vs 58.1% ECAPA-TDNN and 66.2% best multimodal fusion), and ablations show Keyboard Signature Normalization is the biggest contributor (unseen 63.7% w/o KSN vs 81.3% full).

Constrained LLM decoding turns noisy key predictions into much higher sequence recovery, raising sentence exact-match from 42.6% (raw) to 72.8% (GPT-4) and showing largest gains on human-like passwords (+8–12% exact-match) while offering little benefit on high-entropy random strings (≤2–3%).

nodes.title: You Snooze, You Lose: Automatic Safety Alignment Restoration through Neural Weight Translation
nodes.one_liner: Safety alignment can be reinstated post-hoc by translating LoRA weights directly—non-linearly and layer-aware—avoiding the usual utility–alignment tax and any need for target-domain data.
points: A non-linear weight-space translator for LoRA adapters (NeWTral-MoE) restores safety zero-shot at deployment time, cutting average Attack Success Rate from ~70% (unsafe experts) to ~13% while preserving ~90% knowledge fidelity across 4 model families and scales up to 72B.

Against strong linear baselines, SafeLoRA reduces unsafe behavior but still requires a per-target safe reference adapter (violating zero-resource constraints), while NeWTral’s pre-trained translator achieves the best overall safety–utility harmonic mean (“Final Score”) across all tested architectures.

Layerwise routing reveals safety control is heterogeneous across depth: the MoE consistently routes more aggressive translation into MLP blocks (primary drivers of behavioral safety) while keeping attention blocks comparatively untouched to preserve domain reasoning, enabling late-layer “decision zone” refusal spikes without broad utility collapse.

nodes.title: Dependency-Aware Privacy for Multi-turn Agents
nodes.one_liner: Stateful, dependency-aware DP for LLM agents flips the multi-turn asymmetry: more turns improve utility without compounding privacy loss.
points: RootGuard’s root-only caching makes multi-turn privacy invariant to adversary-chosen functions and query count (post-processing), while independent per-release noising provably degrades with each additional release and can be amplified by up to a function’s Lipschitz constant (reported >100× for common nonlinear medical formulas).

On 8 NHANES-based medical workflows at ε=0.1 and total budget B=(2k+1)ε, RootGuard cuts target error by 2.3–3.0× versus independent noising (e.g., 7.6–7.8% vs 17.1–20.3% wMAPE) and reduces clinical Risk Class Error by 2.2–3.3×, with gains increasing as the adversary forces more turns.

Under MAP reconstruction with matched per-root noise (εr=0.1), independent noising becomes 3.5× easier to reconstruct as queries grow (21.8%→6.2% wMAPE for 1→16 queries), whereas RootGuard’s reconstruction accuracy is invariant because repeated queries return the same cached outputs.

nodes.title: Shattering the Echo Chamber: Hidden Safeguards in Manuscripts Against the AI Takeover of Peer Review
nodes.one_liner: Peer-review integrity can be defended by embedding invisible, parser-visible PDF instructions that either force chatbot refusal or watermark outsourced reviews—without altering what humans see.
points: A committee-side PDF defense that exploits structural–visual decoupling can disrupt end-to-end LLM review outsourcing at scale, reaching up to 84% defense success across 17,844 trials over 7 commercial chatbot settings and 12 academic venues while remaining visually identical for humans (MSSIM = 1.00).

Intra-stream PDF injection (editing original page content streams rather than appending new ones) materially improves robustness because common sanitization strategies that nullify prior PDF prompt-injection baselines drive their defense success to 0%, whereas the strongest intra-stream mechanism achieves up to 76% (explicit refusal/warning) and 84% (implicit marker) average success.

Lexical diversification of hidden payloads is a measurable force multiplier, raising defense success by 18.7% (explicit) and 9.6% (implicit) versus non-mutated seeds, and enabling lightweight deployment with ~1 second average processing time per manuscript on a commodity PC.

nodes.title: AgenticVM: Agentic AI for Adaptive Software Vulnerability Management
nodes.one_liner: Specialized agents with strict tool/LLM boundaries can turn thousands of raw vulnerability alerts into a small, auditable high-risk queue while still filling in missing CVSS details at near-90% accuracy.
points: A modular multi-agent workflow (rules + BERT + LLM agents) compressed scanner noise into actionable queues with up to 97.9–98% alert reduction (e.g., 3,983 raw detections → 82 prioritized items), directly lowering triage load without dropping risk visibility.

A BERT-small CVSS v3.1 base-metric predictor inferred missing attributes from vulnerability text at 89.3% overall accuracy (83–94% across individual metrics), enabling earlier prioritization when authoritative scoring is incomplete or delayed.

Compared to a single-agent LLM baseline, the agent-decomposed design produced dramatically stronger queue reduction (81.3% vs 12.5% on Online Boutique; 97.9% vs 28.5% on Train-Ticket), indicating that deterministic validation/prioritization plus provenance-aware orchestration is more robust than monolithic prompting.

nodes.title: Tailored Prompts, Targeted Protection: Vulnerability-Specific LLM Analysis for Smart Contracts
nodes.one_liner: AST-derived, vulnerability-scoped context plus tailored prompts turns a general-purpose LLM into a competitive multi-class smart-contract vulnerability detector with strong recall and practical false-positive control.
points: A publicly released corpus of 31,165 professionally annotated smart-contract vulnerability instances spanning 2,874 projects, 73,000+ Solidity files, and 15 major blockchain platforms enables realistic evaluation beyond toy benchmarks.

Vulnerability-specific prompting paired with AST-filtered, function-centric context achieves 0.92 average positive recall and 0.87 average negative recall across 13 vulnerability categories, indicating high coverage with materially reduced false positives for auditor workflows.

Adding few-shot in-context examples with explanation-style chain-of-thought increases average accuracy from 0.85 to 0.91, showing that reasoning scaffolds can substitute for fine-tuning in targeted smart-contract audits when context is tightly curated.

nodes.title: Root-Cause-Driven Automated Vulnerability Repair
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Automated vulnerability repair has hit an evaluation ceiling: oracle-passing rates look equal while human experts still see a statistically significant root-cause quality gap.
points: A root-cause-driven patching agent that combines diversified dynamic fault localization (AFL++ crash-variant tracing) with evidence-weighted function ranking achieves an 85.4% plausible-patch rate on 178 real-world C/C++ vulnerabilities, beating a specialized static-localization baseline (71.9%) while avoiding symbolic-execution approaches that effectively fail to scale (2.2% plausible).

Replay-based automated oracles (PoC replay + test suite + 12h fuzzed-variant suppression) cannot reliably distinguish patch quality at the frontier, as a general-purpose coding agent and the root-cause-focused agent are statistically indistinguishable on plausible-patch rate (~86% vs ~85%) yet differ materially in whether patches fix causes versus symptoms.

Blinded expert review on the 144 bugs where both tools produced plausible patches prefers the root-cause-focused patches in 47% of cases vs 27% for the general-purpose agent (26% ties; sign test p=0.0065), implying that evaluation and deployment pipelines should include root-cause-sensitive checks to reduce “oracle-passing but incomplete” security repairs.

nodes.title: VulKey: Automated Vulnerability Repair Guided by Domain-Specific Repair Patterns
nodes.one_liner: Compact, learnable repair-pattern guidance (action + semantic key element) materially outperforms both label-prefix “knowledge” and exemplar-based prompting for real-world vulnerability patching.
points: A two-stage pattern-matching + pattern-guided generation pipeline reaches 31.5% exact-match repair accuracy on PrimeVul C/C++, a +7.6 point absolute gain over the strongest fine-tuned baseline (23.9%) and roughly 3× better than GPT-5 (10.6%).

Naïvely injecting domain knowledge can backfire: adding only a CWE-type prefix slightly reduces accuracy (23.9%→23.6%), and few-shot prompting with random real patches halves performance (23.9%→11.9%), showing that unstructured or noisy guidance harms AVR.

Hierarchical repair patterns improve breadth and transfer, raising semantic-equivalence success from 36.5% to 43.2% on PrimeVul and doubling Vul4J Java repairs versus prior SOTA (18 vs 9), while also boosting multiple CWE categories including +40 points for double-free (20%→60%).

nodes.title: When RAG Chatbots Expose Their Backend: An Anonymized Case Study of Privacy and Security Risks in Patient-Facing Medical AI
nodes.one_liner: A patient-facing medical RAG chatbot leaked its system prompt, full knowledge base, and the last 1,000 chats through ordinary browser devtools—demonstrating that deployment security, not model guardrails, is the dominant failure mode.
points: Unauthenticated browser-level inspection exposed the chatbot’s full RAG internals—system instructions, model/embedding choices, retrieval and chunking parameters, backend endpoint structure, and machine-readable API schema—indicating a broken client–server trust boundary rather than an LLM prompt-injection failure.

An open endpoint returned up to the 1,000 most recent patient–chatbot conversation records (full user query text, bot responses, timestamps, and tags) with no login, directly contradicting public privacy assurances and creating a high-likelihood health-data privacy incident risk.

The entire knowledge base (8 documents) was enumerable and reconstructible via leaked document metadata and chunk text (plus similarity scores and embeddings), showing that RAG deployments can leak both user data and proprietary/clinical content even when the visible chat output appears “safe.”

nodes.title: AFL-ICP: Enhancing Industrial Control Protocol Reliability via Specification-Guided Fuzzing
affiliations: Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Turning multimodal ICP specifications into executable grammars plus a spec-conformance oracle lets fuzzing move beyond “crash-only” into reliably catching silent protocol deviations that can disrupt industrial operations.
points: Specification-guided fuzzing improved protocol state coverage by 46.15% over AFLNet and 53.29% over ChatAFL, showing that machine-executable grammars derived from standards materially increase deep-state reach in complex industrial control protocols.

Across four widely used ICPs, the framework uncovered 24 previously unknown vulnerabilities—including 16 semantic/logic flaws that do not crash targets—demonstrating that conformance-oracle checking can expose availability- and correctness-impacting deviations missed by crash-only fuzzing.

Crash discovery also increased substantially, finding 87 unique crash-inducing sequences (+77.6% vs AFLNet and +129.0% vs ChatAFL) and isolating 8 distinct memory-safety bugs, indicating that better state/transition navigation translates into more exploitable low-level defects as well.

nodes.title: CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers
nodes.one_liner: The interesting twist is treating the SOC’s constraint as “reasoning capacity” and proposing federated sharing of signed agent skills/playbooks—rather than data—as the lever for scalable, audit-ready defense in regulated finance.
points: Financial-sector SOCs face a reasoning bottleneck where SIEMs reportedly cover only ~21% of MITRE ATT&CK techniques and ~two thirds of SOC teams cannot keep pace with alert volumes, implying that many preventable intrusions persist due to uninvestigated alerts rather than missing telemetry.

Isolated LLM cyber wins are already strong (e.g., 87% autonomous one-day CVE exploitation and 4.3× multi-agent gains on zero-day exploitation), but they do not meet financial-institution requirements for composable workflows, persistent multi-tenant state, audit-grade evidence, and regulation-mapped outputs.

A hybrid, on-prem, multi-agent architecture with SIEM/XDR grounding, partitioned RAG, bounded HITL gates, and append-only audit logs is positioned as the practical path to meet DORA/NIS2/EU AI Act obligations while enabling privacy-preserving federation that shares agent skills/playbooks instead of raw customer data.

nodes.title: Pen-Strategist: A Reasoning Framework for Penetration Testing Strategy Formation and Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Separating pentest planning (RL-trained strategy reasoning) from execution control (supervised step/tool classification) yields large, measurable gains in both autonomy and reliability—without relying on external LLM APIs.
points: A GRPO-trained Qwen-3-14B strategist model raised strategy-derivation similarity from 0.39 to 0.73 (≈87% relative gain), outperforming the strongest evaluated commercial baseline (0.65) while remaining locally deployable for sensitive pentest data.

A separate step-and-tool selector (frozen GPT-2 embeddings + dual-head CNN) reached 82.87% next-step accuracy and 48.88% exact MCP-toolset accuracy, beating commercial LLMs by up to 28% on step prediction and eliminating tool-mismatch execution errors in ablations.

When plugged into existing automated pentesting agents, improved planning translated into materially higher execution success, lifting HTB subtask completion by ~43–53% depending on framework and boosting CTF performance from 72.38% to 85.71% on CTFKnow and from 5 to 17 solves on PicoCTF (beating GPT-5’s 13).

nodes.title: SkCC: Portable and Secure Skill Compilation for Cross-Framework LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Treating agent skills like compilable artifacts—complete with an IR, analyzers, and backends—simultaneously tackles portability, performance variance from prompt formats, and supply-chain style skill vulnerabilities.
points: A strongly-typed intermediate representation (SkIR) plus per-platform emitters reduces cross-framework skill adaptation work from O(m×n) manual rewrites to O(m+n), enabling “write once, run anywhere” skills across Claude, Codex, Gemini, and Kimi.

Platform-specific compilation materially improves agent task success on format-sensitive systems, raising Pass@1 from 21.1%→33.3% on Claude Code (+12.2pp, p≈0.01) and 35.1%→48.7% on Kimi CLI (+13.5pp, p<0.01), while showing smaller gains on Codex (+3.8pp) and near-neutral results on Gemini (22.2%→22.2%).

Compile-time safety hardening is largely automatable: Anti-Skill Injection triggers on 94.8% of 233 community skills (HTTP-safety in 91.4%), intercepting malformed or risky skills before deployment and delivering 10–46% runtime token savings with sub-10ms average compilation latency.

nodes.title: An Evaluation of Chat Safety Moderations in Roblox
nodes.one_liner: Message-by-message filtering is structurally mismatched to adversaries who can distribute intent across turns and rapidly adapt obfuscations after seeing moderation feedback.
points: A non-intrusive OCR pipeline recovered 91.76% of ground-truth chat lines at 97.44% average matched similarity, enabling large-scale auditing where no text API exists.

Across 2,040,839 moderated-on-screen Roblox chat messages from 105,214 users, model-assisted filtering flagged 7,254 unsafe conversations, with manual review showing high false-positive pressure (322 true positives vs 118 false positives) and implying substantial residual harm even after moderation.

Conversation-level harms—especially grooming, sexual content, harassment, threats/violence, self-harm, and PII/off-platform pivoting—frequently passed message-level filters, while repeat offenders iteratively evaded detection via multi-line fragmentation, spelling/leet/codeword obfuscation, and probing that exploits turn-by-turn context gaps.

nodes.title: Stateful Agent Backdoor
nodes.one_liner: Persistent state turns agent backdoors from one-shot triggers into APT-like, cross-session workflows that can bypass session-level tool isolation with high reliability.
points: A single trigger injection can persist in an agent’s cross-session storage and drive a multi-phase, autonomous backdoor that executes across permission-isolated sessions, achieving 80%–95% end-to-end exfiltration success on four instruction-tuned open-weight models.

Modeling the backdoor as a Mealy machine enables per-transition backdoor “stitching,” where each phase can be trained independently yet composes reliably in execution, with ≥90% per-transition success and episode success closely matching the product of per-transition rates (within about ±3 percentage points).

False-positive exfiltration remains low (0%–5%) even when intermediate false positives like unintended data reads occur (up to 25%–35% in some settings), implying that multi-stage gating plus state consistency checks can significantly attenuate accidental full attack completion while still permitting high targeted ASR.

nodes.title: QASecClaw: A Multi-Agent LLM Approach for False Positive Reduction in Static Application Security Testing
affiliations: Department of Information and Communication Engineering, University of Rajshahi
Department of Computer Science and Engineering, University of Rajshahi
nodes.one_liner: Using an LLM as a fail-open, post-SAST reviewer (not a primary detector) can eliminate most false alarms while keeping recall nearly intact, but policy-heavy CWEs like trust-boundary violations remain a key failure mode.
points: A multi-agent LLM verifier layered on top of Semgrep boosts overall F1 from 78.39% to 90.93% on OWASP Benchmark v1.2 (2,740 Java cases) by raising precision from 69.5% to 95.1% while only slightly reducing recall from 90.0% to 87.1%.

False positives drop 88.6% (560→64) and false-positive rate falls from 42.3% to 4.8%, showing that LLM-based contextual triage can convert noisy SAST output into high-signal alerts without materially sacrificing vulnerability coverage.

Per-CWE results indicate the biggest gains are on injection classes (e.g., XPath Injection F1 66.7%→93.3%, SQLi 72.8%→94.1%, XSS 72.7%→89.6%), but trust-boundary violations degrade (CWE-501 F1 76.8%→59.3%) due to substantial true-positive suppression, implying CWE-specific prompting/guardrails are necessary.

nodes.title: Patch2Vuln: Agentic Reconstruction of Vulnerabilities from Linux Distribution Binary Patches
nodes.one_liner: Binary patch diffs can be turned into actionable vulnerability narratives by an agent, but the decisive limit is whether the right changed function is surfaced and behaviorally reachable under safe local tests.
points: An offline tool-using language-model agent working only from local binary-derived evidence localized a verified security-relevant patched function in 10/20 (50%) Ubuntu security-update .deb pairs and produced an accepted root-cause class in 11/20 (55%), while correctly labeling all 5/5 negative controls as “unknown.”

Most failures were pre-model evidence bottlenecks: 6/20 (30%) security pairs failed because the binary differ/ranker never surfaced the true function and 1/20 (5%) failed due to context-export omission, leaving only 3/20 (15%) attributable to model reasoning or validation after the right function reached the prompt.

Bounded local old/new validation produced 2 minimized behavioral differentials (both tcpdump) but 0 crashes/timeouts/sanitizer or memory-corruption proofs across all 25 pairs, implying near-term gains hinge more on binary-diff coverage and reachability-aware validation than on stronger language modeling alone.

nodes.title: Enhancing Agent Safety Judgment: Controlled Benchmark Rewriting and Analogical Reasoning for Deceptive Out-of-Distribution Scenarios
nodes.one_liner: Removing explicit risk cues exposes large, systematic safety-judgment failures in agent trajectories, and retrieval of “how-to-reason” analogs can recover much of the lost robustness without updating model weights.
points: ROME rewrites 100 unsafe agent trajectories into 300 label-preserving deceptive cases across contextual ambiguity, implicit risks, and shortcut decision-making, with strong human–panel agreement (Cohen’s κ=0.85) and high human inter-rater reliability (Fleiss’ κ=0.81) supporting benchmark fidelity.

Deceptive rewrites sharply reduce zero-shot safety-judgment performance, with implicit-risk masking via technical/euphemistic framing emerging as the most persistent blind spot (e.g., GPT-4o F1 drops to 31.46% and DeepSeek-V3 to 27.03% on IR).

ARISE, a retrieval-guided analogical reasoning add-on (top-k=3 from a 2,000-trajectory base), materially improves robustness without retraining (e.g., IR F1: GPT-4o 31.46%→67.88%, DeepSeek-V3 27.03%→76.09%) and can simultaneously reduce over-refusal (GPT-4o SDM false-positive rate 50%→9%) at the cost of substantial prompt inflation (~6.38× characters).

nodes.title: RefusalGuard: Geometry-Preserving Fine-Tuning for Safety in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Preserving the internal “refusal geometry” during adaptation can prevent jailbreak collapse even when fine-tuning includes a tiny amount of harmful supervision.
points: Fine-tuning a safety-aligned LLM on just 10 harmful examples collapses refusal robustness, driving attack success rates (ASR) to ~0.69–0.75 and dropping refusal rates to ~0.25–0.33 across AdvBench, DirectHarm4, and JailbreakBench for Gemma-2-9B and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct.

A geometry-preserving representation fine-tuning constraint (REFUSALGUARD) keeps post-adaptation ASR near zero (≈0.0085–0.03) with refusal ≈0.97–0.99 across multiple model families (LLaMA, Gemma, Qwen), outperforming representation-level baselines like Circuit Breaker (ASR ≈0.215–0.300) and REPBEND (ASR ≈0.105–0.175).

Safety degradation tracks measurable internal failure modes—refusal-direction alignment falls (e.g., 0.91→0.63), cone drift and task–safety gradient interference rise (e.g., drift 0.00→0.31, interference 0.12→0.46), and refusal representations become more brittle (top-1 cone-coordinate mass 0.31→0.53), implying that preserving refusal geometry directly targets the mechanism of jailbreak susceptibility rather than relying on more data or heuristics.

nodes.title: Probe-Geometry Alignment: Erasing the Cross-Sequence Memorization Signature Below Chance
nodes.one_liner: A single scalar alignment per depth can invert and suppress memorization probes below chance, turning representational unlearning into a lightweight, auditable, and capability-preserving intervention rather than a blunt behavioral suppression.
points: A leave-one-out cross-sequence linear probe reveals a consistent deep-layer memorization signature in pretrained models—showing memorization-specific accuracy gaps of +0.32 (Pythia-70M), +0.19 (GPT-2 Medium), and +0.30 (Mistral-7B) over a pure-distinguishability baseline—while a same-architecture random-init control collapses to −0.04 at the deepest layer where the pretrained signature peaks.

The probe-read direction is causally separable from behavioral recall: projecting it out in Pythia-70M flips the local signature from +0.44 to −0.19 yet changes memorized log-probability by only 0.04–0.19 nats/token, indicating internal probe-detectable retention can be removed without materially affecting generation behavior.

Probe-Geometry Alignment (PGA) erases the cross-sequence signature below chance across scales with only rank-one-per-depth interventions (e.g., Pythia-70M L6: 0.11±0.04 over 4 seeds; GPT-2 Medium: 0.06 via MD-PGA k=2; Mistral-7B: 0.42) and remains robust to probe variants and re-fit attackers while preserving zero-shot capability (max |Δacc|=2.9 pp; mean Δacc=+0.2 pp).

nodes.title: Privacy Without Losing Place: A Paradigm for Private Retrieval in Spatial RAGs
affiliations: The City University of New York
nodes.one_liner: Structured, DP-sampled public anchors plus coarse direction/distance bins can deliver ~400 m location privacy in Spatial RAGs without collapsing generation quality, but discretization induces a surprising non-monotonic privacy–utility curve.
points: PAS replaces raw GPS coordinates with an anchor–direction–distance token and achieves neighborhood-level location hiding with ~367–400 m expected adversarial localization error at ε=1.0 while keeping the true location client-side.

Across six retriever–generator configurations, PAS reduces top-5 retrieval quality from Recall@5 ≈0.85–0.87 to ≈0.45–0.58 and nDCG@5 from ≈0.78–0.81 to ≈0.43–0.53, yet typically retains ≥55–60% of baseline retrieval signal under strong obfuscation.

Downstream answer quality degrades far less than retrieval—F1 is roughly stable (sometimes slightly higher) while citation correctness drops from ≈0.74–0.80 to ≈0.51–0.66—implying LLMs can partially compensate for noisier spatial retrieval but grounding reliability becomes the primary failure mode.

nodes.title: From Parameter Dynamics to Risk Scoring : Quantifying Sample-Level Safety Degradation in LLM Fine-tuning
nodes.one_liner: Tracking *directional* parameter dynamics (not just pre/post deltas) turns benign fine-tuning fragility into a measurable, transferable, sample-level risk signal.
points: Benign instruction fine-tuning consistently drives cumulative parameter drift in danger-aligned directions while staying near-zero (or slightly negative) on the safety-aligned direction, tightly tracking a large Safety Score collapse (e.g., ~5.0 to <1.0 on Qwen3-8B).

Safety degradation follows a two-phase dynamic where early training accumulates substantial danger-direction drift with only mild safety loss, followed by a sharp safety collapse after leaving a local “safe basin,” implying that monitoring directional drift can provide an early warning signal before refusal behavior catastrophically fails.

A continuous per-sample risk score (SQSD) based on the projection gap between sample-induced updates and danger vs safety directions yields monotonic risk-to-harm ordering in 10/12 settings, with extreme-subset separation averaging ~49.86 ASR points and transferring across architectures (Llama↔Qwen), scales (8B→14B/32B), and tuning methods (LoRA→full fine-tune).

nodes.title: Securing the Agent: Vendor-Neutral, Multitenant Enterprise Retrieval and Tool Use
nodes.one_liner: The striking result is that simple, server-enforced ABAC gating turns multitenant RAG from ~100% leakage to 0% leakage with near-zero overhead, but only if the vector backend can push down authorization filters to avoid catastrophic recall loss at scale.
points: Ungated multitenant retrieval leaks cross-tenant data in 98–100% of probes because relevance ranking ignores authorization, while ABAC-based retrieval gating reduces cross-tenant leakage (CTLR) and authorization violations (AVR) to 0% under the stated trust assumptions.

Server-side orchestration does not replace access control but makes it enforceable by moving the inference→tool→inference loop inside the server trust boundary, preventing compromised clients from bypassing gated retrieval or invoking unauthorized tools even when client-side frameworks control agent composition.

ABAC gating adds ~19 ms to the search path (dominated by ~14 ms auth round-trip) with no throughput degradation, whereas relying on post-retrieval filtering without predicate pushdown preserves security but can collapse Recall@5 from 1.0 to 0.002 at 50K chunks, making metadata-filter pushdown a practical requirement at scale.

nodes.title: Architectural Obsolescence of Unhardened Agentic-AI Runtimes
nodes.one_liner: A drop-in hardened agent runtime can turn F1–F4 detection from 0% to 100% because the decisive factor is architecture (audit + reconciliation + admission/egress/trust primitives), not model choice or configuration.
points: Across a 1,600-sample in-vivo CLI harness (plus an 80,000-sample stress test and a 10-LLM generalization run), upstream OpenClaw showed 0.000 recall for every F1–F4 divergence mode while enclawed-oss achieved precision=recall=F1=accuracy=1.000 on the same Discord/Telegram workflows, implying unhardened agent runtimes can provide effectively no structural safety coverage even when “working” in production paths.

The performance gap is attributed to missing runtime primitives rather than tunable settings: OpenClaw’s 14,419-file source walk found none of seven required structures (biconditional checker, hash-chained audit log, extension admission gate, two-layer egress guard, Bell-LaPadula policy lattice, module-signing trust root, bootstrap seal), indicating closing F1–F4 detection requires re-architecture rather than configuration hardening.

Hardened runtimes are measurably improvable under realistic traffic: a six-line append-only expansion of enclawed-oss’s DLP regex catalog increased F3 true-positive detection by 14.6% net with unchanged precision, whereas OpenClaw lacks a corresponding enforcement surface to implement the same incremental defense improvement.

nodes.title: DecodingTrust-Agent Platform (DTap): A Controllable and Interactive Red-Teaming Platform for AI Agents
nodes.one_liner: Realistic, controllable simulations plus autonomous multi-surface attack search reveal that agent security is often a harness-design problem as much as a model-alignment problem.
points: DTAP delivers a large-scale, reproducible agent red-teaming stack spanning 14 high-stakes domains and 50+ full-stack simulated environments, paired with DTAP-BENCH (6,682 tasks, 3,876 malicious goals) and verifiable, environment-state judges to prevent reward-hacking and false positives.

State-of-the-art agent frameworks remain broadly exploitable under policy-grounded attacks, with peak ASR reaching 55.7% in the indirect threat model (Google ADK) and 59.6% in the direct threat model (OpenClaw + DeepSeek-V4-Pro), while even the most robust system still exceeds 25% ASR.

Attack success concentrates in internalized and compositional surfaces—skill/tool injections and multi-vector chains outperform environment-only injections—and common “execute-then-refuse” harness behavior (batch tool calls) materially increases real-world risk by performing harmful actions before issuing refusals.

nodes.title: Dynamic Authorization for Knowledge-Base Agents in 6G
nodes.one_liner: Treating each predicate as a distinct trust boundary and enforcing access via logical inference makes zero-trust practical for multi-vendor 6G knowledge bases while sharply limiting lateral movement after agent compromise.
points: A hybrid authorization scheme combining RBAC with First-Order Logic enforces least-privilege at the knowledge-graph triple (subject–predicate–object) level, explicitly removing permission inheritance that enables overbroad access in role-only models.

Denying queries with variable predicates (e.g., ?p) blocks ontology/relationship “crawling,” turning wildcard predicate discovery into a default-access-denied event except within tightly scoped administrative ontologies.

Continuous, per-query inference checks integrated into the logical reasoner (PDP+PEP in the reasoning layer) enable dynamic session control, including immediate revocation by retracting an agent’s active authorization graph so subsequent requests fail by construction.

nodes.title: Redefining AI Red Teaming in the Agentic Era: From Weeks to Hours
nodes.one_liner: Red teaming shifts from library-centric scripting to an agentic, trace-instrumented workflow where operators specify intent and receive compliance-mapped, auditable findings at scale.
points: A natural-language, no-code red teaming interface backed by 45+ attacks, 450+ transforms, and 130+ scorers compresses operator workflow time from weeks to hours by automating attack selection, composition, execution, and reporting.

In a Llama Scout case study (68 goals, 674 attacks, 7,727 trials), the system achieved an ~85% attack success rate with 401 full jailbreaks (59.5%) and 232 Critical-severity findings (34.4%), indicating that high-impact failures remain common even under automated, repeatable testing.

Persona/social-engineering transforms were the most reliable bypass (skeleton-key framing and role-play wrapper reached 100% success), while base64 encoding was less effective (75%), implying defenses that focus on superficial obfuscation can still be systematically defeated by contextual framing and multi-turn escalation.

nodes.title: TRAP: Tail-aware Ranking Attack for World-Model Planning
nodes.one_liner: World-model agents can be backdoored at inference time by targeting the planner’s long-tail trajectory ranking rather than one-step predictions, yielding high-success, low-overhead planning hijacks.
points: A small universal visual patch (9% area, ℓ∞ budget ε=64) can reliably hijack world-model planning by perturbing the relative ranking of a few high-value imagined trajectories, reaching near-perfect Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 98.1% on DreamerV3 Crafter and 100% on multiple DMControl tasks.

Trajectory-ranking manipulation is substantially more effective than an untargeted random patch in settings where shallow perturbations are “washed out” by long-horizon imagination (e.g., DreamerV3 Crafter mean return drop shifts from −0.1% to 63.2% and ASR from 43.9% to 98.1%).

The attack transfers across distinct world-model paradigms and can collapse performance in online planning controllers (TD-MPC2) with mean return drops of 92.4–99.8% and 100% ASR across tested DMControl tasks, indicating a broadly shared ranking-based attack surface in model-based decision-making.

nodes.title: Agentic Vulnerability Reasoning on Windows COM Binaries
nodes.one_liner: Tool-augmented agents can move from binary-only race detection to MSRC-confirmed CVEs by tightly coupling COM metadata, decompilation navigation, and interactive debugger verification.
points: An end-to-end agentic pipeline for COM race bugs reaches 0.973 macro-F1 on 20 COM objects (40 vulnerability cases), beating same-model production coding agents by up to +0.208 F1 and outperforming a state-of-the-art static analyzer by 3.3× (0.973 vs 0.299).

Debugger-grounded PoC synthesis is the key differentiator: the strongest setup automatically produces verified crashing PoCs for 67.5% of cases (27/40), while production agents without COM inspection plus dynamic debugging verify essentially 0 cases even on frontier models.

In real Windows deployments, the system uncovered 28 previously unknown local-privilege-escalation vulnerabilities across 9 COM services (24 UAF, 4 DF), all confirmed by MSRC with 16 CVEs and $140,000 in bounties, indicating agentic binary analysis can translate directly into actionable 0-days at scale.

nodes.title: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI
nodes.one_liner: Deterministic message-flow integrity plus intent-level TBAC is positioned as a practical way to close the “OAuth sees scopes, not user intent” gap for tool-using agents—yet multi-turn conversations still cause double-digit over-authorization errors in realistic adversarial dialogues.
points: A zero-trust interception layer with five deterministic integrity checks (tool-definition integrity, unrequested execution, tool-swap detection, parameter integrity, and result fidelity) blocks structurally valid but malicious middleware behaviors such as tool poisoning, call substitution, argument tampering, and falsified tool outputs before they reach the LLM or downstream services.

Multi-turn semantic authorization remains materially harder than single-turn: on the Multi-Agent multi-turn benchmark, false-positive rates (over-scoping risk) rise to 10–20% depending on model (e.g., 0.10 Claude Sonnet 4.6 vs 0.20 Claude Opus 4.6), implying semantic-only TBAC is not yet safe as a standalone gate for high-stakes tools.

The two-stage CASA semantic pipeline (conversation task extraction → task-tool matching) can reach high single-turn accuracy (up to 0.98) and low over-scoping (down to 0.01 FPR in Multi-Agent ST with Claude Sonnet 4.6), but multi-turn context introduces both under-scoping (up to 0.07 FNR) and over-scoping that should trigger conservative fallbacks when intent confidence is low.

nodes.title: Partial Evidence Bench: Benchmarking Authorization-Limited Evidence in Agentic Systems
nodes.one_liner: It formalizes and measures the high-impact enterprise failure mode where an agent stays within permissions but still overclaims result completeness—turning governance-critical “silent incompleteness” into a reproducible metric suite.
points: A deterministic 72-task benchmark across due diligence, compliance audit, and security incident response isolates “authorization-limited evidence” where systems can respect ACLs yet still mislead users by presenting partial authorized evidence as a complete basis for action.

Checked-in baselines show silent filtering is maximally unsafe (unsafe_completeness_rate = 1.000 in all three scenario families), while fail-and-report eliminates unsafe completeness (0.000) and preserves perfect answer and gap-report scores (Answer = 1.000, Gap = 1.000) without reducing the task to blanket abstention.

Exploratory model runs show unsafe completeness is model- and scenario-dependent (e.g., gpt-4o unsafe rate 0.048 on due diligence vs 0.000 on compliance audit, and claude_haiku unsafe rate 0.286 on due diligence but 0.000 on compliance audit), indicating completeness awareness and structured gap reporting are distinct capabilities from local answer correctness.

nodes.title: Multi-Agent Strategic Games with LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Small, theory-motivated tweaks to a strategic environment (more players, known endpoint, public messaging) reliably flip LLM agents between cooperation and war—and their logged rationales track the expected game-theoretic mechanisms.
points: In a repeated security-dilemma game across 320 simulations and four LLMs, moving from two to three agents raised conflict termination from 65% to 81.3%, indicating that added actors sharply increase preemptive pressure and escalation risk even under identical payoff orderings.

Making the horizon common knowledge (exactly 10 periods) produced 100% conflict and a +35 percentage-point increase versus baseline in a model-fixed-effects estimate, with agents frequently articulating backward-induction logic—showing that endpoint knowledge alone can deterministically unravel cooperation.

Enabling cheap-talk communication cut conflict to 42.5% (−22.5 percentage points vs baseline) and shifted the remaining conflicts toward unilateral rather than simultaneous attacks (79.4% unilateral), implying messaging primarily suppresses mutual first-strike dynamics by supporting signaling, reciprocity, and informal rule-making.

nodes.title: WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
affiliations: Vietnam National University, Ho Chi Minh City
nodes.one_liner: The key surprise is that a small, fast multimodal guard can achieve near-perfect OOD recall and 0% real-agent ASR once it is trained on both guard-targeted injections and iteratively adapted attacks.
points: A compact parallel guard trained on a 177,585-sample multimodal dataset spanning 709 high-traffic URLs and 10 replicated high-risk platforms reaches ~99.3% accuracy/F1 on a held-out test set and 100% recall on multiple out-of-distribution web-agent prompt-injection benchmarks (Popup/EIA/VPI/WASP), indicating that breadth of domains + dual-modality supervision can close the typical generalization gap.

Explicit guard-targeted training (10,500 malicious + 10,500 benign PIG-augmented samples) eliminates catastrophic PIG failures seen in prior guards—raising recall from as low as 28–92% (and sometimes near 0% for baselines under PIG) to 100% across HTML-only, screenshot-only, and dual-modality PIG settings—showing that “attack-the-guard” needs to be modeled directly rather than treated as ordinary injection.

Adaptive co-evolution training (A3T) sharply reduces bypassability under iterative attackers, cutting sample success rate at a 10-try budget from ~21.9–28.8% (after PIG-only) to ~3.1–5.6% while keeping detection near-perfect, and in deployment drives prompt-injection attack success rate on VPI to 0% without adding latency because the guard remains faster than the agent (≈2.2–2.5s/step vs ≈3.6–12.0s/step for common agent backbones).

nodes.title: IPI-proxy: An Intercepting Proxy for Red-Teaming Web-Browsing AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
nodes.one_liner: A practical IPI red-teaming harness that targets the real retrieval surface (live whitelisted webpages) instead of the model API or mock pages.
points: An intercepting MITM proxy can red-team indirect prompt injection under strict domain whitelists by rewriting live HTTP responses in flight, avoiding the unrealistic assumption that agents can visit attacker-controlled URLs.

A unified, deduplicated library of 820 prompt-injection payloads aggregated from six benchmarks/datasets enables cross-source coverage and repeatable filtering/rotation by attack type, severity, and source for systematic evaluations.

Separating payload choice from embedding method (HTML comment, invisible CSS, or semantic prose) and from insertion location (6 HTML anchors) turns IPI testing into a parameter sweep that more closely matches real-world attacker degrees of freedom on trusted pages and widgets.

nodes.title: RUBEN: Rule-Based Explanations for Retrieval-Augmented LLM Systems
nodes.one_liner: Minimal rule explanations for RAG make prompt-injection and safety failures attributable to specific retrieved-source combinations rather than vague feature-importance scores or overwhelming sets of counterfactuals.
points: RUBEN mines minimal if-then rules over RAG document subsets to recover provenance patterns—identifying the smallest source combinations whose presence guarantees an output predicate holds across all supersets.

The system turns safety evaluation into a targeted search for consistent exploit triggers, surfacing when as few as two adversarially edited retrieved sources are jointly sufficient to bypass an LLM’s “no contact info” guardrail while stronger models may yield no valid exploit rules under the same setup.

A lattice-based, Apriori-style top-down traversal with dynamic-programming caching prunes invalid branches to cut expensive LLM calls, enabling interactive, real-time rule discovery and streaming of minimal rules in a web application.

nodes.title: Portable Agent Memory: A Protocol for Cryptographically-Verified Memory Transfer Across Heterogeneous AI Agents
nodes.one_liner: It turns agent memory into a transferable, cryptographically verifiable, least-privilege object that maintains continuity across LLM vendors while explicitly hardening the import path against memory-based prompt injection.
points: A portable, JSON-first agent-memory artifact spanning episodic/semantic/procedural/working/identity components preserved cross-model capability with Transfer Continuity Scores of 0.83–0.92 across Claude↔GPT-4↔Gemini versus 0.28–0.45 without memory (≈2.4× mean improvement).

Cryptographic provenance using a Merkle-DAG (BLAKE3 content addressing + Ed25519 root signing) achieved 100% tamper detection across 1,000 single-field mutations and 500 parent-reference manipulations, making memory corruption/poisoning auditable rather than silent.

An injection-resistant re-hydration pipeline (structural framing + escaping + schema/type enforcement) blocked or quarantined 200/200 memory-mediated prompt-injection attempts, including obfuscated patterns, yielding 0 successful instruction-path executions in evaluation.

nodes.title: No More, No Less: Task Alignment in Terminal Agents
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Terminal agents frequently finish the main job while silently doing extra, unrequested work, and today’s defenses tend to stop both the bad instructions and the crucial ones.
points: TAB introduces 89 underspecified terminal tasks where agents must use a necessary environmental cue while ignoring a co-located irrelevant distractor, operationalizing task alignment as the product of cue utilization (U) and distraction resistance (R).

Across ten frontier terminal agents, capability and alignment diverge sharply: GPT-5.5 leads on Terminal-Bench resolution (73%) and cue utilization (85%) yet achieves only 23% task alignment due to low distraction resistance (27%), while Claude Opus 4.7 reaches 72% task alignment driven by high distraction resistance (94%).

Six prompt-injection defenses generally cut distractor execution but often collapse task success by suppressing the very cues needed for completion (e.g., on GPT-5.4 mini, SIC reduces distractor execution from 0.65 to 0.03 while dropping TAB resolution from 0.36 to 0.01), implying selective relevance reasoning is required rather than blanket filtering.

nodes.title: When Child Inherits: Modeling and Exploiting Subagent Spawn in Multi-Agent Networks
nodes.one_liner: Subagent spawning turns “context sharing” into a lateral-movement primitive: inheritance, tool overprivilege, and weak lifecycle controls allow one foothold to scale into network-wide compromise.
points: Four framework-level flaws—unrestricted memory inheritance, missing role-to-tool access control, post-spawn memory divergence, and unauthorized sibling termination—let a single compromised agent propagate control and disrupt peers across a multi-agent hierarchy despite intended trust boundaries.

Proof-of-concept exploits on a stock OpenClaw deployment show practical impact ranging from stale verification/lost-update state errors to OS-level persistence by subagents (e.g., chmod+execute and autostart registration) because spawned agents retain an overly broad tool surface.

The same behaviors reproduced across five additional LLM vendors, implying that swapping models does not materially improve security because the primary attack surface is the orchestration layer (capability scoping, memory projection, and lifecycle governance) rather than model-specific inference quirks.

nodes.title: LLM Advertisement based on Neuron Auctions
nodes.one_liner: Instead of auctioning text slots or tokens, this work auctions controllable slices of the model’s internal computation (FFN neurons) to make generative ads both priceable and user-quality-aware.
points: Brand steering via amplifying top‑k FFN “brand neurons” produces a largely monotonic increase in brand mentions/CTR proxies with weak cross-brand interference, supporting the use of neuron-count budgets as a controllable, continuous ad-intensity knob.

Brand-associated FFN activations are reported to lie in approximately orthogonal subspaces, enabling near-independent multi-advertiser interventions that avoid rigid text-slot insertion while preserving fluent autoregressive generation under a unified norm-rescaling hook.

A differentiable menu-based pricing mechanism with a user-utility penalty (weighted by w_user) tunes the monetization–quality trade-off, with higher w_user consistently improving response quality metrics while reducing platform revenue and bidder surplus in both 2- and 3-bidder settings.

nodes.title: CALYREX: Cross-Attention LaYeR EXtended Transformers for System Prompt Anchoring
nodes.one_liner: A small, frozen-backbone architectural change—late-layer cross-attention that can only read the system prompt—measurably boosts multi-turn rule adherence and cuts many-shot jailbreak success without paying the usual fine-tuning “reasoning tax.”
points: Adding zero-initialized system-prompt→full-input cross-attention blocks to a frozen decoder improves instruction adherence at 8B with +7.4% IFEval and +16.3% multi-turn SysBench versus a parameter-matched late-layer MLP adapter, indicating architectural routing can outperform generic late-stage adaptation for following system rules.
