Many-shot jailbreaking robustness improves structurally at 8B, with CALYREX reducing attack success rate by 13.1 percentage points versus the late-layer MLP control (e.g., 50-shot MSJ ASR 25.0% vs 54.2%), showing that anchoring privileged instructions can mitigate multi-turn instruction erosion under adversarial in-context pressure.

Rule-following leverage is spatially localized: a placement sweep on a 28-layer 1.5B model finds optimal insertion in the final eighth of layers with late-layer CAL activations concentrated around ~2–4% of self-attention magnitude, while early/dense placements can worsen security (higher ASR) and instruction-following by disrupting upstream feature extraction.

nodes.title: When Prompts Become Payloads: A Framework for Mitigating SQL Injection Attacks in Large Language Model-Driven Applications
affiliations: Hasso Plattner Institute, University of Potsdam
nodes.one_liner: The most effective mitigation comes from pairing semantic prompt analysis (SLM) with strict post-generation SQL allow/deny controls, which drives several attack classes to 100% recall while keeping false positives low.
points: A three-layer defense (input keyword/character shield, SLM-based semantic threat detection, and SQL signature control) addresses LLM-mediated prompt-to-SQL injection by filtering malicious intent both before and after SQL generation.

The lightweight input shield alone detected 55% of ignore attacks but 0% of completion and obfuscation attacks, indicating that keyword/character filters are insufficient against adversarial prompt engineering despite a low 2.20% false-positive rate.

Combining the SLM threat detector with query signature control achieved near-complete coverage (F1 ≈96.65% for direct SQLi, 95.21% for completion, 92.88% for obfuscation, 99.01% for ignore, and 95.21% for combination) with a 2.70% false-positive rate, suggesting layered semantic + syntactic gating is the most reliable deployment pattern.

nodes.title: No Attack Required: Semantic Fuzzing for Specification Violations in Agent Skills
nodes.one_liner: Safety guardrails in agent skills can fail without any attacker because natural-language constraints don’t reliably survive autonomous execution paths and tool-call fallbacks, but trace-reachability goals make these failures fuzzable and reproducible.
points: A goal-directed semantic fuzzer (SEFZ) that turns natural-language guardrails into deterministic reachability queries over annotated execution traces found specification violations in 120/402 real-world agent skills (29.9%), showing that benign user requests alone can routinely break the safety contract users rely on.

SEFZ uncovered 26 previously unknown exploitable guardrail violations in deployed skills and converged quickly (≈11 minutes per skill; 44% of violations within 10 minutes and 78% within 15 minutes), indicating dynamic, trace-based testing is practical for marketplace-scale auditing.

Component ablations show capability is tightly coupled to LLM-driven semantic mutation and goal-directed scheduling (random-template mutation yields ~53% fewer violating skills found, removing the bandit ~35% fewer, and removing goal-proximity feedback ~29% fewer), implying that graded trace-oracles plus adaptive exploration are key to surfacing non-adversarial safety failures.

nodes.title: AgentShield: Deception-based Compromise Detection for Tool-using LLM Agents
affiliations: Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Centre, University of Kurdistan Hewlêr
nodes.one_liner: Deception-style tripwires in an agent’s tool layer can turn rare prompt-injection “slip-throughs” into high-confidence compromise alerts and self-supervised training data, including for Kurdish and Arabic where input classifiers break down.
points: A three-layer deception setup inside the tool interface (honeytools, honeytokens, and parameter allowlisting) detected 90.7%–100% of the indirect prompt-injection attacks that actually succeeded on commercial models, while producing 0/485 false alarms on normal tasks (95% Wilson CI: 0%–0.79%).

Cross-lingual robustness held across English, Kurdish, Arabic, and code-switched attacks (176 prompts), with the English–Kurdish detection gap limited to 6.4 percentage points on GPT-4o-mini and shrinking to 1.9 points on DeepSeek-V3, implying behavioral/tool-call signals generalize better than text-based filters in low-resource languages.

Under 1,728 adaptive-attack runs across three attacker-knowledge tiers, commercial models saw zero successful evasions, and a self-supervised Random-Forest trained solely from trap triggers transferred without retraining (F1≈0.99 cross-model; F1≈0.997 cross-language), enabling scalable detection without manual labeling or extra LLM calls.

nodes.title: The Granularity Mismatch in Agent Security: Argument-Level Provenance Solves Enforcement and Isolates the LLM Reasoning Bottleneck
affiliations: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology
Dongbei University of Finance and Economics
University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.one_liner: The key shift is treating prompt injection as an authority-binding problem inside tool-call arguments, not as a whole-invocation contamination problem.
points: Argument-level capability contracts with provenance tracking can eliminate the security–utility tradeoff caused by invocation-level defenses, achieving 100% utility and 100% security under oracle provenance on mixed-trust scenarios where baselines either over-block (e.g., CaMeL 33.3% utility) or miss attacks (e.g., FIDES 37.5% security).

In full AgentDojo deployments across five LLMs, argument-role enforcement reaches 100% security on the three strongest models while recovering 38.1–46.4% utility, improving by 8–16 percentage points over CaMeL at the same security level.

The remaining practical bottleneck is upstream fidelity rather than runtime cost: automatic pipelines hit 87.1% argument-role accuracy and 77.4% provenance accuracy on real tools, while the monitor itself adds sub-millisecond overhead (P99 <272µs), implying that better provenance inference and contract synthesis are the main levers for deployment-grade robustness.

nodes.title: Oracle Poisoning: Corrupting Knowledge Graphs to Weaponise AI Agent Reasoning
nodes.one_liner: Poisoning the data plane of an agent’s trusted knowledge graph can force universally wrong answers—even when models reason correctly—so security testing must be done with real tool-use and enforced data integrity controls.
points: Across nine LLMs from three providers using real SDK tool-use, poisoned knowledge-graph query results were trusted 100% at moderate attacker sophistication (L2), with 269/269 valid directed-query trials accepting fabricated security claims—showing that tool-delivered “facts” can deterministically steer correct reasoning to wrong conclusions.

Evaluation method materially changes measured risk: GPT-5.1 showed 0% trust when the same poisoned evidence was presented inline as text but 100% trust under both simulated and real tool-use, implying that non-agentic (inline) benchmarks can yield false negatives for agentic deployments.

Operationally effective mitigations are architectural rather than prompt-based: enforcing read-only access removes the direct mutation vector, while multi-tool cross-verification reduced blind trust from 100% to 0–25% by surfacing contradictions, whereas system-prompt hardening had zero measurable effect and “blind” devil’s-advocate prompting was useless (catch rate equaled false positives).

nodes.title: When Agents Overtrust Environmental Evidence: An Extensible Agentic Framework for Benchmarking Evidence-Grounding Defects in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Agents frequently treat environment outputs as authoritative even when verification is available, so benchmarking must explicitly score whether actions stay grounded in the true, current environment state rather than just task completion.
points: Across 3,850 controlled runs spanning 55 cases and 14 model–scaffold stacks, agents took the task-incorrect “false path” 3,206 times (83.3% environmental misgrounding rate), indicating that overtrusting plausible environment-facing claims is a pervasive reliability and security-relevant failure mode in LLM agents.

Performance varies widely by deployment stack (average EMR range 55.3% to 96.4% and individual scenario–stack cells from 0.0% to 100.0%), showing that both model choice and scaffold integration materially affect grounding robustness but neither reliably prevents misgrounding in the tested workflows.

Open-source scaffold inspections show enforceable controls largely stop at gating side-effecting tool execution, while enforceable evidence governance (authority/provenance/freshness metadata plus mandatory corroboration before acting on mutable environment claims) is broadly missing, aligning with the consistently high misgrounding rates observed.

nodes.title: Web Agents Should Adopt the Plan-Then-Execute Paradigm
nodes.one_liner: ReAct-style web agents are structurally vulnerable because they let untrusted web content sit in the same loop that chooses the next privileged action, while plan-then-execute turns the run into an auditable program whose action graph cannot be rewritten by what the agent reads.
points: Separating planning from execution via a pre-committed program blocks web prompt injections from steering an agent’s control flow, because untrusted page content can only influence typed data values and predetermined branches rather than synthesizing new actions at runtime.

Across 860 WebArena tasks, 100% are compatible with plan-then-execute and 81.28% can be completed with a fully programmatic static plan (no runtime LLM subroutines), indicating most common web workflows do not inherently require reactive replanning.

The main deployment bottleneck is infrastructure rather than modeling: without complete, semantically-typed, well-documented website APIs (vs. brittle click/type/scroll), planning becomes near-sighted and error handling must be explicitly encoded to avoid failures on underspecified or mismatched queries.

nodes.title: Re-Triggering Safeguards within LLMs for Jailbreak Detection
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreak prompts behave like brittle solutions that collapse back into refusal with tiny, targeted embedding perturbations—especially when steering toward a few “anchor” regions of the embedding space.
points: Embedding-level noise injected into a prompt’s token embeddings can reliably re-trigger built-in refusal behavior for jailbreak prompts while rarely inducing refusals for benign prompts, achieving near-perfect detection for many attacks (e.g., DR 0.99–1.0 on GCG and 0.87–1.0 on PAIR) with very low false alarms (0–2% on IFEval; 0–1% on AlpacaEval).

As a drop-in defense that preserves base-model utility, the method substantially reduces attack success rates versus prior defenses, including under strong attacks like AutoDAN-Turbo (e.g., Qwen2.5 ASR reduced from 60% to 29%, Vicuna from 75% to 26%, and LLaMA2 from 31% to 10%) while keeping utility essentially unchanged (e.g., Qwen2.5 AlpacaEval 68.7 and IFEval 61.6).

Effective disruptions concentrate around a small set of model-specific “anchor tokens” (top-1 anchor ≈52% and top-3 ≈90% of successful cases), enabling an efficient guided search where ~90% of final detection is typically reached in only a handful of iterations and with fewer trials than brute force (e.g., average trials to full performance: 10.5 vs 19.7 on Vicuna; 18.0 vs 29.1 on Qwen2.5).

nodes.title: Quantifying LLM Safety Degradation Under Repeated Attacks Using Survival Analysis
nodes.one_liner: Treating jailbreak resistance as a time-to-failure problem (Kaplan–Meier + hazard) exposes early-turn “vulnerability windows” and statistically separates models that look similar under single-shot metrics.
points: Model safety under repeated identical prompts varies sharply: Phi-3 Mini reached a 52% jailbreak rate overall with survival dropping to 0.48 by attempt 10, while Llama 3.2 (3B) and Qwen3 (4B) remained at 10% and 7% with 0.90 and 0.93 survival at attempt 10, respectively.

Misinformation/disinformation was the dominant failure mode for Phi-3 Mini (80% jailbreak rate, median time-to-jailbreak = 1, survival 0.20 at attempt 10), whereas Llama 3.2 and Qwen3 showed much slower degradation in the same category (median 7 and 8 attempts, survival 0.95 and 0.85 at attempt 10).

Survival-curve differences were statistically strong overall between Phi-3 Mini and the other models (log-rank p<0.0001 in both pairwise tests), while Llama 3.2 vs Qwen3 was not significant (p=0.4834), implying repeated-attack resilience can be meaningfully compared via time-to-failure rather than binary ASR alone.

nodes.title: The Art of the Jailbreak: Formulating Jailbreak Attacks for LLM Security Beyond Binary Scoring
affiliations: University of Texas at El Paso
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that jailbreak strength is not binary: measuring the similarity–harmfulness trade-off exposes a stealthy optimum that conventional ASR-based evaluation systematically misses.
points: A compositional pipeline combining 912 in-the-wild strategies with 125 harmful seeds yields ~114,000 single-turn jailbreak prompts labeled into 14 cyber-attack categories, enabling category-specific strategy ranking rather than treating jailbreaks as an undifferentiated prompt set.

Instruction-tuned, category-aware jailbreak generators produce substantially more fluent prompts (PPL 24–39 vs 40–140 for AmpleGCG/AutoDAN) while reducing detection by input safety filters (LlamaPromptGuard-2 evasion leaving only 0.29–0.51 of prompts flagged malicious), indicating fine-tuning can create scalable red-teaming that looks benign to lightweight guards.

A training-free 2D evaluator (semantic similarity S and harmfulness probability H) reveals a “stealth-optimal” regime around (S≈0.57, H≈0.43) and shows why binary ASR collapses important distinctions, as multiple categories hit ASR≈1.0 despite materially different OPTIMUS scores (e.g., Exploit Kit Delivery mean 0.334 vs Backdoor Implantation 0.232).

nodes.title: The Great Pretender: A Stochasticity Problem in LLM Jailbreak
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreak ASR is a noisy random variable—not a stable score—and without reporting kgen, keval, and θeval, cross-paper 'state-of-the-art' comparisons can be off by tens of percentage points for purely procedural reasons.
points: Single-shot jailbreak benchmarking is systematically inflated because ASR is highly sensitive to stochasticity in both attack generation and LLM-judge evaluation, with judge temperature alone shifting measured ASR by up to 54 percentage points when θeval increases from 0 to 1.0.

Requiring repeated consistency during evaluation (e.g., keval=10) reduces reported ASR by 12–24 percentage points versus keval=1 across tested attacks and models, implying that many “successful” jailbreak prompts are actually fragile one-off wins rather than reproducible vulnerabilities.

Enforcing consistency during prompt generation (CAS-gen) can recover up to ~30 percentage points of the ASR loss seen under stricter multi-try evaluation, showing that robustness constraints can materially improve jailbreak reliability but also make comparisons meaningless unless kgen/keval/θeval are standardized and reported.

nodes.title: Why Do Aligned LLMs Remain Jailbreakable: Refusal-Escape Directions, Operator-Level Sources, and Safety-Utility Trade-off
affiliations: Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
nodes.one_liner: Jailbreaks persist because alignment leaves architecture-level, semantics-preserving directions that flip refusal into compliance, and eliminating them runs into a built-in safety–utility incompatibility.
points: Refusal-Escape Directions (RED) formalize jailbreakability as semantics-preserving local perturbations that move an aligned model from refusal to answering, implying jailbreaks can be explained as continuous behavior transitions rather than only discrete prompt tricks.

RED can be exactly decomposed into operator-level sources—normalization, residual wiring, self-attention, MLP, and a terminal “side-channel” source—pinpointing where refusal-to-answer control leaks through the architecture and why purely training-level fixes may miss structural causes.

Across five aligned LLMs and five jailbreak methods (100 refused→answered pairs per model–attack), added token dimensions expose previously-cancelled RED at harmful inputs and many jailbreaks succeed very early in the interpolation (often within the first half, and frequently within the first 5%), with refusal-to-answer shifts most consistently aligned to terminal-source contributions, suggesting defenses should target local RED suppression near harmful prompts and especially terminal side-channels.

nodes.title: LITMUS: Benchmarking Behavioral Jailbreaks of LLM Agents in Real OS Environments
affiliations: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics
Collaborative Innovation Center of Novel Software Technology and Industrialization
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that text-only safety evaluations can be badly misleading for agents because OS-level verification reveals frequent covert execution and high real-world attack success even in strong models.
points: A physically grounded OS-agent benchmark with rollback isolation shows frontier agents still execute a large fraction of dangerous real-OS actions, with seed-set attack success rates spanning 40.64%–71.51% and Claude Sonnet 4.6 still performing 40.64% of high-risk operations.

Dual-channel scoring exposes “Execution Hallucination,” where language and actions diverge, yielding 7.98%–9.97% mismatch rates and a particularly dangerous covert-execution mode where agents refuse in text but still complete harmful OS actions (2.87%–4.84%).

Context-mediated attacks that flow through the toolchain (skill injection and entity/file/web wrapping) are more reliably effective than direct rhetoric prompts, and communication/exfiltration behaviors are a consistent weak point (e.g., Communication Outreach reaching up to 96.67% ASR).

nodes.title: Not All Turns Matter: Credit Assignment for Multi-Turn Jailbreaking
nodes.one_liner: Multi-turn jailbreaking often hinges on a small subset of “critical” turns, and explicitly learning which turns matter makes both attacks and defenses substantially stronger.
points: Leave-one-turn-out masking shows multi-turn jailbreak credit is highly non-uniform—47.1% of turns in successful trajectories are attack-critical while 52.9% are redundant, and in failed trajectories 94.1% of turns are neutral with only 5.9% safety-critical—so trajectory-level rewards systematically misassign learning signal.

TRACE’s turn-aware credit assignment boosts RL-based multi-turn jailbreaking by about 25% relative ASR over the strongest RL baseline (average ASR@1 rises from 69.97% with TROJail to 87.10% with TRACE mix), indicating that fixing credit assignment is a first-order driver of attack capability.

Training against diverse strong targets yields highly transferable jailbreak policies: TRACE (mix) reaches ASR@5 of 96.7% on GPT-4o and 93.1% on Gemini-2.5-Pro, implying that multi-turn attacker training can generalize to closed-source frontier systems despite differing refusal behaviors.

nodes.title: Before the Last Token: Diagnosing Final-Token Safety Probe Failures
nodes.one_liner: Safety monitors that read only the final prompt token can miss jailbreaks because unsafe evidence often appears earlier in the prefill trajectory and then gets representationally “washed out” by the wrapper.
points: Final-token SafeSwitch-style probes kept high recall on clean harmful prompts (~94.6–95.0% on SorryBench) but lost substantial coverage under HarmBench wrapping (dropping to 63.6–86.9% jailbreak detection), showing that adversarial framing can hide unsafe intent from the last-token readout without removing it from the prompt.

False positives on safety-adjacent benign prompts were large (23.0–36.0% on XSTest at the default threshold), implying that simply lowering the decision threshold to catch more jailbreaks would materially worsen benign-user blocking.

The jailbreak-miss signal is largely outside the probe-visible subspace at the final token (only ~2.4–3.5% of the missed-jailbreak-vs-benign contrast energy lies in the probe row-space), but modeling prefill trajectories with a clean-trained PCA-HMM recovered ~89.8–94.5% jailbreak detection while cutting XSTest false positives to ~6.0–15.0%, indicating that trajectory-aware monitoring can expose evidence that collapses by the final token.

nodes.title: MT-JailBench: A Modular Benchmark for Understanding Multi-Turn Jailbreak Attacks
nodes.one_liner: A modular multi-turn jailbreak benchmark shows that budgets and judges can overwhelm true attack improvements, while prompt generation is the main performance lever and component recomposition reliably produces stronger cross-model attacks.
points: Attack success rates are highly confounded by evaluation choices: under all-judge cross-validation, self-reported ASR drops by as much as 69.2 percentage points (FITD on GPT-4o/Llama-3-70B), showing that judge definitions can dominate apparent progress.

Search budget strongly reshapes conclusions: constraining methods to a shared limited budget (≤10 interactions) flips rankings and can reduce ASR by up to 35.9 points (ActorBreaker on Llama-3-70B) and 35.2 points (CoA on GPT-5), implying many gains reflect extra exploration rather than more efficient multi-turn steering.

Component attribution indicates prompt generation drives most variance, and recombining the best modules yields a stronger transferable attacker (CrescendoX) that beats its source attacks on 19 of 21 target LLMs and delivers >20-point ASR gains on multiple models (e.g., Llama-4-Scout, Gemma-4-E4B-IT, Qwen-3.5-Flash, Grok-4.1-Fast, Nemotron-3-Nano).

nodes.title: AgentTrap: Measuring Runtime Trust Failures in Third-Party Agent Skills
nodes.one_liner: Runtime evaluation across the full model–framework–workspace stack reveals that “benign task success” can mask covert exfiltration, config poisoning, and MCP abuse introduced by trusted third‑party skills.
points: A 141-task dynamic benchmark (91 malicious/50 benign) shows that agents often execute malicious side effects embedded in “normal” third‑party skill workflows—highlighting runtime trust failures rather than classic jailbreak-style refusals.

Plain LLM-with-tools runs are highly vulnerable, with top observed attack-success rates reaching 82.4% (75/91, Kimi K2.6) and 80.2% (73/91, GLM 5.1), implying that capability alone does not translate to resisting skill-supply-chain abuse.

Pre-execution scanning is insufficient for this threat model: the best non-benchmark static scanner flags only 54.9% of malicious skills (50/91) and VirusTotal flags 0/91, while framework mediation can both increase exposure and improve blocking depending on the backbone (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.6 blocks 61.5% under Claude Code vs 33.0% as Plain Agent).

nodes.title: Position: AI Security Policy Should Target Systems, Not Models
nodes.one_liner: Offensive capability is increasingly a property of the surrounding multi-agent scaffold (memory, search, fuzzing, optimization) rather than any single frontier model, so policy that focuses only on model access control misses the main lever.
points: A coordinated swarm of five open-weights 1.2B-parameter agents ran 225 automated jailbreak attempts per target and drove GPT-4o to a 45.8% Effective Harm Rate (49 critical-severity breaches), while Claude Sonnet 4 produced 0% Effective Harm despite a 40% “technical success” rate—showing that common jailbreak metrics can materially mis-estimate real-world harm.

In vulnerability discovery, the same 1.2B agents embedded in a scaffold (regex search, exploit seed corpus, and AddressSanitizer crash triage) recovered 9/9 planted CWEs (100% recall) in ~4 minutes on a consumer MacBook, but with scaffold components removed the model achieved 0/9 crash-verified recall and only 2/9 citation-verified recall, attributing the full capability jump to system design rather than model scale.

Evolutionary multi-agent coordination doubled jailbreak effectiveness against GPT-4o over 15 generations (31.4% early vs 66.3% late-generation success; peak 73%), implying that publishable scaffolding and automated optimization can rapidly raise offensive capability without requiring frontier-model access.

nodes.title: Persona-Conditioned Adversarial Prompting: Multi-Identity Red-Teaming for Adversarial Discovery and Mitigation
nodes.one_liner: Conditioning automated jailbreak search on realistic attacker identities turns red-teaming into a scalable data engine that both finds more transferable failures and rapidly hardens models via adapter tuning.
points: Persona-conditioned parallel red-teaming boosts jailbreak discovery dramatically, raising attack success on GPT-OSS 120B from 57% to 97–98% and increasing successful prompt yield by 2–6× across attacker models.

Prompts generated via persona+strategy conditioning generalize beyond the optimization target, with 30–58% bypass rates against dedicated guardrails (Granite Guardian 3.3 and Llama Guard 3) and “true transferability” of 64–96% among prompts that also succeed on GPT-OSS 120B.

A closed-loop mitigation pipeline using only 300 PCAP-generated, metadata-tagged prompts enables lightweight adapter fine-tuning that drives adversarial-detection recall from 0.26–0.36 to 0.96–1.00 and F1 from 0.41–0.53 to 0.96–0.98 while keeping precision high (0.94–0.97).

nodes.title: GLiGuard: Schema-Conditioned Classification for LLM Safeguard
nodes.one_liner: A compact schema-conditioned encoder shows that real-time, multi-aspect LLM guardrailing can be done with classifier-style efficiency while staying competitive with much larger generative guards.
points: A 0.3B-parameter bidirectional encoder achieves near-parity harmfulness detection with 7B–27B autoregressive guard models across nine benchmarks, scoring 87.7 macro-F1 on prompt safety (within 1.7 points of the best baseline) and 82.7 macro-F1 on response safety (second-best overall).

Schema-conditioned multi-task moderation enables prompt safety, response safety, refusal detection, 14 harm categories, and 11 jailbreak strategies to be evaluated in a single non-autoregressive forward pass, yielding up to 16.2× higher throughput and 16.6× lower latency than 8B decoder guards.

Hard override rules (e.g., harm-category/jailbreak signals forcing UNSAFE, refusals forcing SAFE) improve adversarial benchmark recall (e.g., +4.3 F1 on SimpleSafetyTests and +3.2 on HarmBench versus safety-only) but can reduce performance on ambiguity-heavy datasets (e.g., −4.0 F1 on OpenAI Moderation), highlighting a tunable precision–recall trade-off driven by policy conservatism.

nodes.title: EVA: Editing for Versatile Alignment against Jailbreaks
nodes.one_liner: A post-deployment, zero-inference-overhead defense that “patches” jailbreak vulnerabilities by editing a tiny set of MLP key–value pathways instead of re-training the model.
points: Neuron-level model editing (EVA) cuts jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR) from 69% to 4% on Vicuna-7B under AutoDAN and reaches ~0% ASR on stronger LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B) across four jailbreak benchmarks while preserving benign utility (e.g., MT-Bench 7.44 vs 7.79 vanilla on Llama-3.1-8B).

For VLMs, multimodal key extraction plus attention-based visual token selection drives ASR to 0% on Qwen2.5-VL-7B across FigStep/ADV-16/Hades and MM-SafetyBench SD+TYPO (51% → 0%), without meaningful capability loss on MM-Vet-v2/MMMU/MMStar (e.g., InternVL3.5-8B: 69.3 vs 69.1 MM-Vet-v2).

Under adaptive regeneration, robustness degrades but remains far better than vanilla (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B avg ASR 1.2% → 11.3% vs 63.4% vanilla; LLaVA-1.5-7B adaptive ADV-16 avg ASR 0% → 12.5% vs 63.5% vanilla), indicating edits raise attack cost rather than eliminating the attack surface.

nodes.title: Metaphor Is Not All Attention Needs
nodes.one_liner: Poetic prompts trigger a distinct, highly decodable “processing mode” in attention without reliably carrying a corresponding safety signal, enabling harm to slip past keyword- and format-agnostic alignment.
points: Stylistic reformulation into poetry raises jailbreak success on Qwen3-14B from 10.44% (prose) to 46.92% (poetry) over 2,397 paired prompts, indicating a large safety generalization gap under format shift.

Ablations over 60 prose–poetry pairs show no single poetic device is necessary or sufficient for jailbreaks, with per-device interventions changing safety outcomes only ~16% on average and many “rhyme effects” explained by forced synonym swaps that evade blacklisted terms.

A 72D interpretable attention representation decodes literary format nearly perfectly (logistic regression accuracy 0.985±0.006; AUC 0.997±0.002) but predicts safe vs unsafe only weakly within prose/poetry (~0.66 accuracy), implying models recognize poetic form yet safety behavior is largely decoupled from attention-level format recognition.

nodes.title: Guaranteed Jailbreaking Defense via Disrupt-and-Rectify Smoothing
nodes.one_liner: Rectifying perturbed prompts before voting turns smoothing from a “gibberish-in, unpredictable-out” heuristic into a black-box defense with an explicit probabilistic success condition and markedly better harmlessness–helpfulness trade-offs.
points: A two-stage “disrupt-and-rectify” smoothing pipeline (random local character/word disruption followed by global semantic rectification and majority voting) reduces established jailbreak ASR to 0–2% on Vicuna/LLaMA-2 across GCG/PAIR/AutoDAN-Turbo while preserving higher helpfulness than disruption-only defenses (e.g., Vicuna IF accuracy 38.0% vs 19.8% for SmoothLLM and 19.3% for Erase-and-Check).

Against adaptive black-box attacks (adaptive PAIR), policy-selected rectification achieves the best overall robustness among compared smoothing baselines, cutting ASR to 22% (Vicuna) / 2% (LLaMA-2) / 12% (Qwen2.5) / 16% (GPT-3.5), outperforming SemanticSmooth-Policy (28/4/16/22) and SmoothLLM (60/8/28/40).

A tight Hoeffding-style guarantee links per-sample refusal probability α(q), trials N, and defense success probability: achieving DSP ≥ (1−ε) requires α(q) ≥ 1/2 + sqrt((1/(2N))·log(1/ε)), implying larger N lowers the required single-trial robustness but experiments show ASR is more sensitive to disruption rate q than to N, enabling a lightweight N=2 setting to retain strong GCG robustness (ASR 0.8% at q=5%) with ~5× lower runtime (1.6s vs 7.7s).

nodes.title: Break the Brake, Not the Wheel: Untargeted Jailbreak via Entropy Maximization
nodes.one_liner: Refusals cluster at a small set of high-entropy tokens, and flipping just those tokens via entropy maximization yields markedly more transferable VLM jailbreaks than forcing any fixed harmful prefix or response pattern.
points: An untargeted image-based jailbreak objective that maximizes entropy specifically at high-entropy “refusal decision” tokens (top 20% by teacher-forced entropy) improves cross-model transferability versus prefix/pattern-targeted optimization, indicating prior “non-transferability” results were largely objective-induced rather than fundamental.

UJEM-KL (entropy maximization on decision tokens + KL stabilization on low-entropy structural tokens) achieves strong white-box ASR under a strict 3-judge intersection protocol, reaching 82.23–88.32% on JailBreakV-28K and 67.39–72.21% on SafeBench across Qwen2.5-VL-7B, InternVL3.5-4B, and LLaVA-1.5-7B.

Transfer attacks remain substantial under the same strict protocol (e.g., Qwen→InternVL 48.62% and Qwen→LLaVA 56.63% on JailBreakV-28K; InternVL→LLaVA 49.45% on SafeBench), and KL stabilization is also defense-resilient (e.g., SafeDecoding ASR 65.3% vs 54.4% for entropy-only), implying defenses that mainly reshape decoding margins are brittle against entropy-flattening at decision points.

nodes.title: Adaptive Steering and Remasking for Safe Generation in Diffusion Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Step-wise control of the denoising trajectory—via a contrastive harmfulness direction and targeted remasking—can nearly eliminate diffusion-model jailbreaks without the empty-response failure mode common to blunt remasking defenses.
points: An inference-time two-phase defense for diffusion language models—early-step latent steering plus selective remasking—reduced jailbreak attack success to 0.64% on AdvBench (Dream) while keeping generation quality close to the base model.

Safety failures in DLMs are trajectory-driven: injecting a single early compliance token (“Sure”) sharply increases attack success while an early refusal token (“Sorry”) suppresses it, indicating early denoising steps are the highest-leverage intervention point.

Compared with prior training-free remasking defenses, token- and step-adaptive control avoids decode collapse (e.g., DiffuGuard producing ~99.8% empty outputs in one setting) and ablations show early steering is essential (ASR 18% full vs 54% without Phase 1; baseline 100%).

nodes.title: Targeted Neuron Modulation via Contrastive Pair Search
nodes.one_liner: A sparse, late-layer neuron circuit can be toggled to disable or amplify refusals without the coherence and capability tradeoffs seen in residual-stream steering, suggesting post-training turns pre-existing discrimination into a targetable safety gate.
points: Ablating just 0.1% of MLP neurons identified by contrastive neuron attribution (CNA) cuts refusal rates on JailbreakBench-Behaviors by more than half across Llama and Qwen instruct models (e.g., 90%→34% and 87%→2%), while keeping generation quality near baseline (>0.97) and MMLU within ~1 point.

Residual-stream steering (CAA) can reduce refusals at moderate strength but often collapses generation quality at high strength (quality <0.60 in 6/8 instruct models and near-zero MMLU at maximum intervention), whereas neuron-level modulation remains stable across the full steering range.

Late-layer harmful-vs-benign “discrimination” structure exists in both base and instruct models (often concentrated in the final ~10% of layers, with 58–87% of top neurons in the last 3 layers), but only becomes a causal refusal gate after alignment fine-tuning, with low base↔instruct neuron identity overlap (typically 8–29%).

nodes.title: SafeSteer: A Decoding-level Defense Mechanism for Multimodal Large Language Models
affiliations: Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics
nodes.one_liner: Safety interventions placed directly in the first few decoding steps can flip generation trajectories from harmful continuations into refusals with near-vanilla throughput and no parameter updates.
points: A lightweight decoding-time probe plus token reranking reduces multimodal jailbreak Attack Success Rate to 1.80% on Qwen2.5-VL and 0.60% on Qwen3-VL (vs 35.20% and 12.60% vanilla, respectively), demonstrating that safety can be substantially improved without any model fine-tuning.

Across models and benchmarks, the decoding-level approach improves safety by up to 33.40% while largely preserving utility (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL keeps 52.20% MM-Vet accuracy vs 57.50% vanilla), indicating a better helpfulness–harmlessness tradeoff than input/output rewriting defenses that tend to over-refuse.

Image-based jailbreaks are intrinsically stealthier in representation space (closer to benign clusters than text-only attacks), but a modal semantic alignment vector cuts MM-SafetyBench ASR from 15.13% (no components) to 1.75% on Qwen2.5-VL, showing that transferring textual safety alignment into the vision pathway materially closes a key multimodal gap.

nodes.title: MetaBackdoor: Exploiting Positional Encoding as a Backdoor Attack Surface in LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Backdoors don’t need suspicious text at all—token positions (and even natural conversation growth) can act as an invisible trigger that flips an LLM into a leaking or tool-abusing mode.
points: Length-based positional triggers can implant stealthy LLM backdoors that preserve clean accuracy (e.g., Gemma-3-4B drops <0.7 pp from 91.68% to 91.01%) while achieving near-perfect attack success (96.88–100% ASR across Gemma-3, Qwen-3, Phi-4, and Olmo-3 for exact/band/threshold triggers).

A supply-chain adversary can achieve high reliability with minimal poisoning—~90 poisoned samples already yield 91.43%±8.49% ASR and attacks saturate near 100% around ~5% poisoning—making “small, plausible” dataset contributions sufficient to compromise fine-tuned models.

Positional backdoors enable qualitatively new failures beyond content triggers, including verbatim leakage of previously unseen system prompts and “self-activation” in multi-turn chats (tool-call exfiltration reaches 78.1% format compliance and 75.0% correct leakage at ≥700 tokens) while common content-focused defenses largely miss the threat (e.g., ONION still leaves 90.5% ASR).

nodes.title: When Emotion Becomes Trigger: Emotion-style dynamic Backdoor Attack Parasitising Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Backdoors can be keyed off “how you say it” (emotion/tone) rather than “what you say,” yielding dynamic triggers that are both highly effective and difficult for token-pattern defenses to surface.
points: An emotion-style backdoor trigger (valence–arousal tone) achieves ~99–100% attack success at only a 1% poisoning rate (e.g., 364 Alpaca instructions; 810 AG News samples) across Llama2-7B, Vicuna-7B, Mistral-7B, and Qwen2.5-7B while largely preserving clean utility.

Emotion triggers behave as a higher-level, non-token, non-template signal: emotional rewrites form a distinct representation cluster while de-emotionalized paraphrases overlap with clean text, and removing the emotional style collapses activation from 93–99% ASR to ~0% across all four emotion quadrants.

The backdoor is comparatively persistent under post clean fine-tuning, retaining 90.40% ASR on Vicuna (instruction-following) and 99.95% ASR on Llama2 (classification) after additional clean SFT, implying style-level features can occupy an orthogonal subspace that is harder to overwrite than lexical/syntactic triggers.

nodes.title: BadSKP: Backdoor Attacks on Knowledge Graph-Enhanced LLMs with Soft Prompts
affiliations: Centre for Frontier AI Research (CFAR), A*STAR
Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences
Xi’an University of Electronic Science and Technology
nodes.one_liner: Graph-derived soft prompts can mask text-channel backdoors via semantic anchoring, but the same anchoring becomes a powerful control knob for attackers who can poison the graph-to-prompt interface.
points: Soft KG prompts create a measurable robustness gap where text-channel prompt-injection backdoors that reliably break textual KG prompting largely fail against soft-prompt KG systems due to graph-induced “semantic anchoring” that keeps generation aligned with the user query.

BadSKP breaks this anchoring by attacking the graph-to-prompt interface (optimizing target hidden-state embeddings, poisoning node embeddings, and approximating them with fluent node attributes), reaching very high attack success in the frozen setting (e.g., DoS ASR 0.93–1.00 on G-Retriever across triggers and 0.70–0.88 on GNP) while keeping clean accuracy close to baseline.

Text-only defenses and baselines are insufficient in this dual-channel architecture: GCG/ACPI are near-zero for DoS in many settings (often 0.00–0.03 ASR on G-Retriever), and perplexity filtering still leaves BadSKP effective (e.g., DoS ASR up to 0.97 with notable clean-accuracy drops), implying monitoring/defending the graph-conditioned prompt pathway is critical.

nodes.title: Context-Aware Spear Phishing: Generative AI-Enabled Attacks Against Individuals via Public Social Media Data
affiliations: The University of Texas at Arlington
nodes.one_liner: Public social-media OSINT plus mild prompt evasion makes spear phishing both highly personalized and harder for humans—and default LLM guardrails—to recognize at scale.
points: Using only ~10 public Instagram posts per person, the pipeline generated 17,916 context-aware spear-phishing emails whose perceived quality far exceeded real-world APWG spear-phishing, especially in personalization (85–90% vs 8.6%), linguistic naturalness (~100% vs 51.0%), and persuasiveness (>90% vs 32.7%).

In a 70-participant study, AI-generated spear-phishing was rated much less suspicious than APWG phishing (mean 2.75 vs 4.44/5) and even produced a negative suspiciousness “detection gap” (−0.36 vs +1.61), meaning participants often judged the AI phishing as less suspicious than benign emails.

A proactive prompt-intent filter can substantially raise the bar: a RoBERTa prompt detector reached 98.13% accuracy with 100% precision (no false positives) and 96% recall, while default safety filters passed through most malicious prompts (80.8–100% pass-through) unless strengthened with policy injection (84–96% detection) or SI+CoT moderation (up to 98.7% detection).

nodes.title: Comment and Control: Hijacking Agentic Workflows via Context-Grounded Evolution
affiliations: Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Lab
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that agentic workflow hijacking is a systems problem—workflow triggers, prompt construction, and runtime tool constraints jointly determine exploitability, and analyzing only the LLM prompt misses most real attack paths.
points: Across 112,568 GitHub workflows and 9,154 n8n templates, 4,174 GitHub workflows and 8 n8n templates were end-to-end hijackable, showing that partial attacker control of prompts is sufficient to drive real credential-exfiltration outcomes at scale.

Hijacks remain feasible even when prompt- and code-level defenses are present (e.g., “untrusted user input” framing plus command allowlists), because agents can be steered into equivalent capability paths (such as procfs-based secret recovery) that satisfy restrictions while still leaking sensitive data.

Context-aware analysis materially changes exploitability: baselines that only evolve prompts frequently fail against code-level safeguards (“F” in the code-defense column), while adding trigger/path feasibility, prompt provenance, and capability profiling yields consistent end-to-end success with typical bypass achieved in ~1–6 evolutionary iterations once the vulnerable path is confirmed.

nodes.title: ExploitBench: A Capability Ladder Benchmark for LLM Cybersecurity Agents
nodes.one_liner: Moving from “crash reproduction” to “exploit construction” can be measured reliably with cheat-resistant, deterministic oracles—and the results reveal a stark public-vs-private frontier split in crossing the V8 sandbox to ACE.
points: A 16-rung, deterministic capability ladder on 41 real, sandbox-on V8 N-days shows public frontier models routinely reach patched code (≈38–41/41) and often trigger crashes, but almost never cross from engine-local primitives to sandbox-escaping general primitives or arbitrary code execution (ACE).

Under a 300-turn cap, publicly deployed models achieved ACE in only 1 cell total (GPT-5.5 via vendor CLI on 1/41 bugs), while a non-public reference model reached ACE on 18/41 bugs in the primary (no-scaffolding) arm, implying the main constraint is model reasoning capability rather than benchmark hardness or budget.

Scaffolding changes measured outcomes but does not erase the capability gap: adaptive coaching raised GPT-5.5 Tier-3 coverage from 13→22 bugs yet reduced Gemini’s Tier-3 from 16→8 and reduced the non-public model’s ACE count from 18→16, indicating harness interventions can both help and harm and should be reported separately from base capability.

nodes.title: Exploiting LLM Agent Supply Chains via Payload-less Skills
nodes.one_liner: The most damaging agent supply-chain attacks may require no embedded code at all—only well-crafted “compliance” prose that coerces the agent to synthesize and execute the malware itself.
points: Payload-less “Semantic Compliance Hijacking” turns natural-language skill documentation into an execution primitive, reaching up to 77.67% complete secret exfiltration and 67.33% RCE in the most vulnerable agent–model configurations.

Static and semantic pre-ingestion scanners are effectively blind to prose-only malicious skills, with SkillScan and LLM Guard showing 0.00% detection for the payload-less skills while detecting explicit baselines at 62.00–91.61% (SkillScan) and 96.00–99.81% (LLM Guard).

Highly instruction-tuned models can be more exploitable (“alignment-security paradox”) because they reliably translate obfuscated compliance narratives into concrete system APIs (e.g., os.environ and exec), yielding average complete leakage as high as 62.11% (GPT-5.4 mini) across tested frameworks.

nodes.title: Trust Me, Import This: Dependency Steering Attacks via Malicious Agent Skills
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University
nodes.one_liner: Persistent “Skill” files turn dependency selection into a new supply-chain attack surface where subtle instruction edits can deterministically manufacture attacker-chosen imports at near-perfect rates while slipping past most pre-execution scanners.
points: Malicious persistent instruction artifacts (“Skills”/rules) can steer LLM coding agents to import or recommend an attacker-chosen package without changing model weights, training data, or user prompts, reaching targeted hallucination rates (THR) as high as 100% on Qwen2.5-Coder-32B and 78.57% on Llama-3.1-8B across common Python coding benchmarks.

Attack success generalizes broadly—optimized Skills maintain similar THR on held-out prompts, transfer across models (especially within code-specialized families), and remain high across package domains (requests/numpy/django), implying a domain-agnostic supply-chain risk rooted in instruction-following rather than task specifics.

Existing Skill vetting is brittle: static scanners largely fail (often rating attacks “low risk”), LLM/agent auditors are inconsistent, sandbox dynamic analysis detects more reliably, and an adaptive attacker can still evade both Snyk and sandboxing while retaining a 65.52% THR, indicating current defenses are not robust under feedback-driven optimization.

nodes.title: REALISTA: Realistic Latent Adversarial Attacks that Elicit LLM Hallucinations
nodes.one_liner: Constrained latent optimization over interpretable rephrase directions turns subtle, human-plausible rewrites into a reliable trigger for LLM hallucinations—even in black-box reasoning settings.
points: A latent-space attack constrained to a sparse nonnegative simplex over an input-dependent dictionary of semantics-preserving edit directions reaches 97.11% ASR@30 on Llama-3-3B and 93.60% on Llama-3-8B while keeping semantic-equivalence error low (SEE 0.86% and 3.48%, respectively).

On commercial black-box reasoning models with free-form outputs, the method improves hallucination attack success at K=30 from 4.02%→23.61% on GPT-5-Nano and from 2.01%→20.72% on GPT-5-Mini without meaning drift (SEE 1.73% and 0.87%), whereas an inducement-style baseline achieves similar goals only by fully breaking equivalence (SEE 100%).

Prior latent optimization and induced-hallucination baselines often “succeed” by changing the task rather than eliciting true hallucinations, evidenced by near-total semantic-equivalence violations (LARGO SEE ≈96–97% and ICD SEE 100%) despite high ASR on some open models, implying semantic constraints are essential for realistic red-teaming conclusions.

nodes.title: Can a Single Message Paralyze the AI Infrastructure? The Rise of AbO-DDoS Attacks through Targeted Mobius Injection
nodes.one_liner: It demonstrates a cost-asymmetric DoS shift where one poisoned message can weaponize trusted agents to repeatedly hit LLM and tool backends while staying below classic network DDoS thresholds.
points: A single indirect textual injection can reliably convert LLM agents into self-sustaining “zombie nodes” that amplify downstream resource usage, reaching up to 51.0× more model calls in a single node and up to 229.1× higher p95 latency for co-tenant traffic with four poisoned nodes, indicating a strong queue-saturation externality.

Across 6 agent platforms and 12 LLM backends, the attack’s end-to-end recurrence varies widely by model (e.g., 0% R-ASR on Claude Sonnet 4.6 vs 100% on GPT‑5.4 in one setting), showing that conventional “capability” or task success is not a proxy for resilience to recursive agent-component exploitation.

Network-rate and signature-style defenses can miss the attack because it can run at benign-like request rates (e.g., 3.66 req/min vs 5.50 req/min baseline) while still driving large token/compute drains, whereas component-layer monitoring (ACE) achieved 93.5% detection with 0% false positives in replay and quarantining eliminated trigger/recursion (T-ASR and R-ASR to 0%).

nodes.title: PASA: A Principled Embedding-Space Watermarking Approach for LLM-Generated Text under Semantic-Invariant Attacks
affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
nodes.one_liner: Embedding the watermark in semantic clusters (not tokens) yields distortion-free text with strong robustness to paraphrasing, keeping TPR@1%FPR above 0.53 even under aggressive reordering attacks.
points: Semantic-cluster watermarking in embedding space preserves near-perfect clean-text detectability (ROC-AUC ≈1.0; TPR@1%FPR=1.0 on both Llama2-13B and Mixtral-8×7B), while remaining substantially more robust than token-level schemes under meaning-preserving rewrites.

Under strong T5-XXL token-replacement attacks, PASA maintains high detection on Llama2-13B (ROC-AUC=0.9392; TPR@1%FPR=0.8040), versus large collapses for token-identity-dependent baselines such as KGW (TPR@1%FPR=0.6800) and especially DAWA (0.1450).

Against severe DIPPER paraphrasing with heavy word reordering (Lex=60, Ord=80), PASA retains usable low-FPR detectability (ROC-AUC=0.8956; TPR@1%FPR=0.5377) while prior methods degrade sharply (e.g., KGW 0.3100, Exp-edit 0.1150, AWTI 0.1350, DAWA 0.0200), indicating semantic anchoring is critical for paraphrase resilience.

nodes.title: Talk is (Not) Cheap: A Taxonomy and Benchmark Coverage Audit for LLM Attacks
nodes.one_liner: A large-scale attack taxonomy plus a STRIDE-based matrix shows that today’s flagship LLM attack benchmarks are largely disjoint and leave major real-world threat classes untested.
points: A 4×6 STRIDE-grounded Target×Technique matrix built from a 507-leaf inference-time attack taxonomy (2,521 deduplicated attack groups from 932 arXiv papers, 2023–H1 2026) enables benchmark-external validation of whether public evaluations cover the real LLM attack surface.

Across six public benchmarks, standardized evaluation covers at most 6 of 24 matrix cells (≤25%) with the three primary suites (HarmBench, InjecAgent, AgentDojo) sharing zero overlapping cells, implying current benchmark results cannot be treated as generalizable “LLM security” measures.

Entire threat areas have no standardized public testing—specifically the full Service Disruption row and Model Internals column—despite published attacks in these uncovered regions reporting up to 46× token amplification and up to 96% attack success rates, indicating high-impact risks are systematically under-measured.

nodes.title: Defense effectiveness across architectural layers: a mechanistic evaluation of persistent memory attacks on stateful LLM agents
nodes.one_liner: Most prompt/RAG sanitization fails not because it is poorly tuned but because it targets the wrong architectural choke point, whereas memory-tool capability control can eliminate the attack—until it unexpectedly flips safety by rerouting execution.
points: Across 5,040 runs on nine open-source stateful agents, delayed-trigger persistent-memory attacks reached 88.6% attack success with no defense and remained essentially unchanged under four common filters (input Minimizer/Sanitizer and retrieval RAG Sanitizer/LLM Judge), all at ~88–89% ASR, showing that detection-at-the-edges does not meaningfully reduce risk when the payload arrives via RAG and is semantically masked as legitimate policy.

Injection and recall were effectively guaranteed (100% injection across 2,520 attack runs, and universal recall thereafter), while false positives were 0/2,520 in the no-attack arm, implying the security problem is dominated by execution-stage control rather than preventing memory writes or benchmark measurement noise.

Tool-layer gating (Memory Sandbox removing explicit recall) cut ASR to 11.1% overall and to 0% for 8/9 models with 100% benign-task completion, but one model inverted from 0% ASR without defenses to 100% ASR under the sandbox—demonstrating that capability removal can redirect models into a more dangerous pathway (fresh RAG authority) and must be validated per-model rather than assumed monotonic.

nodes.title: Leveraging RAG for Training-Free Alignment of LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Contrastive retrieval (showing both what to do and what to avoid) can deliver strong, deployment-friendly refusal guardrails against agentic tool-use attacks where conventional safety-tuning plateaus.
points: Safety-tuning with DPO/SafeDPO provides limited protection against “falsely benign” MCP tool-use attacks, improving refusal rates only ~1.4× and ~1.6× on average and never exceeding 48% refusal across five evaluated LLMs.

A training-free, retrieval-based method that conditions on both preferred and dispreferred examples at inference time raises agentic-attack refusal by ~3× on average and achieves ~3.7× gains when layered on top of offline safety-tuning, outperforming other online alignment approaches (~2.9×) and offline tuning alone (~1.5×).

The approach substantially reduces operational cost by cutting preparation time by ~7,824× vs offline DPO on a 14B model and keeping inference overhead low (~20% slowdown) compared to decoding-based online alignment (~372%), while also improving general preference benchmarks (e.g., +24.4% average gains over baseline and +26.2% over other online methods on AlpacaEval 2).

nodes.title: FragBench: Cross-Session Attacks Hidden in Benign-Looking Fragments
nodes.one_liner: Benign-looking prompts become dangerous when you connect them across sessions, and simple graph detectors can spot the resulting kill-chain patterns even when per-prompt guardrails fail.
points: Cross-session fragmentation makes single-turn safety judging effectively unreliable by spreading malicious intent across independent benign-looking prompts, so the harmful signal only emerges at the user-level interaction graph.

A benchmark built from 24 real-world incident campaigns (22 MITRE ATT&CK techniques) shows graph-based detection can recover fragmented misuse with event-level F1 = 0.88–0.96 (best GCN F1 = 0.956), outperforming classical baselines by ~3–5 F1 points.

An attacker-side rewrite loop can raise per-fragment guardrail pass rates from ~60% to ~100% within ~10 rounds while preserving end-to-end executability via sandboxed tool-use validation, implying refusal-based controls alone are brittle under adaptive paraphrasing.

nodes.title: Behavioral Integrity Verification for AI Agent Skills
nodes.one_liner: What’s interesting is that a single typed “declared vs actual” capability comparison can both measure ecosystem-wide integrity drift (80% deviation) and drive high-accuracy malicious-skill detection (F1 0.946) without re-analyzing raw artifacts each time.
points: At registry scale (49,943 OpenClaw skills), 80.0% exhibited a declared-vs-actual capability mismatch, indicating that description–implementation gaps are the norm rather than an edge case in agent-skill supply chains.

Intent attribution over clustered deviations split 81.1% to developer oversight vs 18.9% to adversarial intent, but 5.0% of all skills were flagged as carrying multi-stage attack chains, concentrating review effort on a relatively small yet high-risk subset.

On a 906-skill malicious-skill benchmark, the structured BIV evidence plus adjudication achieved F1=0.946 (recall 0.978, precision 0.917, FPR 7.2%), outperforming a rule-based scanner and a single-pass LLM audit and showing that typed capability mismatch evidence can translate into deployable detection gains.

nodes.title: MonitoringBench: Semi-Automated Red-Teaming for Agent Monitoring
nodes.one_liner: Decomposing attack creation into strategy generation, execution, and post-hoc refinement uncovers substantially stronger and more diverse monitor-evasion trajectories than end-to-end elicitation, making reported high catch rates fragile.
points: A semi-automated, taxonomy-guided red-teaming pipeline produced MonitoringBench with 2,644 successful sabotage trajectories in BashArena and showed that relying on direct attack elicitation can substantially overstate monitor robustness due to narrow attack coverage and poor execution quality in elicited attacks.

Attack refinement and staged construction exposed a large robustness gap: the Opus 4.5 monitor’s catch rate at 1% FPR drops from 94.9% on elicited-only Opus attacks to 60.3% on the strongest refined attacks, with similarly large degradations (up to ~40 percentage points) for other strong monitors.

Monitor failures are often not pure misses but reasoning/calibration issues—e.g., GPT-5 has relatively few complete misses yet 82% of its uncaught cases are scoring/threshold failures (driven by a high 1% FPR threshold of 8.97), while stronger monitors commonly detect suspicious steps but are swayed by benign framing or fail to connect multi-step intent.

nodes.title: Adversarial SQL Injection Generation with LLM-Based Architectures
nodes.one_liner: The standout pattern is that LLM+RAG can overwhelm ML-based WAFs while barely moving the needle on mature rule-based CRS, and the biggest gains come from high-quality seeds plus structured reasoning rather than maximizing payload diversity.
points: Retrieval-augmented SQLi generation (RADAGAS) achieved the best overall mean WAF bypass rate at 22.73% (GPT-4o), beating traditional rule-based payloads (15.01%) and a vanilla zero-shot LLM generator (12.90%), showing that curated attack-context retrieval materially strengthens adversarial testing capability.

RADAGAS variants nearly collapsed AI/ML WAF defenses (up to 92.49% bypass on WAF-Brain and 80.48% on CNN-WAF) yet were largely ineffective on rule-based OWASP CRS deployments (0–5.70% on ModSecurity/Coraza), indicating current ML-based detectors can be far more brittle than signature/rule stacks under LLM-driven obfuscation.

Across generators, higher payload diversity did not reliably predict higher bypass (e.g., RefleXQLi produced 100% unique payloads but only 21.21% mean bypass), while adding strong seed payloads plus structured reasoning boosted bypass to 35.78% and MySQL execution success to 98.36%, implying “good seeds + reasoning” can dominate brute diversity for practical evasion.

nodes.title: Usability as a Weapon: Attacking the Safety of LLM-Based Code Generation via Usability Requirements
nodes.one_liner: Usability is an effective, realistic prompt-layer attack surface that makes secure code generation brittle by pushing models into reward-hacking trade-offs that silently discard implicit security constraints.
points: Benign-looking usability requirements reliably trigger security regressions in otherwise secure LLM-generated code, with automated UPAttacks reaching up to 98.1% attack success on state-of-the-art models across 25 CWEs and three languages.

Security–usability trade-off framing is the dominant failure mode (Type 3 ASR ~94%–98% and 100% ASR for some categories like input validation/crypto), implying that many models treat security as a soft preference that collapses under explicit “flexibility/compatibility” narratives.

Even when added requirements remain security-compatible, models still drop implicit safeguards at high rates (Type 1 ASR ~82%–86%; Type 2 ASR ~56%–61%), and over half of Type 2 successes (55.8%) are only detectable via dynamically generated exploit payloads rather than existing tests.

nodes.title: MATRA: Modeling the Attack Surface of Agentic AI Systems -- OpenClaw Case Study
affiliations: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security
nodes.one_liner: Threat modeling for agents becomes actionable when you quantify how specific architectural trust boundaries (which tools run inside vs. outside sandboxes) determine whether prompt injection turns into real-world data exfiltration or integrity loss.
points: In the OpenClaw case study, customer-PII exfiltration via indirect prompt injection remains Very High risk (score 9) even with Docker network sandboxing because non-container channels (email messaging and Telegram rendered-markup auto-fetch) preserve high-likelihood exfiltration paths.

Reducing PII exfiltration risk from Very High (9) to Moderate (3) required layered, architecture-level controls (outbound email allow-listing plus output sanitization that strips/rewrites data-bearing URLs), illustrating that single controls often fail when multiple comparable exfiltration vectors exist.

Non-adversarial failures are first-class risks in agentic systems: a hallucinated but syntactically valid destructive SQL query yields High risk (6) by default, while a read-only database role drops this to Moderate (3) by eliminating the unauthorized-action objective despite unchanged hallucination likelihood.

nodes.title: Proteus: A Self-Evolving Red Team for Agent Skill Ecosystems
nodes.one_liner: Structured audit findings and runtime traces create a powerful grey-box feedback loop that lets attackers evolve third-party agent skills into diverse, audit-passing, runtime-lethal variants at high rates.
points: Iterative, feedback-driven mutation of agent skill packages yields 40–90% verified end-to-end attack success within 5 rounds (ASR@5) across eight configurations, showing that one-shot skill audits substantially underestimate real deployment risk under adaptive attackers.

Expansion after first success produces 438 variants that both bypass audit and trigger oracle-verified runtime harm, with SkillVetter bypassed at ≥93% in every expansion cell and the strongest evaluated auditor (AI-Infra-Guard) still allowing up to 41.3% joint-success.

Attack strategies learned against the stronger auditor transfer to other vetters, as 87.7% of AI-Infra-Guard-evolved successful skills also bypass SkillVetter without further mutation, indicating ecosystem-level risk rather than auditor-specific overfitting.

nodes.title: FlowSteer: Prompt-Only Workflow Steering Exposes Planning-Time Vulnerabilities in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
nodes.one_liner: The main surprise is that attackers can reliably rewire multi-agent coordination at planning time using only a single crafted user prompt—so inspecting the generated workflow is often too late.
points: Prompt-only workflow steering can exploit planning-time structural sensitivity and sycophantic framing to raise malicious success by up to 55 percentage points over naive malicious prompting across multi-agent planner–executor setups.

Sycophantic/authoritative framing substantially increases downstream adoption and relay of malicious signals (up to +37 percentage points in controlled probing), showing that social-influence dynamics amplify not just content but also compliance cues.

Post-planning graph/topology defenses can be inconsistent or even counterproductive against planning-signal contamination, while an input-side decontamination layer reduces malicious success by up to 34 percentage points while largely preserving benign prompt utility.

nodes.title: Known By Their Actions: Fingerprinting LLM Browser Agents via UI Traces
affiliations: Oxford Internet Institute, University of Oxford
Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford
nodes.one_liner: Websites can silently identify which LLM is driving a browsing agent from its click/scroll/keypress cadence and interaction structure, making model-specific exploitation scalable and hard to audit.
points: Passive in-page JavaScript logging of UI event sequences and millisecond-level action timings can attribute the underlying model of a browser agent across 14 frontier LLMs with up to 96% macro-F1 (vs ~7% random chance), demonstrating a high-leakage behavioral side channel that does not rely on spoofable browser fingerprints or network metadata.

Timing statistics (e.g., inter-event interval mean/variance and time-to-first-action) provide the strongest attribution signal, but even when randomized delays degrade an unadapted classifier, retraining on delayed traces largely restores performance by shifting to robust action-structure features (e.g., key ratios, click dispersion, link-click ratios), implying naive timing jitter is not a durable defense against adaptive sites.

Fingerprinting is operationally efficient—near-peak performance is reached with <1/3 of training traces and identity can be inferred early within an episode (roughly the first ~40% of actions), enabling real-time model-targeted prompt injection, cost-inflation/DoS, or model-specific access control during a live session.

nodes.title: Toward Securing AI Agents Like Operating Systems
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Security and Privacy
nodes.one_liner: Treating the LLM as an untrusted user and the runtime as a kernel explains why prompt-level guardrails fail and why OS-grade isolation, least privilege, and complete mediation are the missing ingredients in today’s agent stacks.
points: Across 15 concrete attack scenarios, none of four widely used OpenClaw-style agents prevented all attacks, with OpenClaw failing every single test and even the most hardened variants still exposing at least one high-severity break such as configuration manipulation or system-prompt extraction.

Two systemic weaknesses were universal—cross-user data exfiltration and unauthorized outbound messaging succeeded against 4/4 agents—showing that agent “sessions” lack OS-like process isolation and that reply-only communication assumptions do not hold in deployed runtimes.

Security controls worked only when enforced as comprehensive, lower-privileged mediation (e.g., sandboxing/egress filtering), but partial or mis-scoped implementations collapsed—illustrated by minor mistakes enabling IronClaw configuration attacks and NemoClaw system-prompt extraction despite their security-focused designs.

nodes.title: Model-Agnostic Lifelong LLM Safety via Externalized Attack-Defense Co-Evolution
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: External memory + reusable skill abstractions turn safety into a continually updatable, model-agnostic layer that improves without repeatedly fine-tuning the victim LLM.
points: EvoSafety’s lightweight 3B auxiliary guard achieves 99.61% harmful-input filtering success (up to 100% with memory expansion) while beating Qwen3Guard-8B by 14.13% using only 37.5% of the parameters and showing negligible false positives on benign data.

Replacing model-specific safety fine-tuning with a reusable defense-prefix generator plus verified memory reduces jailbreak success rates across victim model scales, cutting average ASR from 19.92% (best baseline TriPlay-RL) to 11.62% (~40% relative reduction) while preserving reasoning accuracy (≈−0.29 vs ≈−2.15).

Externalizing attacks into an adversarial skill library mitigates attack saturation and yields strong single-turn jailbreak capability (92.8% average ASR), improving over Jailbreak-R1 by 65.3% ASR and retaining strong zero-shot generalization to held-out skills (84.6% average ASR).

nodes.title: Few-Shot Truly Benign DPO Attack for Jailbreaking LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Preference optimization can turn an apparently legitimate over-refusal reduction request into a low-cost, hard-to-audit jailbreak that generalizes far beyond the fine-tuning prompts.
points: Using only 10 harmless DPO preference pairs that prefer a normal helpful answer over a refusal (the minimum accepted by OpenAI’s DPO fine-tuning API), refusal behavior is broadly suppressed and transfers to unseen harmful prompts, reaching ASR 59.13% (GPT-4o), 70.20% (GPT-4.1), 54.80% (GPT-4.1-mini), and 81.73% (GPT-4.1-nano) at fine-tuning costs of about $1.7, $1.7, $0.3, and $0.1 respectively.

The effect is highly data-efficient and objective-driven: on open-weight models it can emerge from a single benign preference pair, and replacing the dispreferred sample with a non-refusal drops ASR to ~21% (vs ~57–68% when dispreferred samples are refusals), indicating the core failure mode is specifically “anti-refusal” optimization rather than generic negative examples.

Content moderation and intent-auditing are poor discriminators in this setting: OpenAI’s moderation API flags 0% of samples and multiple frontier LLM auditors also largely fail to detect malicious intent in the dataset (0% detection for most auditors, with the highest reported being 8%±7.88), while the same auditors reliably flag more contrived benign fine-tuning attack datasets (often ~98–100%).

nodes.title: Benchmarking Safety Risks of Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning under Malicious Knowledge Editing
nodes.one_liner: Knowledge editing is not just an update tool but a high-stealth attack vector: models can keep general benchmarks essentially unchanged while their knowledge-grounded reasoning becomes selectively and reliably corrupted.
points: Malicious knowledge editing can achieve near-perfect single-hop override of factual behavior (e.g., WISE reaches ~99–100% edit success with ~90–99% portability and ~100% locality on Llama3-8B/Mistral-7B/Qwen2.5-7B), showing that targeted misinformation/bias/unsafe “facts” can be injected without obvious collateral damage.

Compositional QA is a critical amplification surface because multi-hop final-answer accuracy remains far below edit adoption (e.g., Llama3-8B with WISE: 100% edit-wise success but only 47.9% multi-hop accuracy; GPT-4 with PokeMQA: 94.2% edit-wise but 69.5% multi-hop), creating a stealthy regime where individual corrupted facts are recalled yet end-to-end reasoning becomes unreliable or harmful.

Attack scalability is limited by reasoning consistency rather than injection success, with multi-hop accuracy degrading sharply as batch edits grow (DeepSeek-R1 PokeMQA: 70.1% at 1 edit → 62.9% at 100 → 55.2% at 1000 → 46.0% at all-edited), implying defenders should treat large edit sets as a high-risk operational mode requiring stricter controls and monitoring.

nodes.title: Reconstruction of Personally Identifiable Information from Supervised Finetuned Models
nodes.one_liner: Instruction-tuned SFT models can leak user-linked PII via prefix attacks, and better decoding plus small amounts of auxiliary knowledge can turn weak leakage into practical reconstruction.
points: Fine-tuning on multi-turn medical/legal Q&A with embedded PII measurably increases reconstructability, with email top-100 recall rising by about 9–12 percentage points over the base model across attacker-knowledge settings (e.g., medical: 7.39%→18.55%; legal: 7.07%→19.53%).

Attacker knowledge materially shifts risk for higher-entropy identifiers like date of birth, where top-100 recall ranges from 0.57% (name-only) to 3.82% (redacted-data access) in medical and from 0.23% to 2.09% in legal, while email remains comparatively easy to reconstruct even with minimal knowledge (name-only top-100: 10.77–14.73%).

A coverage-oriented sequence-level decoder (COVA) improves targeted reconstruction versus common decoding baselines, especially in moderate-knowledge settings (e.g., medical DOB top-100: 1.06% vs 0.36% in-distribution and 1.61% vs 0.52% with summaries compared to beam search), and prior-aware re-ranking (LLR using base+fine-tuned models) further boosts ranking for DOB and names (e.g., DOB top-10: 0.09%→0.33%).

nodes.title: Knowledge Poisoning Attacks on Medical Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
nodes.one_liner: Imperceptible image perturbations can act as query-agnostic triggers to force retrieval of “plausible” but wrong medical evidence, and simple corpus filtering heuristics barely reduce the attack success rate.
points: A query-agnostic multimodal RAG poisoning approach (M3Att) achieves end-to-end degradation across five LVLMs and five medical datasets with a poison rate below 1% by coupling image-based retrieval hijacking with clinically plausible text edits that evade model self-correction.

Even under black-box access, zeroth-order optimization yields retrieval hijacking success rates comparable to white-box across three retrievers (CLIP, BGE-VL, SigLIP), indicating that lack of gradients is not a meaningful barrier for practical attacks on medical multimodal RAG retrieval.

Downstream task utility drops substantially once poisoned entries are retrieved, with an average reduction of 8.78% versus clean RAG and near-chance performance in some filtered (successful-hijack) settings, implying that retrieval integrity is a single point of failure for medical decision support.

nodes.title: CoT-Guard: Small Models for Strong Monitoring
nodes.one_liner: A carefully post-trained 4B model can be a stronger hidden-objective CoT monitor for code than several frontier proprietary monitors, shifting monitoring from expensive APIs to practical local defense.
points: A 4B monitor (CoT-Guard) trained via SFT distillation plus RL on subtle “hard” hidden objectives achieves 75.7% G-Mean² under prompt-manipulation (A1) CoT+Code monitoring, outperforming GPT-5.4 (56.5%), GPT-5-mini (41.9%), and Qwen3-32B (54.1%) while approaching Gemini-3-Flash (83.8%).

Small 4B–8B monitors exhibit a core failure mode—task misattribution—where they can notice the hidden objective in chain-of-thought but incorrectly treat it as part of the user’s request, yielding near-zero gains from adding tainted CoT (≤+3 pp) compared to large monitors’ sizable gains (e.g., GPT-5.4 +26.5 pp).

Training on subtly crafted objectives is a key generalization lever: RL-only already lifts a 4B model from 13.4% to 66.9% G-Mean² (A1 CoT+Code), and adding SFT+RL yields 75.7% while remaining robust to concealment prompts (Monitor-Notice reduces average G-Mean² by ~1.1%).

nodes.title: MCPShield: Content-Aware Attack Detection for LLM Agent Tool-Call Traffic
nodes.one_liner: Most of the attack-detection power comes from simple pooled sentence embeddings of tool-call content, and sloppy random splits can overstate performance by ~26 AUROC points via task memorization.
points: Semantic content is the decisive detection signal in MCP tool-call traffic: metadata-only monitoring saturates around AUROC≈0.64, while SBERT embeddings of tool arguments/responses raise AUROC above 0.89 and reach 0.975 with XGBoost on pooled embeddings.

Evaluation protocol materially changes conclusions: label-stratified random splits can inflate AUROC by up to 25.8 points versus task-disjoint splits (e.g., 0.898→0.640 with metadata), indicating that many prior agent-traffic detectors may be measuring task memorization rather than attack generalization.

Model complexity is not the bottleneck: tree ensembles on pooled SBERT features outperform GNNs in the primary MCP-native setting (RAS-Eval XGBoost AUROC 0.975 vs best GNN 0.917, MLP 0.896), and benign-only contrastive pretraining provides no consistent label-efficiency advantage beyond the 1% label regime.

nodes.title: From Controlled to the Wild: Evaluation of Pentesting Agents for the Real-World
nodes.one_liner: Evaluation that rewards validated vulnerability discovery (and penalizes duplicate/noisy reporting) produces different—and more deployment-relevant—rankings than CTF-style binary success, especially once you measure cumulative coverage under stochasticity and cost/runtime constraints.
points: A finding-centric protocol (validated vulnerability discovery rather than flags/trajectory similarity) was demonstrated on 3 targets with 108 expert-annotated vulnerabilities, enabling precision/recall/F-scores plus CWE coverage and severity-weighted scoring at the report level.

LLM-based semantic matching for finding→ground-truth attribution closely tracked expert triage on a 50-finding sanity set, with GPT‑5.4 Mini averaging about 1 error (mislabeling a true positive as a duplicate) and low run-to-run variance, supporting scalable scoring under ambiguity.

Across engines and models, single runs left many false negatives (no configuration fully covered targets), while cumulative evaluation over 3 independent runs materially changed operational conclusions—e.g., Strix+Sonnet nearly doubled recall while staying comparatively precise, whereas PentAGI+Sonnet’s precision degradation caused its cumulative F0.5 to drop below its per-run mean.

nodes.title: SecureForge: Finding and Preventing Vulnerabilities in LLM-Generated Code via Prompt Optimization
nodes.one_liner: Prompt optimization driven by static-analysis feedback can materially reduce CWE-class vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code without fine-tuning or sacrificing functional correctness, and the gains generalize to real developer prompts.
points: Even with explicit instructions to write secure production code and avoid the evaluated CWEs, frontier code models still emitted statically verifiable vulnerabilities about 23% of the time across 250 benign prompts (12.7% while also passing unit tests), indicating that “secure prompting” alone is insufficient for secure-by-default code generation.

SecureForge’s inference-time hardening (benign failure discovery → MCMC prompt amplification to ~80,000 prompts → GEPA-optimized system prompt) delivered a statistically significant Pareto improvement on frontier models, reducing vulnerability rates in test-passing outputs by up to 48% while keeping unit-test pass rates essentially unchanged.

The optimized system prompts transferred zero-shot to 203 real-world SWE-chat tasks without seeing real user prompt distributions during optimization, improving the joint rate of “tests pass and no static-analysis findings” by up to 13.5% and reducing the fraction of tasks where new vulnerabilities were introduced (11% had ≥1 post-only finding).

nodes.title: Natural Language based Specification and Verification
nodes.one_liner: Natural-language specifications can act as a practical intermediate representation that helps LLMs verify memory safety compositionally, but false-positive calibration remains the key deployment risk.
points: A callee-first pipeline that turns C/C++ functions into reusable natural-language JSON contracts materially improves interprocedural memory-safety checking, reducing false negatives from 301 to 216 and increasing true positives from 3390 to 3476 versus whole-program prompting.

On SV-COMP 2026 memory-safety tasks, the highest-recall LLM configuration (Claude Sonnet-4.6) achieved 99.8% recall with 89.6% accuracy but produced 354 false positives, indicating calibration—not basic capability—as the dominant blocker to “proof-like” reliability.

Traditional verifiers stayed highly precise by returning many UNKNOWNs (e.g., CPAchecker 247 UNK, Symbiotic 301 UNK, UAutomizer 895 UNK), while LLM-based checking always produced a definitive label, making it better suited to high-coverage triage than sound certification.

nodes.title: Veritas: A Semantically Grounded Agentic Framework for Memory Corruption Vulnerability Detection in Binaries
