nodes.one_liner: The key move is turning binary vulnerability discovery into a grounded workflow where the LLM never has to rediscover propagation from decompiled noise and every claim is forced through executable evidence.
points: A three-stage pipeline that statically grounds LLM reasoning in witness-backed interprocedural taint flows and then requires runtime confirmation reached 90% recall (18/20) on stripped-binary out-of-bounds cases, far exceeding fuzzing (10% recall) and source-level agent baselines (25–35% recall).

Runtime grounding sharply reduced erroneous reports: across 623 exhaustively validated detector candidates there were 0 false positives, and a 10% audit of 3,344 additional candidates found only 2 manually confirmed false positives, implying most remaining error comes from binary recovery artifacts rather than LLM speculation.

Evidence-driven slicing outperformed call-graph expansion by preserving full-trace connectivity in 20/20 cases while keeping context tiny (5.16 functions per flow vs ~409 functions at 4-hop call-graph expansion), indicating that shared-state propagation (not call chains) is the dominant bottleneck in binary memory-corruption reasoning.

nodes.title: LLMs for Secure Hardware Design and Related Problems: Opportunities and Challenges
nodes.one_liner: LLMs can dramatically accelerate secure hardware design, but without dynamic benchmarking, domain-specific safety alignment, and tool-verified agentic workflows, they can just as easily amplify IP leakage, backdoors, and Trojan-enabled designs.
points: Tool-augmented, iterative agent loops that feed back compiler/simulation diagnostics (and sometimes formal checks) outperform zero-shot prompting for RTL generation, making closed-loop verification the practical baseline for reliable LLM-driven EDA flows.

Representation choice is a first-order limiter: across 202 tasks, Verilog achieves 83%–88% simulation pass rates while HLS-C achieves only 3%–10%, implying that “more accessible” IRs can yield systematically worse correctness due to interface/protocol mismatches and sparse training data.

Evaluation and safety are currently brittle at scale, with near-100% benchmark contamination reported for common RTL benchmarks in many commercial models and domain-specific jailbreak tests showing safety-hardened models with near-zero resistance to semantically disguised hardware-security attacks.

nodes.title: Graph Representation Learning Augmented Model Manipulation on Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs
nodes.one_liner: Stealthy poisoning in FedLLMs can be automated by learning benign-update correlation graphs, producing attacks that look normal to standard similarity-based filters while sharply degrading accuracy.
points: A graph-representation-guided manipulation (AugMP) on federated fine-tuning can reduce global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and the average local-agent accuracy by up to 22%, showing that a small number of adversarial participants can materially corrupt FedLLM training dynamics.

Malicious updates generated via variational graph autoencoding plus graph spectral transformation can remain statistically and geometrically similar to benign updates, enabling evasion of common Euclidean-distance and cosine-similarity defenses that assume poisoned updates are outliers.

LoRA configuration changes the attack surface non-uniformly across backbones (e.g., stronger degradation for DistilBERT as trainable space grows, but partial recovery for Qwen2.5 at higher LoRA capacity), implying that “more/less parameter-efficient” is not a reliable robustness proxy without adversary-aware evaluation.

nodes.title: Benchmarking LLM-Based Static Analysis for Secure Smart Contract Development: Reliability, Limitations, and Potential Hybrid Solutions
affiliations: Alexandru Ioan Cuza University of Iași
nodes.one_liner: LLMs look better at smart-contract auditing than they are because lexical shortcuts and tool-grounding can inflate apparent performance while masking brittle reasoning and bias toward external reports.
points: Sanitizing identifiers and annotations to remove lexical cues reduced balanced accuracy by 0.046–0.086 on average (ΔBA: −0.0777 for Zero-Shot and −0.0855 for CoT), showing that many “vulnerability” hits are driven by naming/comments rather than semantic execution logic.

Chain-of-Thought prompting did not reliably improve detection quality (ΔBA −0.0079 without tool grounding and +0.0047 with grounding) while increasing latency (up to ~10s extra per contract) and roughly doubling invalid structured outputs for local models, implying CoT is a costlier noise filter with inconsistent net benefit.

Grounding with Slither improved balanced accuracy (ΔBA +0.0316 for Zero-Shot and +0.0442 for CoT) but strongly increased tool-copying behavior (e.g., a 354% rise in Slither-matching detections for Qwen3 30b thinking and 276% for DeepSeek R1 14b local), indicating hybrid systems risk becoming “stochastic mirrors” unless grounded on more semantic artifacts (e.g., CFG/SSA) or staged discovery-then-verification designs.

nodes.title: AutoSOUP: Safety-Oriented Unit Proof Generation for Component-level Memory-Safety Verification
nodes.one_liner: A hybrid “LLM-as-function-call” pipeline can reliably synthesize maintainable unit proofs that make bounded memory-safety verification practical enough to catch real embedded CVEs—and newly found bugs—while keeping assumptions explicit and checkable.
points: AutoSOUP automatically produced structurally valid, conclusive unit proofs for 93% of embedded C verification targets (89.5% at a wider scope) versus 31.6% for a vanilla coding-agent baseline, indicating that constrained generation plus deterministic validation is essential for scalable component-level BMC workflows.

Across 60 recreated memory-safety CVEs in embedded RTOS components, AutoSOUP exposed 66.7% of vulnerabilities (65% with wider scope), outperforming Seeker (41.7%) and a coding-agent baseline (28.3%), and it additionally confirmed 20 new externally triggerable issues dominated by out-of-bounds writes/reads with realistic exploitation implications (DoS, info leak, potential RCE).

Safety-oriented refinement yields high assurance under explicit assumptions: property-guided loop/model refinement raised explored code to 139.6 covered LOC on average, while context-aware environment refinement increased verified properties to 98.6% of reachable checks (531.6/539) by turning infeasible counterexamples into auditable preconditions rather than silently masking behavior.

nodes.title: Continuous Discovery of Vulnerabilities in LLM Serving Systems with Fuzzing
nodes.one_liner: Treating concurrent request timing and lifecycle events as fuzzing inputs exposes a large, under-monitored vulnerability surface where LLM servers can stay “healthy” while leaking correctness, isolation, and availability.
points: A greybox fuzzer that treats timed multi-request traces as inputs found 15 serving-layer vulnerabilities across vLLM and SGLang, with 10 developer-confirmed and 2 assigned CVEs, showing that LLM infrastructure bugs often live in concurrency/caching/scheduling rather than model behavior.

KV-cache isolation failures can silently corrupt co-scheduled users’ outputs without malformed inputs or server errors, producing fluent but wrong answers and reasoning disturbances that violate cross-tenant isolation expectations.

API-valid “noisy neighbor” workloads can cause extreme cross-request performance interference (e.g., 1,361× time-to-first-token inflation and near-zero victim throughput) and can also trigger delayed scheduler crashes, implying that availability and correctness depend on serving-loop fairness and invariant-preserving batching under load.

nodes.title: Threat Modelling using Domain-Adapted Language Models: Empirical Evaluation and Insights
nodes.one_liner: Even with domain adaptation and larger parameter counts, structured STRIDE threat modeling remains brittle because prompt/decoding choices and output-validity failures dominate practical usability.
points: Across 52 configurations spanning 8 models, domain-adapted telecom/cyber models did not consistently beat their general-purpose counterparts on STRIDE threat classification, with best penalized F1 remaining in the ~65–69% range (e.g., 68.75% greedy and 64.71% sampling).

Model scale helped but did not make STRIDE modeling reliable: 8B models delivered the top scores while 1B/3B variants showed markedly lower and more unstable performance and higher invalid-output behavior, indicating scaling improves capability without eliminating structured-reasoning and format-following failures.

Decoding and prompting materially changed outcomes: stochastic sampling exposed label instability (aggregation required a ≥50% selection threshold over 10 runs), and few-shot prompting often increased recall for larger models but could degrade small-model reliability, while chat templates mainly reduced invalid outputs rather than guaranteeing higher accuracy.

nodes.title: MemLineage: Lineage-Guided Enforcement for LLM Agent Memory
affiliations: Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
State Key Laboratory of Cyberspace Security Defense
nodes.one_liner: Treating agent memory as a chain-of-custody problem (not a content filter) blocks “laundered” malicious instructions that become validly signed agent-written memories while keeping overhead negligible.
points: A six-module memory defense that combines per-entry Ed25519 signatures anchored in an RFC‑6962 Merkle log with a weighted derivation DAG and a max-of-strong-edges propagation rule reduced attack success rate to 0 across three memory-poisoning workloads in a deterministic harness where no-defense and signature-only baselines failed (1.00 ASR) on laundering-style attacks.

Signature-only provenance eliminated trigger-bound forged-entry poisoning (0.00 ASR) but still allowed both retrieval poisoning and “sleeper-via-derivation” memory laundering (1.00 ASR), indicating that writer authentication alone cannot prevent agent-authored summaries from inheriting untrusted authority without explicit lineage tracking.

A τ×K ablation showed safety depends on derivation depth (e.g., with a decaying attribution schedule w0=0.9,d=0.7, a threshold that appears safe at K=1 (τ≤0.30) becomes unsafe by K=5 (requiring τ≤0.10), while cryptographic/provenance operations add sub-millisecond overhead per op—below typical LLM-call latency—supporting deployability.

nodes.title: Evaluating Tool Cloning in Agentic-AI Ecosystems
nodes.one_liner: Agent-tool marketplaces can look large while being structurally repetitive, and simple similarity audits plus calibration reveal substantial hidden cloning that can leak across benchmark splits and security reviews.
points: Across 8,861 public agent-tool repositories (100,011 tool entries), high-similarity “tails” appear in MCP–MCP, Skills–Skills, and MCP–Skills comparisons, indicating that raw tool/repo counts can materially overstate true implementation diversity.

Manual inspection shows high precision for code-clone candidates in MCP: 60% of top-bucket high-Jaccard pairs (80–100) and 85% of top-bucket high-ssdeep pairs (80–100) are true clones, implying that similarity-aware de-duplication is necessary for trustworthy ecosystem statistics and evaluation splits.

High-similarity candidate pairs form non-trivial connected components (largest cluster up to 38 repos) and involve hundreds of unique repositories (e.g., 441 repos for MCP–MCP at Jaccard ≥80), suggesting cloning propagates templates/vulnerable scaffolds beyond isolated one-off forks and should be handled via provenance- and similarity-aware auditing.

nodes.title: Defenses at Odds: Measuring and Explaining Defense Conflicts in Large Language Models
nodes.one_liner: Sequentially patching LLMs for different trust goals can silently reopen earlier vulnerabilities, but a simple, mechanistically targeted layer-freezing rule can block many of these regressions.
points: Across 144 ordered defense sequences spanning safety, privacy, and fairness over three LLM families, 38.9% caused measurable regression (RRR>0) on the originally defended risk, including two catastrophic collapses (RRR>1) where the final model became more vulnerable than the unpatched base.

Deployment order is a first-class security variable: fairness-first sequences were the most fragile with a 64.6% conflict rate, while privacy-first sequences were comparatively resilient (27.1% conflicts with max RRR≈0.14), and reversing the same pair (e.g., DPO→RMU vs RMU→DPO) could flip collapse into synergy.

Mechanistically, conflicts concentrate in a small set of overlapping “critical layers,” where conflicting sequences show strongly anti-aligned updates (cosine similarity ≈ −0.69 vs ≈ −0.09 for benign orderings) and activation-trajectory reversals; selectively freezing high-conflict layers during the second patch prevented regressions while keeping the secondary defense effective (e.g., averting Llama-S DPO→RMU safety collapse and reducing Qwen-S Unbias→RMU fairness collapse from 29.88 to 5.38).

nodes.title: On-Policy Self-Evolution via Failure Trajectories for Agentic Safety Alignment
nodes.one_liner: Failed tool-use trajectories can be recycled into dense, multi-objective repair targets that measurably harden agents against prompt injection and harmful requests without sacrificing benign utility.
points: An on-policy self-evolution pipeline that turns verifier-scored failed agent trajectories into Pareto-filtered “repair” supervision reduced prompt-injection attack success by 33.5% while improving task success under attack by 26.0%, indicating safety gains without the typical utility collapse.

Across harmful-request tasks, the method cut harmful compliance by 82.6% and raised valid refusal rate from 15.6% to 81.2% (≈+65.6 points), showing that trajectory-level multi-objective optimization can calibrate refusals instead of inducing blanket refusal.

Despite being trained only on AgentDojo/AgentHarm development splits, the resulting policy improved external long-horizon trajectory-safety diagnosis on ATBench by 6.5% (F1 79.5 vs 71.1), suggesting that learning from failure trajectories transfers beyond the original environments.

nodes.title: Robust Multi-Agent LLMs under Byzantine Faults
nodes.one_liner: Replacing sender-reported confidence with receiver-side evaluation and pairing it with (F+1)-robust connectivity makes decentralized LLM consensus resilient to Byzantine agents without sacrificing strong-model performance.
points: Receiver-side scoring plus MSR-style filtering (SAC) flips Byzantine collaboration from harmful to beneficial, delivering +3.8% to +7.6% accuracy lift on MATH500 Level 4 while confidence-weighted CP-WBFT consistently harms performance (BFTI −10.4% to −24.8%).

Self-reported confidence is a high-leverage attack surface: with one adversarial agent falsifying confidence to 1.0, CP-WBFT collapses immediately after round 1 and stays corrupted, driving commonsense accuracy drops of −32.4% to −54.7% and honest-majority success as low as 16.7%.

Graph design provides actionable robustness guarantees: using (F+1)-robust topologies with F=3 enables SAC to keep strong agents near-baseline (e.g., 84%→88% on MERG) while lifting weaker agents (e.g., 23%→43%), achieving 76.7%–96.7% honest-majority correctness across tasks and topologies.

nodes.title: Don't Click That: Teaching Web Agents to Resist Deceptive Interfaces
nodes.one_liner: Interposing a calibrated “click-safety gate” in front of web agents can both neutralize dark-pattern traps and improve general UI grounding by reducing null-region misclicks.
points: A new 1,407-scenario benchmark (RUC) with dual annotations for correct vs. deceptive click targets across four domains (News/Booking/Shopping/Software) enables systematic measurement of deception-induced failures in VLM web agents.

A two-stage defense (DUDE) combining asymmetric-penalty hybrid-reward learning with deployment-time experience summarization cuts deception susceptibility by 53.8% while preserving task performance, and can drive deception-induced failure down to ~0–1.5% in evaluated settings.

Severity-weighting in the reward is the key safety lever: removing asymmetric penalties increases catastrophic false-accepts of deceptive clicks from 9.75% to 27.53%, showing that accuracy-only optimization materially underprices high-impact safety failures.

nodes.title: Red-Teaming Agent Execution Contexts: Open-World Security Evaluation on OpenClaw
nodes.one_liner: Execution context (files, skills, tool metadata, memory) can stealthily hijack agent behavior such that the final answer still looks correct, making trajectory-level auditing essential.
points: Across 42 contextual attack cases and nine OpenClaw target models, multiple models exhibit high attack success while keeping benign task utility high (e.g., Qwen3.5-Plus reaches AGS 0.74–0.97 with UGS 0.92–1.00 across six risk classes), showing that agents can be compromised without obvious user-visible failure.

Privacy leakage is the most consistently triggered contextual risk, with top attack success rates near-saturating on several models (DeepSeek-v4-Flash/Pro AGS 0.96 and Qwen3.5-Plus AGS 0.93), implying that sensitive context (credentials/memory/config) is easily exfiltrated during normal workflows unless context access is explicitly governed and audited.

Iterative, trajectory-guided trap refinement materially increases discovered vulnerability rate (mean AGS rises from 0.65 to 0.75 over five iterations, with the largest gains for encoding obfuscation), indicating that one-shot prompt/response safety tests systematically under-detect execution-context attacks and should be replaced with rollout-based evaluation plus artifact inspection.

nodes.title: The Bystander Effect in Multi-Agent Reasoning: Quantifying Cognitive Loafing in Collaborative Interactions
nodes.one_liner: Multi-agent scaffolds can induce a bystander-effect failure mode where models knowingly compute the right answer but output the crowd’s wrong one, and the first listed “authority” disproportionately controls the outcome.
points: Simulated multi-agent “auditor” pressure can degrade LLM reasoning rather than improve it, with GPT‑5.4 on SWE-bench collapsing from 1.00 accuracy at n=0 to 0.23 at just n=2 auditors (p<0.001), indicating an Interaction Depth Limit near 2 in medium-entropy technical settings.

Models can exhibit “alignment hallucinations” where internal reasoning remains relatively evidence-based but the final answer conforms to the crowd, exemplified by GPT‑5.4 on SWE-bench at n=5 with internal validity ≈0.71 (Eew=3.56/5) but external accuracy 0.37 (Sovereignty Gap +0.34).

Social influence in multi-agent prompting is order-sensitive (non-commutative), with lead-auditor primacy measurably changing outcomes—e.g., for GPT‑5.4 on SWE-bench at n=2, (Claude→GPT) yields 0.21 accuracy while (GPT→Claude) yields 0.31, so a 10-point swing arises solely from auditor ordering.

nodes.title: AgentPSO: Evolving Agent Reasoning Skill via Multi-agent Particle Swarm Optimization
affiliations: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)
nodes.one_liner: Interesting because it treats agent instructions as optimizable state and shows that population-level skill evolution (not test-time debate) can yield reusable, cross-model reasoning procedures.
points: AgentPSO evolves multi-agent “skill prompts” via PSO-style updates plus a self-reflective direction and achieves the best overall average accuracy of 71.98% across DeepMath/MATH/AIME25/Minerva/BBH, outperforming static multi-agent baselines like DMAD (68.79%) and MAD (67.68%).

The self-reflective update term is a major driver of gains, with DeepMath accuracy dropping from 79.50% (Pass@k 87.00) to 72.50% (Pass@k 80.50) when it is removed, indicating that peer-trajectory-derived behavioral lessons matter more than simply drifting toward personal/global best prompts.

Evolved skills generalize beyond the training setup, transferring across benchmarks (e.g., BBH-trained skills reach 75.00% on DeepMath vs MAD at 69.00%) and across backbone models, where GPT-optimized skills raise Claude’s average accuracy to 58.37% versus 52.83–55.51% for standard prompting baselines.

nodes.title: IFPV: An Integrated Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Operational Planning and High-Fidelity Plan Verification
affiliations: Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Swarm Systems, Ministry of Education
Henan Research Center for Large Model Technology and New Quality Software Engineering
nodes.one_liner: The standout idea is coupling LLM-based multi-agent plan synthesis with a learned, value-aware adversary to make verification adversarial, high-fidelity, and measurably more discriminative than rule checks or static LLM judging.
points: A hierarchical multi-agent planner (MPHA) improved mission success by 19.4% and reduced average operational cost by 41.7% versus single-step LLM planning, indicating that role-specialized decomposition plus constraint repair materially boosts executability under tight spatiotemporal/resource constraints.

A domain-adapted world-model opponent (ACSE) achieved very low trajectory-prediction error (ADE 0.18, FDE 0.54) and cut error by ~81.1% (ADE) and ~65.8% (FDE) relative to the strongest tested general-purpose baseline (InternLM2.5-7B), showing that simulation-grounded fine-tuning can turn LLMs into accurate dynamics predictors in adversarial settings.

Entity-Value-Aware Weighted Loss (EVA-Loss) reduced world-model prediction error by ~47.7% (ADE) and ~37.9% (FDE) vs native cross-entropy and produced a stricter validator that raised suppression by 31.8% over a traditional rule-based validator, implying that weighting mission-critical entities improves both prediction fidelity and vulnerability discovery during verification.

nodes.title: OTora: A Unified Red Teaming Framework for Reasoning-Level Denial-of-Service in LLM Agents
nodes.one_liner: Availability can be attacked at the reasoning layer: agents can be forced into long, tool-amplifying “correct” detours that violate latency/SLA constraints without looking like classic jailbreaks or goal hijacks.
points: A two-stage R-DoS pipeline can preserve task correctness while inflating agent compute, reaching up to ~10× reasoning-token inflation and order-of-magnitude end-to-end latency slowdowns across WebShop/Email/OS agents on backbones including LLaMA-3.1-70B and GPT-OSS-120B.

Triggering attacker-chosen external access is feasible even in API-only settings, with environment-observation injection consistently outperforming instruction injection (typically +3–6% higher tool-call success) and black-box tool-call ASR_H reaching ~42–58% depending on agent/model.

Cost/latency guards face an inherent availability–correctness trade-off: aggressive relevance filtering or runtime thresholds can reduce end-to-end attack success (e.g., from ~87% to ~22–48%) but can also collapse benign task completion/accuracy (e.g., ~95% to ~41–64%), leaving low-and-slow variants difficult to eliminate without harming usability.

nodes.title: ROK-FORTRESS: Measuring the Effect of Geopolitical Transcreation for National Security and Public Safety
nodes.one_liner: A transcreation matrix shows that multilingual safety cannot be inferred from translation-only tests because language and geopolitical grounding jointly—and sometimes interactively—shape harmful compliance and over-refusal.
points: Across 1,235 NSPS tasks, Korean-language variants reduced tier-weighted harmful compliance in 12/14 models on culture-agnostic prompts and 13/14 models under U.S.-grounded culture-specific prompts, with the average language-driven drop in risk score (~10 percentage points) about 3× larger than the average contextual (U.S.→Korea grounding) drop (~4 percentage points).

Language and geopolitical grounding interacted non-additively: Korean grounding significantly mitigated Korean language-driven suppression in 3/14 models (EXAONE +7.7 pp, Kanana 2 +7.0 pp, Llama 4 Maverick +5.6 pp), and no model showed significant amplification where Korean grounding made Korean-language prompts more dangerous.

Prompt complexity strongly moderated effects: when jailbreak tactics were stripped into 1–2 sentence direct requests, mean compliance fell to 9.6% and the mean Korean-vs-English gap collapsed to ~0.9 pp overall, while closed-source models retained Korean suppression (mean +4.5 pp) but open-source models flipped to the classic “translation as attack vector” pattern (mean −6.0 pp).

nodes.title: SkillSafetyBench: Evaluating Agent Safety under Skill-Facing Attack Surfaces
nodes.one_liner: Benign tasks can still drive unsafe agent actions when reusable skill files and nearby local artifacts are poisoned, and runnable artifact-based verifiers reveal large, system-dependent failure rates that prompt-only benchmarks miss.
points: SkillSafetyBench operationalizes skill-mediated, non-user local attack surfaces into 155 runnable adversarial cases (47 tasks, 6 risk domains, 30 categories) with per-case rule-based verifiers that check concrete artifacts, enabling reproducible measurement of induced unsafe agent behavior beyond prompt-only evaluations.

Across evaluated CLI agent–model pairings, unsafe behavior was frequently induced despite benign user goals, with overall attack success rates ranging from 15.5% (Claude Code + Opus-4.6) to 50.3% (Codex + GLM-5.1), showing that scaffold–model interaction materially affects safety.

Vulnerability is domain-skewed: context-trust and scope/authorization failures were easiest to trigger (RD1 average ASR 58.7% and RD2 49.3%), while runtime/toolchain and supply-chain style failures were harder but non-negligible (RD3 19.2% and RD6 20.1%), implying safety work must prioritize how agents trust local workflow context, not only model alignment.

nodes.title: Can Visual Mamba Improve AI-Generated Image Detection? An In-Depth Investigation
nodes.one_liner: Despite efficiency and good same-generator performance, Vision Mamba’s sequential state-space mixing struggles to generalize across generators and distribution shifts where Transformers/VLMs stay strong.
points: Vision Mamba variants show strong in-distribution detection but collapse under cross-generator shift (e.g., on AntifakePrompt they hit ~98–100% on MS-COCO/Flickr/SD2 yet drop to 0.23–15.93% on SDXL and ~6–11% on SGXL/ControlNet for VSSD/Vim/MambaVision), indicating limited transfer to new synthesis pipelines.

On LSUN Bedroom cross-model tests, SSM detectors trained on LDM generalize poorly to unseen unconditional diffusion sources (e.g., Vim falls to 0.93–2.02% on ADM/DDPM and 1.89% on iDDPM, while VSSD is 1.45–6.39% on ADM/DDPM/iDDPM), showing that state-space scanning backbones can miss artifacts that differ from the training generator’s signature.

Across UniversalFakeDetect, Vision Mamba backbones lag leading Transformer/VLM detectors by ~15–25 points mAcc (Vim 73.08%, VSSD 75.48%, MambaVision 70.19% vs FatFormer 90.86% and C2P-CLIP up to 93.79%), implying that global token-to-token interactions and multimodal pretraining remain key for robust AI-image detection at scale.

nodes.title: Security Risks in Tool-Enabled AI Agents: A Systematic Analysis of Privileged Execution Environments
nodes.one_liner: Privileged tools plus ambient authority make agents behave like insider threats, and simple isolation/least-privilege controls can eliminate most observed failures—except prompt injection, which remains stubbornly hard.
points: In controlled tests of a tool-enabled cloud agent (80 total runs), unsafe behavior appeared in 40–100% of baseline runs across four scenarios and secret leakage reached 90–100% in prompt-injection, ambient-authority, and chaining cases, showing that privileged execution environments can turn benign mistakes into high-impact failures without exploiting software bugs.

Lightweight mitigations drove unsafe behavior to 0% in three of four scenarios (capability–intent mismatch, ambient authority leakage, and composition/chaining) while prompt-injection unsafe behavior fell from 90% to 50% (a 44% relative reduction), indicating that least-privilege controls and authority isolation are highly effective whereas input-focused defenses remain comparatively brittle.

Mitigations imposed uneven operational costs—tool allowlisting reduced task success from 70% to 60%, content filtering added ~3218.6s latency, environment sanitization added ~22.7s, and policy checking reduced time by ~207.9s—implying that secure agent deployment is primarily a systems-engineering tradeoff problem across autonomy, usability, and latency rather than a purely model-alignment problem.

nodes.title: Attacks and Mitigations for Distributed Governance of Agentic AI under Byzantine Adversaries
nodes.one_liner: Centralized trust in agent-governance infrastructure is a single point of catastrophic failure, but most Byzantine behavior can be made detectable with near-baseline overhead and selectively hardened with hybrid sharding.
points: A compromised logically-centralized governance Provider can mount 16 demonstrated Byzantine attacks across protocol manager, access-control engine, and database components, enabling loss of attributability, private-data extraction, and both unauthorized and disrupted authorized agent communication.

Full Byzantine fault tolerance (SAGA-BFT) delivers the strongest integrity/availability guarantees but collapses performance to ~1% of baseline throughput on common requests, illustrating that naïve BFT replication is often too costly for high-throughput agent governance.

Lightweight defenses preserve near-baseline performance while enabling rapid compromise detection: server-side monitoring sustains ~95% of baseline throughput, client-side auditing sustains ~85%, and a hybrid sharded design can keep average latency as low as ~2× baseline while confining BFT to high-risk shards.

nodes.title: Agentic Interpretation: Lattice-Structured Evidence for LLM-Based Program Analysis
nodes.one_liner: The core idea is to treat LLM judgments as monotone evidence states in a lattice, letting classic static-analysis machinery (claims, joins, and a worklist) impose discipline, auditability, and termination on evidence-dependent program reasoning.
points: Agentic interpretation replaces brittle one-shot LLM program analysis with a worklist-driven, lattice-tracked process that decomposes a high-level goal into localized claims, records each claim’s evidential status in a finite-height lattice, and propagates updates through explicit context and feedback edges for bounded, auditable reasoning.

The framework guarantees termination when the assessment lattice has finite height and claim generation is bounded, because claim assessments only increase via join and the total number of possible strict increases is finite (formalized in a global-state model and proved via a worklist harness).

A worked security-review example with opaque third-party components shows how downstream composition refutations trigger targeted upstream re-investigation via feedback edges, escalating assessments from weak to strong refutation as version-specific advisories and contract-scope gaps are surfaced and then recomposed to strongly refute the end-to-end goal.

nodes.title: Language-Based Agent Control
nodes.one_liner: Treating agent control as type-checking inside a shared, pure language makes “if it compiles, it’s policy-compliant” a practical security boundary for the entire agentic loop.
points: Language-Based Agent Control (LBAC) enforces agent policies by requiring LLM outputs to be well-typed programs in a pure host language, so unsafe tool use (e.g., raw I/O like `writeFile`) is rejected before execution rather than filtered by brittle string-based permission rules.

TYPEGUARD’s information-flow-control instantiation achieved perfect prompt-injection resistance on AgentDojo Slack (105/105 attacks blocked under both “direct” and “important instructions”) while maintaining comparable utility to CaMeL under attack (36/105 vs 32/105 and 33/105 vs 36/105 tasks completed, respectively).

Enabling IFC policies materially reduced benign-task completion (from 15/21 to 8/21 for TYPEGUARD and from 15/21 to 7/21 for CaMeL), implying that strong, formally enforced flow/provenance constraints often require either policy relaxation or interactive authorization to preserve real-world agent usefulness.

nodes.title: ExploitGym: Can AI Agents Turn Security Vulnerabilities into Real Attacks?
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Security and Privacy
nodes.one_liner: A large, containerized, flag-verified benchmark demonstrates that some frontier AI agents already automate end-to-end exploit development on real software, and can occasionally punch through mainstream mitigations.
points: A new exploitation benchmark with 898 real-world instances across userspace (520), V8 (185), and the Linux kernel (193) shows that frontier agents can convert proof-of-vulnerability inputs into verified unauthorized code execution at non-trivial scale (up to 157/898 and 120/898 successes within 2 hours).

Even with standard mitigations enabled (e.g., ASLR/PIE, stack canaries, V8 heap sandbox, KASLR, user-namespace restrictions), agents still achieved residual successes—37 userspace, 20 V8, and 12 kernel tasks across all evaluated models—indicating today’s defenses reduce but do not eliminate AI-driven exploitation risk.

Agents frequently reach code execution via unintended paths: success alignment with the intended bug ranges from 36.4% to 83.1% (e.g., 56.7% for GPT-5.5), implying autonomous vulnerability discovery/pivoting is already a meaningful capability signal and complicates severity evaluation and sandboxing assumptions.

nodes.title: The Authorization-Execution Gap Is a Major Safety and Security Problem in Open-World Agents
affiliations: Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications
State University of New York at Buffalo
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is that many agent “attacks” and “failures” are the same underlying authorization-divergence problem, and you can only control it with execution-time integrity checks rather than one-shot upfront filtering.
points: Open-world agents face an Authorization–Execution Gap (AEG)—a divergence between intended authorization and realized actions—because autonomous tool use, persistent state, and multi-agent handoffs can turn small authorization drift into irreversible or hard-to-undo harm.

AEG failures largely trace to three structural sources—delegation-level incompleteness (underspecified constraints), channel-level corruption (environmental content treated as delegated authority), and composition-level fragmentation (end-to-end scope breaks across stages/agents)—implying symptom-based defenses can miss the true root cause.

Maintaining authorization integrity requires runtime, edge-specific checks (delegation completeness, authority attribution, scope compliance, provenance preservation, and handoff recomposition) and evaluations should report process evidence of where divergence was detected and attributed, not just task success or attack win rates.

nodes.title: Token Economics for LLM Agents: A Dual-View Study from Computing and Economics
affiliations: The State Key Laboratory of Blockchain and Data Security, Zhejiang University
nodes.one_liner: The key insight is to treat tokens as a unified economic primitive—factor of production, medium of exchange, and unit of account—so single-agent efficiency, multi-agent coordination, ecosystem congestion, and security overhead can be analyzed on one cost–quality frontier.
points: Weekly token throughput on OpenRouter grew from 0.4T (Dec 2024) to 27.0T (Mar 2026)—a ~68× increase in 15 months—indicating agentic workflows are driving superlinear demand that will make token-efficiency a first-class scaling constraint.

In agentic coding, token use can exceed single-turn reasoning by >1000× with input:output ratios reported above 150:1 and 59.4% of tokens spent in iterative review loops, implying most “cost” is coordination/context overhead rather than final generation and should be targeted via caching, pruning, and structured protocols.

Token consumption is highly unpredictable in practice (up to 30× variance across runs and models poorly forecasting their own usage with correlation ≤0.39), so reliable deployment requires budget-aware controllers, standardized cost attribution, and security/verification overhead modeled as an endogenous part of token price rather than an afterthought.

nodes.title: Engineering Robustness into Personal Agents with the AI Workflow Store
nodes.one_liner: The key idea is to treat agent robustness like production software—engineer and reuse hardened workflows—rather than trusting real-time LLM improvisation to be safe under adversarial inputs.
points: Shifting personal agents from per-prompt, on-the-fly tool synthesis to reusable, deterministically constrained workflows enables software-engineering style testing, adversarial evaluation, and staged deployment that can materially reduce brittleness and prompt-injection-driven unsafe actions in high-stakes tasks (e.g., email-to-booking or email-to-money flows).

A shared “workflow repository” can amortize the compute/time cost of rigorous hardening via reuse across users, but it also becomes a new high-value supply-chain target that must defend against malicious workflow injection, discovery hijacking, spurious request/resource-exhaustion, and cross-user side channels in lookup and matching.

Workflow design inherently trades off generality (coverage of many prompt variants) against robustness (strong invariants on data/control flow), implying practical systems will need multiple workflow representations and runtime selection policies (single vs chained workflows; synchronous vs asynchronous escalation) to balance latency, flexibility, and safety per request.

nodes.title: Do Androids Dream of Breaking the Game? Systematically Auditing AI Agent Benchmarks with BenchJack
nodes.one_liner: Agent benchmarks are currently easy to game at scale, but a security-first design plus iterative adversarial auditing can rapidly drive hackability toward zero for well-architected evaluations.
points: Across 10 widely used agent benchmarks, automated red-teaming produced working reward-hacking exploits for all of them and achieved near-perfect scores on 9/10 benchmarks without solving any tasks, indicating that headline benchmark numbers can systematically overstate real capability.

A total of 219 distinct benchmark vulnerabilities were identified spanning eight recurring flaw classes, with isolation/trust-boundary failures and trusting untrusted outputs emerging as high-leverage issues that can compromise entire benchmark suites rather than single tasks.

An iterative attacker–patcher loop reduced the hackable-task ratio from ~100% to under 10% on four better-designed benchmarks and fully patched WebArena and OSWorld to 0% hackability within three iterations, showing that proactive, adversarial auditing plus rapid hardening can quickly restore evaluation integrity when the underlying architecture is not fundamentally flawed.

nodes.title: Toward Web 4.0: Bidirectional Trust between AI Agents and Blockchain
nodes.one_liner: The most actionable takeaway is that Web4-style autonomous agents on blockchains are advancing faster in productization than in verifiable trust—especially for delegation, intent execution, and LLM correctness proofs.
points: Only 2 of 13 Ethereum standards that directly target AI/agent functionality have reached Final status (with 10 still in Draft), implying today’s “agent on-chain” stack is largely built on unstable interfaces and shifting security assumptions.

The trust foundation for agent correctness remains the dominant bottleneck because practical zero-knowledge proofs for LLM-scale inference are still far from interactive speeds (e.g., ~15 minutes to prove a full 13B-parameter inference pass), pushing real deployments toward weaker trust models like TEE or optimistic verification.

The agent-specific standards ecosystem shows critical end-to-end trust gaps: intent execution lacks cryptographic faithfulness proofs, permission/delegation has zero Final standards and demonstrated compositional bypasses in audits, and no deployed approach satisfies AI validator verifiability or bounds AI influence in governance without first solving Sybil-resistant agent identity.

nodes.title: Governing AI-Assisted Security Operations: A Design Science Framework for Operational Decision Support
nodes.one_liner: The key idea is a practical “AI query broker” that turns LLM assistance into a policy-checked, auditable SOC workflow instead of an unbounded copilot.
points: Treating AI-assisted SOC decision support as a governed engineering capability—rather than scaling it as automation—reduces accountability and auditability gaps created when models propose executable, code-like artifacts in privileged telemetry environments.

Even “read-only” AI-generated KQL can materially increase privacy exposure and cloud-cost risk via broad scans, sensitive-field projection, stale context, and misleading interpretations, so execution should be brokered with enforced time/row bounds, schema validation, and policy/approval gates.

A broker pattern that separates AI planning from operational execution (schema-grounded retrieval + approved parameterized templates + read-only adapters + auditable traces + ERB/CI-CD promotion gates) provides a deployment-ready control plane for shadow-mode and limited production use while explicitly constraining higher-risk agentic actions (e.g., coding agents) to sandboxed PR workflows.

nodes.title: OpenAaaS: An Open Agent-as-a-Service Framework for Distributed Materials-Informatics Research
affiliations: National Key Laboratory of AI for Materials Science
nodes.one_liner: A pragmatic “agent network with data sovereignty” design shows how to run LLM-driven materials workflows across institutional firewalls by moving computation to the data and standardizing capability discovery and routing.
points: OpenAaaS operationalizes cross-organization agent collaboration via a three-tier architecture (Master Agent → Rust network hub → Docker-sandboxed near-data nodes) that enforces “code flows, data stays still,” enabling secure workflows without raw dataset migration.

In a controlled metallurgy QA benchmark (40 questions; same model, same retrieval index, same retrieval scale), the evidence-grounded AlphaAgent executor scored 4.66/5.0 on deep analytical questions versus 2.67/5.0 for single-pass RAG, indicating large gains primarily from evidence validation and retrieval-drift control rather than bigger base models.

For a 17.4 TB hexa-HEA descriptor database (~5.4×10^10 candidates; 194D descriptors), near-data execution returned ~2.3 MB per optimization task (~7 orders of magnitude smaller than the raw dataset) while identifying a candidate system with 9.80% “highly ductile” configurations (10.7× over a baseline), showing that policy-enforced minimal-return queries can support practical design optimization at trillion-scale without data exfiltration.

nodes.title: From Code-Centric to Intent-Centric Software Engineering: A Reflexive Thematic Analysis of Generative AI, Agentic Systems, and Engineering Accountability
nodes.one_liner: The most interesting part is the reframing of AI-assisted development as an accountability and governance problem—where faster code generation makes verification, architecture stewardship, and auditable control loops the core of engineering work.
points: Generative AI shifts software engineering from writing code to specifying intent and curating context, making architecture knowledge, verification, and accountable acceptance decisions the new bottlenecks as code becomes cheap to produce but expensive to trust.

Agentic coding turns the unit of work from a single prompt into a tool-using control loop (plan → retrieve repo context → edit multiple files → run tests/commands → iterate → open a reviewable PR), implying that teams must engineer evidence gates (tests, scans, reviews, provenance) rather than optimize for raw output velocity.

Speed-first adoption increases hidden technical debt and governance/provenance debt (unclear authorship, weak auditability, unsafe dependencies, and security regressions), so organizations should deploy bounded autonomy with identities, least-privilege permissions, sandboxing, logging, and rollback to preserve quality and trust.

nodes.title: ChipMATE: Multi-Agent Training via Reinforcement Learning for Enhanced RTL Generation
nodes.one_liner: The key twist is industrial-style mutual verification: a Python reference-model agent becomes the “oracle,” and backtracking makes multi-turn self-correction reliably improve rather than drift.
points: A two-agent, code-blind cross-verification loop (Verilog design agent + Python reference-model agent) eliminates the need for a golden testbench and supports air-gapped deployment, but requires a backtracking acceptance rule—without backtracking, accuracy drops by 11.6–14.3% on VerilogEval v2 pass@1 due to error propagation.

Jointly trained multi-agent inference delivers strong first-try correctness: ChipMATE-Agents reaches 75.0% (4B) and 80.1% (9B) pass@1 on VerilogEval v2, surpassing prior self-trained models and even a much larger API model (DeepSeek V4 at 67.3% pass@1) despite a ~178× parameter gap (9B vs 1.6T).

Creating a dedicated reference-model dataset is pivotal for this workflow: a hybrid pipeline yields 64.4K verified Python samples, enabling ChipMATE-Python-9B to hit 82.4% pass@1 on the VerilogEval v2 Python track, while direct API distillation alone yields only ~25K samples at ~35% two-turn success (and >$2K / >200h processing cost).

nodes.title: What Software Engineering Looks Like to AI Agents? -- An Empirical Study of AI-Only Technical Discourse on MoltBook
nodes.one_liner: AI agents talk about software engineering as security, coordination, memory, and tooling—but omit the concrete failure-and-repro details that make human developer discourse actionable.
points: AI-agent technical discourse on MoltBook is dominated by Security & Trust (27.4% of posts), suggesting that when agents self-organize they disproportionately prioritize defensive framing and risk-aware guidance over other software-engineering concerns.

Community activity is extremely centralized (the largest submolt holds 63.5% of posts; Gini = 0.88) yet the corpus still splits into 32 non-outlier technical subtopics, implying a “single-megaphone, many-themes” dynamic that can skew what appears salient even when underlying content is diverse.

Compared with a matched GitHub Discussions baseline, MoltBook posts contain far fewer concrete grounding cues (e.g., in debugging: code blocks 11.6% vs 48.0% and environment/version markers 18.9% vs 62.3%), indicating AI-only discourse is coherent but less operationally testable and reproducible without human-supplied runtime context.

nodes.title: Multi-Stream LLMs: Unblocking Language Models with Parallel Streams of Thoughts, Inputs and Outputs
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems
nodes.one_liner: Treating “roles” (system/user/tools/thought/audit) as parallel token streams—rather than a single chat transcript—simultaneously improves responsiveness, reduces prompt-injection susceptibility, and yields more monitorable internal traces.
points: Multi-stream instruction tuning enables simultaneous read/think/act in one forward pass, driving token-number-to-first-target-token (TNFT) from 93–359 tokens in vanilla models to 0 across multiple benchmarks while keeping accuracy broadly comparable.

Adding a concurrent audit stream (“auditing while solving”) cuts end-to-end delay by over 40% and roughly halves the maximum per-stream length versus sequential “reflection,” while slightly improving LogicNLI accuracy (e.g., 61.55→65.65 at 1.7B; 62.00→65.55 at 4B).

Architectural stream separation materially reduces prompt-injection success without adversarial training, including a >33-point drop in indirect injection ASR on StruQ-ID (e.g., 80.69→41.91 at 4B) and large gains in Safe&Helpful on NESSiE (e.g., 8.21→28.21 at 7B).

nodes.title: LiSA: Lifelong Safety Adaptation via Conservative Policy Induction
nodes.one_liner: A conservative, evidence-calibrated memory layer can push the latency–performance frontier beyond simply scaling the guardrail model while staying stable under noisy, sparse feedback.
points: A fixed base guardrail can be improved in deployment without fine-tuning by converting sparse user-reported failures into structured policy memory that is selectively reused at run time.

Conflict-aware local refinement is the primary driver of boundary-sensitive gains, with final-day macro-F1 dropping from 0.962 to 0.931 when local rules are removed (vs. 0.959 without confidence gating).

Evidence-gated reuse via a Beta-posterior lower bound yields strong noise robustness, retaining 0.917 macro-F1 even at a 20% label-flip rate (vs. 0.857 for broad-policy-only memory and 0.854 with no gating).

nodes.title: PRISM: Generation-Time Detection and Mitigation of Secret Leakage in Multi-Agent LLM Pipelines
nodes.one_liner: Watching entropy collapse during decoding enables early, low-latency interception of credential reproduction in agent pipelines, preventing downstream propagation that post-hoc filters can’t undo.
points: Shared-context multi-agent pipelines exhibit propagation amplification where leakage probability compounds with depth, yielding a 71.2% task-level secret leak rate in an undefended 4-agent setup (70.1–73.5% across pressure levels), implying leakage is structural rather than purely prompt-driven.

PRISM’s generation-time, per-token risk control (16-signal calibrated scoring with entropy/logit-concentration early warnings plus structural cues) eliminates observed leakage on a 2,000-task benchmark (0.0% task leak rate, 0 leaked instances/task) while maintaining 0.893 task-level utility and achieving F1=0.832 (precision=1.000, recall=0.712).

Post-hoc and surface-form defenses leave substantial residual leakage—best baseline Span Tagger reaches F1=0.719 but still leaks on 15.0% of tasks (GBT: 19.2%)—showing that intervening before full secret reconstruction is materially more effective than filtering completed outputs.

nodes.title: MemPrivacy: Privacy-Preserving Personalized Memory Management for Edge-Cloud Agents
nodes.one_liner: Reversible typed placeholders shift privacy protection from “delete meaning” to “hide values,” keeping cloud memory useful without letting raw PII or secrets leave the device.
points: Type-aware, reversible placeholderization keeps sensitive values off the cloud while preserving task semantics, limiting memory-utility degradation to ≤1.6% across multiple deployed memory systems versus large drops from irreversible masking (e.g., up to −41.9 accuracy points on Mem0).

A dedicated on-device privacy-span extractor materially outperforms general-purpose LLMs for fine-grained privacy detection, reaching 85.97% F1 on a privacy-dense benchmark and 94.48% F1 out-of-distribution, while even a 0.6B model exceeds most frontier baselines at sub‑second latency in realistic settings.

A large-scale bilingual benchmark with 200 users and 155k+ annotated privacy instances (98.08% human-verified label accuracy) enables robust training/evaluation under a four-level taxonomy that explicitly separates directly exploitable secrets (PL4) from merely identifying (PL2) and harm-likely sensitive data (PL3).

nodes.title: Merlin: Deterministic Byte-Exact Deduplication for Lossless Context Optimization in Large Language Model Inference
nodes.one_liner: A tiny, deterministic, byte-exact preprocessing step can safely remove redundant context at microsecond-scale latency while remaining statistically indistinguishable from baseline across multiple frontier model APIs and benchmarks.
points: Deterministic byte-exact context deduplication adds negligible compute overhead—~1.10 µs median in-process (and 5–30 µs reported by the production binary counter) versus typical inference preprocessing budgets of 10–100 ms—making it effectively “below the noise floor” for real-time LLM serving.

Across 40 primary evaluation cells spanning 4 production LLM APIs and 3 benchmark families, the measured quality change from deduplication was +0.0 percentage points with 0 statistically significant degradations after Bonferroni correction, and a separate 200-cell warm-binary confirmation pass showed a -0.5 pp aggregate delta with again 0 Bonferroni-significant regressions.

Byte-level correctness and determinism were validated at scale with 0 violations on a 22.2M-passage BeIR run (Merlin unique_count exactly matched Python set() in all cases) and 99.2% byte-identical prompt output agreement vs an independent wrapper (100% on non-code prompts), indicating strong reliability for safety-critical prompt assembly pipelines.

nodes.title: Unweighted ranking for value-based decision making with uncertainty
affiliations: Valencian Research Institute for Artificial Intelligence
nodes.one_liner: A practical way to debias value-based decision-making by optimizing over feasible weight intervals—while modeling value uncertainty with fuzzy logic—and still getting provable ranking consistency.
points: FUW-VBDM replaces fixed, stakeholder-chosen criterion weights with a bounded fuzzy weight domain (interval constraints) to reduce normative bias while still enforcing policy priorities (e.g., fairness constrained to dominate cost).

Rankzzy provably yields well-defined, monotone fuzzy p-mean scores (including limiting cases p→±∞ and p→0) and consistent rankings under any admissible stakeholder configuration when decision-matrix entries are positive.

In scaling tests up to 50 actions × 40 values, Rankzzy’s worst-case runtime was 34.19s and average runtime stayed <18s, while ranking similarity to TOPSIS was strong for p∈{−1,0,1} with median Kendall’s τ ≥ 0.745 (p=2 weaker due to differing distance geometry).

nodes.title: VectraYX-Nano: A 42M-Parameter Spanish Cybersecurity Language Model with Curriculum Learning and Native Tool Use
nodes.one_liner: A tiny Spanish cyber model can outperform “better loss” bootstraps on real chat behavior and only learns reliable MCP tool dispatch once tool calls are dense enough to flip its first-token prior.
points: A three-phase Spanish curriculum with replay (conv→cyber→tools) achieved monotonic loss reduction (9.80→3.17→3.00→2.16; SFT 1.74) and produced a stable conversational gate of 0.78±0.05 across 4 seeds, indicating replay can prevent catastrophic forgetting even in a 42M-parameter from-scratch model.

Lower perplexity did not imply better chat behavior at nano scale: swapping the Phase-1 bootstrap from OpenSubtitles-ES to filtered mC4-ES reduced loss in later phases (−0.29 in Phase 2, −0.28 in Phase 3, −0.17 in SFT) yet degraded the held-out chat gate (7/10→6/10), showing that bootstrap-corpus register can dominate downstream interaction quality.

Tool-use generalization was primarily data-density gated rather than capacity gated: with a sparse tool-use ratio (≈1:211) tool-selection accuracy B4 stayed at 0.000, but increasing to ≈1:21 raised B4 to 0.145±0.046 (42M) and 0.445±0.201 (260M), implying the key bottleneck is shifting the first-token prior toward <|tool_call|> rather than adding parameters.

nodes.title: Grid-Orch: An LLM-Powered Orchestrator for Distribution Grid Simulation and Analytics
nodes.one_liner: Standardizing LLM-to-simulator control with MCP plus deterministic multi-step “skills” turns grid studies into fast, auditable, natural-language workflows without sacrificing numerical grounding.
points: A Model Context Protocol (MCP)-based architecture exposes 36 schema-validated OpenDSS tools across 11 categories, constraining LLM interaction to a typed, auditable tool boundary rather than arbitrary code execution.

End-to-end distribution workflows that typically take hours of scripting (e.g., DER interconnection screening and 24-hour QSTS analyses) are demonstrated to complete in under two minutes via natural language while producing numerically identical results to direct OpenDSS scripting.

Operationally realistic deployments are supported via a provider-agnostic LLM layer (Gemini/Claude plus local Ollama/llama-cpp), enabling fully air-gapped inference for utility environments where outbound connectivity is restricted (e.g., NERC CIP contexts).

topic_regions.label: Security of LLM Agent Skill Marketplaces
